- The maximum number of lines the switchboard physically supports = **Fan-In**.
- If 10 calls ring simultaneously, the receptionist can only answer 5, and the remaining 5 will be dropped or mishandled — this represents **violating the Fan-In limit**, leading to logic errors.

This analogy captures the essence of Fan-In: a **fixed input capacity** determined by the internal hardware architecture.

### 1.3 Physical Basis — Why Fan-In Has a Limit

The Fan-In limit exists due to two fundamental physical reasons:

1. **Internal Transistor Topology:** Each additional input to a logic gate requires additional transistors (e.g., additional MOSFETs in series/parallel in CMOS, or additional emitter-follower inputs in TTL). More transistors introduce more parasitic capacitance and resistance.

2. **Input Loading Effect:** Each input pin draws or sources a specific amount of current (IIL for LOW, IIH for HIGH). Adding more inputs in parallel effectively multiplies this loading, degrading noise immunity and speed.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" Behind Fan-In

The Fan-In of a gate is not arbitrary; it is dictated by the **internal circuit architecture**. Let us analyze this systematically.

#### 2.1.1 Fan-In in CMOS Logic

In a CMOS NAND gate, the **PUN (Pull-Up Network)** consists of PMOS transistors in *parallel*, while the **PDN (Pull-Down Network)** consists of NMOS transistors in *series*.

For an $n$-input CMOS NAND gate:
- **Number of PMOS transistors in PUN** = $n$ (parallel)
- **Number of NMOS transistors in PDN** = $n$ (series)

The critical issue arises in the PDN: when $n$ NMOS transistors are stacked in series, the effective on-resistance becomes:

$$R_{PDN,\text{eff}} = n \cdot R_{NMOS,\text{on}}$$

This linear increase in pull-down resistance with Fan-In directly **degrades the propagation delay** $t_{pHL}$.

#### 2.1.2 Fan-In in TTL Logic

In standard TTL, each input is connected to a multi-emitter transistor. As the number of emitters (inputs) increases:
- The base-emitter junction capacitance increases.
- The input loading current (IIL) increases proportionally.
- The switching threshold becomes less well-defined.

Hence, TTL gates are rarely manufactured with Fan-In > 8.

### 2.2 The Fan-In Delay Trade-Off

A crucial KTU concept is that **increasing Fan-In degrades speed**. The propagation delay of a logic gate is approximately proportional to the product of its effective output resistance and the load capacitance.

$$t_p \propto R_{\text{eff}} \cdot C_L$$

Since increasing Fan-In increases $R_{\text{eff}}$ (in CMOS series stacks) and also the input capacitance $C_{\text{in}}$ (which appears as part of $C_L$ for the driving gate), the cumulative delay becomes:

$$t_p \approx k \cdot n \cdot C_L$$

where $k$ is a technology constant and $n$ is the Fan-In.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Value** | **Units** | **KTU Relevance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fan-In of a gate | $FI$ | $FI = n$ (number of input pins) | dimensionless | Core definition |
| Effective PDN Resistance (CMOS NAND) | $R_{PDN}$ | $R_{PDN} = n \cdot R_{NMOS,on}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Delay calculation |
| Propagation Delay vs Fan-In | $t_p$ | $t_p \approx k \cdot n \cdot C_L$ | seconds (s) | Speed trade-off |
| Input Capacitance (per input) | $C_{\text{in}}$ | $C_{\text{in}} \approx 5 \text{ pF}$ (typical CMOS) | Farads (F) | Loading analysis |
| Total Input Capacitance seen by driver | $C_{\text{in,total}}$ | $C_{\text{in,total}} = n \cdot C_{\text{in}}$ | Farads (F) | Fan-Out calc basis |
| Input LOW Current (TTL) | $I_{IL}$ | $I_{IL} = -1.6 \text{ mA}$ (max, 74LS) | Amperes (A) | TTL loading |
| Input HIGH Current (TTL) | $I_{IH}$ | $I_{IH} = 40 \text{ \mu A}$ (max, 74LS) | Amperes (A) | TTL loading |
| Standard TTL Fan-In | $FI_{TTL}$ | $FI_{TTL} \in \{2, 3, 4, 8\}$ | count | Datasheet spec |
| Standard CMOS Fan-In | $FI_{CMOS}$ | $FI_{CMOS} \in \{2, 3, 4\}$ (practical) | count | Datasheet spec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** The vertical bar $\vert$ in mathematical expressions (such as absolute value $\vert x \vert$) has been replaced with the LaTeX `\vert` command in all table cells to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Fan-In specification directly impacts:

1. **FPGA/ASIC Design:** When designing complex combinational logic (e.g., an 8-input AND function), if the target technology library only provides 2-input AND gates, the designer must cascade gates or instantiate a *macro* with internal buffering — both of which affect timing closure.
2. **Logic Synthesis:** EDA tools (Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus) report **maximum fanin violations** as DRC (Design Rule Check) errors.
3. **Schematic Capture:** A common student error is connecting 9 inputs to an 8-input NAND gate (SN7430) — this is a direct Fan-In violation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Fan-In on Propagation Delay in a CMOS NAND Gate

**Problem Setup:**
Consider an $n$-input CMOS NAND gate driving a load capacitance $C_L$. We wish to derive the relationship between Fan-In ($n$) and the high-to-low propagation delay $t_{pHL}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Pull-Down Network (PDN) Structure**

In an $n$-input CMOS NAND gate, the PDN consists of $n$ NMOS transistors connected in **series** between the output node and ground. We model each NMOS as a resistor of value $R_{NMOS,on}$ when ON.

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Pull-Down Resistance**

When all $n$ inputs are HIGH, all $n$ NMOS transistors turn ON. The series combination yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{PDN,\text{eff}} &= R_{NMOS,on} + R_{NMOS,on} + \dots + R_{NMOS,on} \\
&= n \cdot R_{NMOS,on}
\end{aligned}$$

This is because resistances in series add linearly.

**Step 3 — Apply the RC Delay Model**

The output node capacitance $C_L$ discharges through the effective resistance $R_{PDN,\text{eff}}$. Using the standard first-order RC discharge model, the 50% propagation delay (time for output to fall from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pHL} &= 0.69 \cdot R_{PDN,\text{eff}} \cdot C_L \\
&= 0.69 \cdot n \cdot R_{NMOS,on} \cdot C_L
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result**

The factor $0.69$ comes from $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$, which is the natural logarithm of 2 — the time constant required for a capacitor to discharge to 50% of its initial voltage.

Therefore, we conclude:

$$\boxed{t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot n \cdot R_{NMOS,on} \cdot C_L}$$

This equation proves that **propagation delay increases linearly with Fan-In**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Effective Input Capacitance Seen by a Driving Gate

**Problem Setup:**
A single output of gate $G_1$ is connected to $k$ inputs of downstream gates. We want to find the total capacitive load presented to $G_1$.

**Step 1 — Identify Per-Input Capacitance**

Each input pin of a CMOS gate has a gate-oxide capacitance. For a typical 0.18 µm CMOS process, $C_{\text{in}} \approx 5 \text{ fF}$ per input. (For our KTU-level analysis, we use $C_{\text{in}}$ as a generic variable.)

**Step 2 — Compute Total Capacitive Load**

Since the inputs are connected in parallel from the perspective of the driving node:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{in,total}} &= C_{\text{in},1} + C_{\text{in},2} + \dots + C_{\text{in},k} \\
&= k \cdot C_{\text{in}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Combine with External Load**

The total load on the driving gate is:

$$C_{L,\text{total}} = C_{\text{wire}} + k \cdot C_{\text{in}} + C_{\text{next-stage}}$$

This relationship is critical when computing the **propagation delay** of the upstream gate in a cascaded logic network.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fan-In vs Fan-Out Comparison

| **Attribute** | **Fan-In** | **Fan-Out** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Number of inputs a gate can accept | Number of equivalent loads an output can drive |
| **Direction of signal flow** | Inputs → Gate | Gate → Outputs |
| **Typical value (TTL 74LS)** | 2 to 8 | 20 |
| **Typical value (CMOS 74HC)** | 2 to 4 | 50 (at low frequency) |
| **Primary physical limit** | Input transistor count, series resistance | Output drive current ($I_{OL}$, $I_{OH}$) |
| **Key degradation effect** | Increased $t_{pHL}$ in CMOS NAND due to series NMOS | Reduced noise margin, increased $t_p$ due to large $C_L$ |
| **Unit** | dimensionless count | dimensionless count (equivalent gates) |
| **Datasheet symbol** | "Number of Inputs" | "Fan-Out ($N$)" |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A 4-input CMOS NAND gate is fabricated in a 0.5 µm process where $R_{NMOS,on} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_L = 10 \text{ pF}$. Calculate $t_{pHL}$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pHL} &= 0.69 \cdot n \cdot R_{NMOS,on} \cdot C_L \\
&= 0.69 \cdot 4 \cdot (1 \times 10^3 \, \Omega) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F}) \\
&= 0.69 \cdot 4 \cdot 10^{-8} \, \text{s} \\
&= 2.76 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{s} \\
&= 27.6 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

For comparison, a 2-input NAND in the same technology would have $t_{pHL} = 13.8 \text{ ns}$, demonstrating the **2× slowdown** due to doubled Fan-In.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Structure of an $n$-Input CMOS NAND Gate

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Input 1] -->|VGS| nodeB[NMOS 1]
    nodeC[Input 2] -->|VGS| nodeD[NMOS 2]
    nodeE[Input 3] -->|VGS| nodeF[NMOS 3]
    nodeG[Input n] -->|VGS| nodeH[NMOS n]
    
    nodeI[VDD Supply] --> nodeJ[PMOS Pull Up Network Parallel]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Output Node Y]
    
    nodeB --> nodeK
    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeL[Ground GND]
    
    style nodeK fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeJ fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeL fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Fan-In Compliance Check Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Design New Logic Circuit] --> stepB{Identify Each Gate in Schematic}
    stepB --> stepC[Look Up Gate Datasheet]
    stepC --> stepD[Read Maximum Fan In Specification]
    stepD --> stepE{Count Input Signals Connected to Gate}
    stepE --> checkF{Connected Inputs <= Max Fan In?}
    checkF -->|YES| passG[Design Compliant Proceed to Simulation]
    checkF -->|NO| failH[DESIGN VIOLATION Add Buffer or Restructure Logic]
    failH --> stepB
    passG --> endI[End Pass to Place and Route]
    
    style startA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style passG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style failH fill:#FF6347,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style endI fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fan-In vs Fan-Out Visual Distinction

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SG1[Fan In Concept]
        inA[Signal A] --> gateX[Logic Gate]
        inB[Signal B] --> gateX
        inC[Signal C] --> gateX
        inD[Signal D] --> gateX
    end
    
    subgraph SG2[Fan Out Concept]
        gateX --> load1[Load Gate 1]
        gateX --> load2[Load Gate 2]
        gateX --> load3[Load Gate 3]
        gateX --> load4[Load Gate 4]
    end
    
    style gateX fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style SG1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style SG2 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Propagation Delay vs Fan-In Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ptA[Fan In n=1] --- ptA1[Delay t p baseline]
    ptB[Fan In n=2] --- ptB1[Delay 2x baseline]
    ptC[Fan In n=4] --- ptC1[Delay 4x baseline]
    ptD[Fan In n=8] --- ptD1[Delay 8x baseline]
    
    ptA --> ptB
    ptB --> ptC
    ptC --> ptD
    
    style ptA1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style ptB1 fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#333
    style ptC1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    style ptD1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Fan-In of a logic gate. Why is it considered a static design parameter?**

**Model Answer:**

Fan-In of a logic gate is defined as the **maximum number of input signals** that the gate can accept at its input terminals, as specified by the manufacturer. Mathematically, Fan-In $= n$, where $n$ is the count of input pins of the gate.

It is considered a **static design parameter** because:
1. It is **fixed by the internal transistor topology** of the gate (e.g., a 2-input NAND has exactly 2 NMOS in series in its PDN).
2. It **cannot be modified** by the circuit designer without changing the IC.
3. It appears as a constant specification on the manufacturer's datasheet.
4. It serves as a hard **Design Rule Check (DRC)** boundary during logic synthesis and schematic capture.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Fan-In and Fan-Out of a digital logic gate.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Sl. No.** | **Fan-In** | **Fan-Out** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of input signals a gate can accept | Number of load gates an output can drive |
| 2 | Limit is set by input transistor count | Limit is set by output drive current capability |
| 3 | Increases propagation delay (in CMOS series stacks) | Increases propagation delay (due to large load capacitance) |
| 4 | Typical value: 2 to 8 | Typical value: 20 (TTL), 50 (CMOS) |
| 5 | Read from datasheet as "Number of Inputs" | Read from datasheet as "Fan-Out (N)" |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of Fan-In in digital logic gates. Describe how the Fan-In of a CMOS NAND gate affects its internal transistor structure and propagation delay. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 4-input CMOS NAND gate fabricated in a process where the on-resistance of each NMOS is $R_{NMOS,on} = 800 \, \Omega$ and the load capacitance is $C_L = 15 \text{ pF}$, calculate the high-to-low propagation delay $t_{pHL}$. Compare this delay with that of a 2-input NAND gate in the same process. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Definition:**
Fan-In is the maximum number of inputs a logic gate can accept, as determined by its internal hardware architecture. For an $n$-input gate, Fan-In $= n$.

**Internal Structure of $n$-Input CMOS NAND:**
- **Pull-Up Network (PUN):** $n$ PMOS transistors in **parallel** between $V_{DD}$ and output.
- **Pull-Down Network (PDN):** $n$ NMOS transistors in **series** between output and GND.

**Effect on Propagation Delay:**
When all inputs are HIGH, all $n$ NMOS transistors in the PDN turn ON. Since they are in series, the effective pull-down resistance is:

$$R_{PDN,\text{eff}} = n \cdot R_{NMOS,on}$$

This increased resistance slows down the discharge of the load capacitance $C_L$ at the output node, increasing the high-to-low propagation delay:

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{PDN,\text{eff}} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot n \cdot R_{NMOS,on} \cdot C_L$$

Hence, **propagation delay increases linearly with Fan-In** in CMOS NAND gates.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $n = 4$ (for 4-input NAND)
- $R_{NMOS,on} = 800 \, \Omega$
- $C_L = 15 \text{ pF} = 15 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$

**Step 1 — Compute effective PDN resistance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{PDN,\text{eff}} &= n \cdot R_{NMOS,on} \\
&= 4 \cdot 800 \\
&= 3200 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $t_{pHL}$ for the 4-input NAND:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pHL} &= 0.69 \cdot R_{PDN,\text{eff}} \cdot C_L \\
&= 0.69 \cdot 3200 \cdot 15 \times 10^{-12} \\
&= 0.69 \cdot 4.8 \times 10^{-8} \\
&= 3.312 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{s} \\
&= 33.12 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $t_{pHL}$ for the 2-input NAND (same process):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{PDN,\text{eff,2}} &= 2 \cdot 800 = 1600 \, \Omega \\
t_{pHL,2} &= 0.69 \cdot 1600 \cdot 15 \times 10^{-12} \\
&= 1.656 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{s} \\
&= 16.56 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$\frac{t_{pHL}(n=4)}{t_{pHL}(n=2)} = \frac{33.12}{16.56} = 2.0$$

**Conclusion:** The 4-input NAND gate is exactly **2× slower** than the 2-input NAND gate in the same process, confirming the linear delay-vs-Fan-In relationship.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the concept of Fan-In and Fan-Out of a digital logic gate. Why is Fan-In considered a critical design constraint in VLSI circuits? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard TTL 74LS30 is an 8-input NAND gate. Its datasheet specifies maximum input LOW current $I_{IL,\max} = -0.4 \text{ mA}$ per input. Calculate the total current drawn by all 8 inputs when all are driven LOW. If a driving gate can source a maximum of $8 \text{ mA}$, determine how many such 74LS30 gates can be driven from a single output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Block Diagram (described):**
A central logic gate has multiple input lines (representing Fan-In) entering from the left and multiple output load gates (representing Fan-Out) connected to the right.

**Fan-In Explanation:**
Fan-In refers to the **number of input signals** a gate can accept. It is a property of the input side of the gate. For example, a 74LS30 has Fan-In = 8.

**Fan-Out Explanation:**
Fan-Out refers to the **number of equivalent loads** an output can drive. It is a property of the output side. For TTL, Fan-Out is limited by $I_{OL}$ and $I_{OH}$ drive capability.

**Why Fan-In is Critical in VLSI:**
1. **Area Overhead:** Each input requires additional transistors (e.g., series NMOS in CMOS NAND), increasing silicon area.
2. **Delay Penalty:** Higher Fan-In increases series resistance in the PDN, slowing down the gate.
3. **Power Dissipation:** More inputs mean more parasitic capacitance switching, increasing dynamic power.
4. **Noise Margin Degradation:** Multiple parallel-connected inputs increase capacitive loading and reduce noise immunity.
5. **Library Cell Limitations:** Standard cell libraries typically provide only 2-, 3-, and 4-input gates; higher Fan-In functions must be decomposed.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Gate: 74LS30 (8-input NAND)
- $I_{IL,\max} = -0.4 \text{ mA}$ per input (the negative sign indicates current flows *out of* the input when LOW)
- Driver source current: $I_{OL,\max} = 8 \text{ mA}$

**Step 1 — Total input LOW current for one 74LS30 gate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{IL,\text{total}} &= n \cdot \vert I_{IL,\max} \vert \\
&= 8 \cdot 0.4 \text{ mA} \\
&= 3.2 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Number of 74LS30 gates that can be driven:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{I_{OL,\max}}{I_{IL,\text{total}}} \\
&= \frac{8 \text{ mA}}{3.2 \text{ mA}} \\
&= 2.5
\end{aligned}$$

Since we can only drive a whole number of gates, we take the floor:

$$N_{\max} = 2 \text{ gates}$$

**Step 3 — Margin check:**
- Driving 2 gates: current required = $2 \times 3.2 = 6.4 \text{ mA}$ (within the 8 mA limit ✓)
- Driving 3 gates: current required = $3 \times 3.2 = 9.6 \text{ mA}$ (exceeds 8 mA limit ✗)

**Conclusion:** A single output can reliably drive a maximum of **2** 74LS30 gates when all their inputs are LOW simultaneously. This result also represents the **Fan-Out** of the driving gate with respect to the 74LS30 load.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Fan-In with Fan-Out:** This is the #1 mistake. Always specify the direction of signal flow. Fan-In = inputs to the gate; Fan-Out = loads driven by the gate's output.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor:** The RC delay formula uses $0.69 \cdot RC$, not just $RC$. Marks are deducted if students write $t_{pHL} = RC$.
> 3. **Sign error in TTL currents:** $I_{IL}$ is conventionally negative because current flows *out* of the input pin (toward the driving gate). Use absolute value when computing total loading.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Always convert pF to F, kΩ to Ω, and mA to A before substituting into equations. Mixing units is a frequent source of arithmetic errors.
> 5. **Ignoring the floor function:** In Fan-Out calculations, always round DOWN to the nearest integer. A fractional gate does not exist.
> 6. **Series vs Parallel confusion:** In CMOS NAND, NMOS are in SERIES (PDN), PMOS are in PARALLEL (PUN). Reversing this is a critical conceptual error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fan-In = number of inputs** a logic gate is designed to accept. It is a **static, manufacturer-specified** parameter.
- Fan-In is dictated by the **internal transistor topology** of the gate (e.g., series NMOS in CMOS NAND).
- **Increasing Fan-In increases propagation delay** in CMOS due to series-stacked NMOS resistance: $R_{PDN,\text{eff}} = n \cdot R_{NMOS,on}$.
- The canonical **delay formula** for a CMOS NAND gate is: $t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot n \cdot R_{NMOS,on} \cdot C_L$.
- Fan-In is a **Design Rule Check (DRC)** parameter in VLSI synthesis — violations are flagged as errors.
- Standard TTL gates have Fan-In of **2, 3, 4, or 8** (e.g., SN7400 = 2, SN7430 = 8).
- Standard CMOS gates typically have Fan-In of **2, 3, or 4** for practical performance reasons.
- Fan-In (input side) is the **conceptual opposite** of Fan-Out (output side) — never interchange them.
- Higher Fan-In increases **silicon area, dynamic power, and parasitic capacitance**.
- The **input LOW current** $I_{IL}$ in TTL is negative by convention; use absolute value for magnitude calculations.
- Always **round down** the Fan-Out calculation to the nearest integer (floor function).
- In VLSI standard cell libraries, when a logic function requires Fan-In > 4, the synthesis tool **automatically decomposes** it into a tree of 2-input gates — this is a common exam question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fan-Out
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT304 - LOGIC CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fan-Out

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fan-Out** is a critical static (DC) electrical characteristic of a digital logic gate that defines the **maximum number of standard logic inputs (loads)** that the output of a single logic gate can reliably drive while still maintaining guaranteed logic-level voltage specifications under worst-case DC operating conditions.

Mathematically, for a driving gate $G_1$ connected to $N$ identical load gates $G_2, G_3, \dots, G_{N+1}$ all operating from the same $V_{CC}$ and $GND$ rails, the **Fan-Out (FO)** is the largest integer $N$ for which the output voltage levels remain within the guaranteed noise margins of the logic family.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Fan-Out is the measure of the ability of a logic gate's output to drive a number of inputs of other similar logic gates without degrading its electrical performance. It is a **DC Steady-State Parameter** evaluated under the worst-case HIGH and LOW output loading conditions.

The two complementary metrics are:
- **DC Fan-Out (Static Fan-Out):** Determined by DC current sourcing/sinking limits of the output stage.
- **AC Fan-Out (Dynamic Fan-Out):** Determined by the maximum permissible signal propagation delay due to combined load and stray capacitances.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 treats Fan-Out as a *load-driving capability parameter* of a logic family, evaluated strictly at the **DC switching thresholds** specified in the manufacturer's datasheet (e.g., $V_{OH(min)}$, $V_{OL(max)}$, $V_{IH(min)}$, $V_{IL(max)}$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single water pump** connected to a **network of sprinklers** in a garden. The pump has a finite maximum flow rate and a maximum allowable pressure drop. If you connect too many sprinklers, the water pressure at each sprinkler falls below the minimum required to spray properly.

In this analogy:
- **Water Pump** $\equiv$ Output stage of the driving logic gate
- **Sprinklers** $\equiv$ Input stages of the load gates
- **Water Flow Rate** $\equiv$ Output Current ($I_{OH}$ or $I_{OL}$)
- **Minimum Spray Pressure** $\equiv$ Minimum required input current ($I_{IH}$ or $I_{IL}$)

The **Fan-Out** is simply the maximum number of sprinklers (loads) you can connect to the pump (driver) before the pressure (voltage) drops below the operational threshold. Connect too many, and the system **fails to operate correctly** — the logic levels collapse and the digital signal becomes ambiguous.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Shortcut:** Fan-Out = "How many gate inputs can ONE gate output feed without breaking the logic levels?"

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following bold constants are universally used in KTU board examinations for Fan-Out analysis:

- **$I_{OH}$** — Output HIGH current (source current) the driver can supply while still guaranteeing $V_{OH} \geq V_{OH(min)}$
- **$I_{OL}$** — Output LOW current (sink current) the driver can absorb while still guaranteeing $V_{OL} \leq V_{OL(max)}$
- **$I_{IH}$** — Input HIGH current flowing into each load input when the input is in the HIGH state
- **$I_{IL}$** — Input LOW current flowing out of each load input when the input is in the LOW state
- **$V_{CC}$** — Standard supply voltage (**$+5\text{ V}$** for TTL, **$+3.3\text{ V}$** to **$+5\text{ V}$** for CMOS)
- **Unit:** Fan-Out is a **dimensionless integer** (number of standard loads)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

A logic gate output has **two stable states**: HIGH and LOW. The maximum number of loads it can drive differs for each state, and the **overall DC Fan-Out is the more restrictive of the two**. The reasoning unfolds as:

1. **Step 1 — Analyze the HIGH State:**
   The output transistor in the HIGH state acts as a **current source**, pushing current OUT of the output pin and INTO each load input. Each load draws a small leakage-style current $I_{IH}$. As more loads are added, the total current demand $N \cdot I_{IH}$ increases, eventually forcing the output voltage to droop below $V_{OH(min)}$.

2. **Step 2 — Apply the HIGH-State Limit:**
   The maximum permissible number of loads in the HIGH state is the floor of the ratio:
   $$N_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \frac{I_{OH(max)}}{I_{IH(max)}} \right\rfloor$$
   where $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ denotes the greatest integer function (you cannot drive a fractional gate).

3. **Step 3 — Analyze the LOW State:**
   The output transistor in the LOW state acts as a **current sink**, pulling current FROM each load input INTO the output pin and then to ground. Each load sources a small current $I_{IL}$. With more loads, the total sink current $N \cdot \vert I_{IL} \vert$ increases, raising the output LOW voltage above $V_{OL(max)}$.

4. **Step 4 — Apply the LOW-State Limit:**
   $$N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \frac{I_{OL(max)}}{\vert I_{IL(max)} \vert} \right\rfloor$$

5. **Step 5 — Combine Both Limits:**
   The DC Fan-Out must satisfy BOTH conditions simultaneously, so the binding constraint is the smaller of the two:
   $$\boxed{FO_{DC} = \min(N_{HIGH},\; N_{LOW})}$$

## 2.2 Why Fan-Out Exists — The Engineering "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> Every transistor junction inside a logic gate has **finite parasitic resistance**. Pushing more current through it causes more $I \cdot R$ voltage drop, which shifts the actual output voltage away from the ideal rail. The manufacturer guarantees the logic levels **only up to a specific maximum current** — beyond that, the noise margin collapses and the system becomes unreliable.

The "**Why**" of Fan-Out is rooted in three physical realities:
- **Finite Drive Strength:** The output stage's transistors have a maximum rated $I_C$ or $I_D$.
- **Worst-Case Design Philosophy:** Digital systems must work for the worst manufactured chip, at the worst temperature, over the worst supply voltage — hence the "min/max" rating system.
- **Cascadability Requirement:** A logic family is only useful if its gates can be cascaded (the output of one feeds the input of another); Fan-Out quantifies this cascadability.

## 2.3 Logic Family Specifics

| Logic Family | Typical $FO_{DC}$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard TTL (74xx)** | **10** | Totem-pole output has moderate drive |
| **Low-Power Schottky TTL (74LSxx)** | **20** | Lower input currents due to Schottky inputs |
| **Advanced Schottky TTL (74ASxx)** | **40** | Higher drive, lower input loading |
| **Standard CMOS (4000 series)** | **50 or more** | Inputs draw only leakage current ($\sim$ pA) |
| **74HC / 74HCT CMOS** | **10 to 20** | Comparable to TTL, balanced for speed |
| **ECL (Emitter-Coupled Logic)** | **25 to 50** | Very high drive, but constant current draw |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the high-yield KTU cheat sheet for Fan-Out problems.**

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| HIGH-state fan-out limit | $N_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{I_{OH(max)}}{I_{IH(max)}} \right\rfloor$ | Dimensionless integer |
| LOW-state fan-out limit | $N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{I_{OL(max)}}{\vert I_{IL(max)} \vert} \right\rfloor$ | Dimensionless integer |
| **DC Fan-Out (overall)** | $FO_{DC} = \min(N_{HIGH},\; N_{LOW})$ | Take the **smaller** of the two |
| Effective input loading per gate | $n_{L}$ (load factor) | Each gate type has a relative unit load |
| Weighted fan-out | $FO_{W} = \dfrac{N_{standard}}{n_{L}}$ | When loads are mixed types |
| AC fan-out bound (capacitive) | $FO_{AC} \leq \dfrac{t_{PHL(max)} - t_{PHL(int)}}{0.69 \cdot R_{O} \cdot C_{g}}$ | Approximate, used in high-speed design |
| Total output sink current | $I_{OL(total)} = N \cdot \vert I_{IL} \vert$ | Must be $\leq I_{OL(max)}$ |
| Total output source current | $I_{OH(total)} = N \cdot I_{IH}$ | Must be $\leq I_{OH(max)}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fan-Out is the foundation of:
- **ASIC/FPGA Pin Planning:** Each output pin has a programmable drive strength; exceeding it causes signal-integrity issues.
- **Bus Driver Selection:** Buffers (e.g., 74LS244, 74HC244) are inserted between a low-drive controller and a heavily loaded bus to *boost* Fan-Out.
- **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS):** In synchronous VLSI, clock signals may drive **thousands of flip-flops**; hierarchical clock buffers restore Fan-Out at each level.
- **PCB Design:** Engineers add line drivers when a control signal must travel across long cables to many devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Fan-Out Equation

We derive the Fan-Out formula by applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the output node of a driving gate $G_D$ that fans out to $N$ identical load gates.

### 3.1.1 HIGH-State Analysis (Current Sourcing)

Consider the driving gate output node $V_{OUT}$ in the HIGH state, connected to $N$ load inputs. By definition, the output transistor sources a current $I_{OH}$ OUT of the pin, and this current splits and flows INTO the $N$ load inputs.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the output node:**

$$I_{OH(total\ sourced)} = I_{OH(max)} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} I_{IH,k}$$

**Step 2 — Since all loads are identical:**

$$I_{OH(max)} = N \cdot I_{IH(max)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$:**

$$N = \dfrac{I_{OH(max)}}{I_{IH(max)}}$$

**Step 4 — Enforce the integer constraint:** Since $N$ must be a whole number of physical gates, we apply the floor function:

$$N_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{I_{OH(max)}}{I_{IH(max)}} \right\rfloor$$

This is the maximum number of load inputs that can be driven in the HIGH state.

### 3.1.2 LOW-State Analysis (Current Sinking)

In the LOW state, the output transistor of $G_D$ acts as a sink. Each load input sources a small current $I_{IL}$ OUT of itself and INTO the driver's output pin. The driver must absorb all of this.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the output node:**

$$I_{OL(max)} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \vert I_{IL,k} \vert$$

**Step 2 — With identical loads:**

$$I_{OL(max)} = N \cdot \vert I_{IL(max)} \vert$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$:**

$$N = \dfrac{I_{OL(max)}}{\vert I_{IL(max)} \vert}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the integer constraint:**

$$N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{I_{OL(max)}}{\vert I_{IL(max)} \vert} \right\rfloor$$

### 3.1.3 Combining the Constraints

For the gate to work correctly in **BOTH** states, $N$ must satisfy both inequalities simultaneously:

$$N \leq N_{HIGH} \quad \text{AND} \quad N \leq N_{LOW}$$

Therefore the **binding (active) constraint** is the minimum:

$$FO_{DC} = \min(N_{HIGH},\; N_{LOW})$$

This completes the rigorous derivation.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Standard TTL NAND Gate

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Style):** A standard 74LS00 NAND gate output drives $N$ identical 74LS00 NAND gate inputs. The datasheet specifies:
> $I_{OH(max)} = 0.4\text{ mA}$, $I_{OL(max)} = 8\text{ mA}$, $I_{IH(max)} = 20\text{ μA}$, $\vert I_{IL(max)} \vert = 0.4\text{ mA}$.
> Determine the DC Fan-Out.

**Step 1 — Compute $N_{HIGH}$:**

$$N_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{400 \times 10^{-6}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 20 \right\rfloor = 20$$

**Step 2 — Compute $N_{LOW}$:**

$$N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 20 \right\rfloor = 20$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$FO_{DC} = \min(20,\; 20) = \mathbf{20}$$

**Conclusion:** Each 74LS00 output can reliably drive **up to 20** 74LS00 inputs. The result is *symmetric* in this case, which is a hallmark of well-designed TTL families.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — CMOS Driving Mixed Loads

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 74HC00 CMOS NAND gate drives a mixed load of 4 standard 74HC gates and 3 standard TTL (74LS) gates. Given:
> 74HC00: $I_{OH}=4\text{ mA}$, $I_{OL}=4\text{ mA}$, $I_{IH}=1\text{ μA}$, $\vert I_{IL} \vert = 1\text{ μA}$
> 74LS00: $I_{IH}=20\text{ μA}$, $\vert I_{IL} \vert = 0.4\text{ mA}$

**Step 1 — Total HIGH-state input current demand:**

$$I_{IH(total)} = (4 \times 1\text{ μA}) + (3 \times 20\text{ μA}) = 4\text{ μA} + 60\text{ μA} = 64\text{ μA}$$

**Step 2 — HIGH-state limit:**

$$N_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{64 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 62.5 \right\rfloor = 62$$

**Step 3 — Total LOW-state input current demand:**

$$\vert I_{IL(total)} \vert = (4 \times 1\text{ μA}) + (3 \times 0.4\text{ mA}) = 4\text{ μA} + 1.2\text{ mA} = 1.204\text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — LOW-state limit:**

$$N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{1.204 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3.32 \right\rfloor = 3$$

**Step 5 — Final Fan-Out:**

$$FO_{DC} = \min(62,\; 3) = \mathbf{3}$$

**Conclusion:** The bottleneck is the **LOW state**, because TTL inputs draw a relatively large $I_{IL}$. Even though the 74HC00 *itself* is a high-drive CMOS gate, the mix of TTL loads cripples its fan-out to just **3**. A **level-shifting buffer** (e.g., 74HCT125) would be inserted to restore usable fan-out.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Fan-Out is Exceeded

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix is a KTU 2024 high-yield recall table.

| Condition | Symptom | Engineering Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| $N > N_{LOW}$ | $V_{OL}$ rises above spec; LOW noise margin vanishes | Insert a **buffer** (e.g., 74LS244) |
| $N > N_{HIGH}$ | $V_{OH}$ droops below spec; HIGH noise margin vanishes | Insert a **buffer** with stronger source |
| $N > N_{AC}$ | Excessive $t_{pd}$; ringing on edges | Add a **line driver**; reduce capacitive load |
| Mixed-family load | $FO$ collapses to a small number | Use a **level translator / interface buffer** |
| Clock net with $N \gg 10$ | Skew, jitter, $V_{OH}$ droop | Build a **clock tree** with hierarchical buffers |

## 3.5 Buffer Insertion Formula

When a single buffer (with Fan-Out $FO_{buf}$) is inserted, the effective Fan-Out of the system becomes:

$$FO_{eff} = FO_{buf} \times FO_{driver}$$

For $k$ cascaded buffers:

$$FO_{eff,k} = \prod_{i=0}^{k} FO_{i}$$

This is why a properly designed clock-distribution network can drive **thousands** of flip-flops — at each level, a buffer *restores* the fan-out budget.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fan-Out Network

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a typical fan-out situation, where a single driver gate $G_D$ feeds $N$ load gates $L_1, L_2, \dots, L_N$, with current flow directions explicitly mapped.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["VCC SUPPLY +5V"] --> B["DRIVER GATE G_D"]
    C["GND REFERENCE 0V"] --> B
    B --> D1["LOAD GATE L_1"]
    B --> D2["LOAD GATE L_2"]
    B --> D3["LOAD GATE L_3"]
    B --> D4["LOAD GATE L_4"]
    B --> D5["LOAD GATE L_5"]
    A --> D1
    A --> D2
    A --> D3
    A --> D4
    A --> D5
    C --> D1
    C --> D2
    C --> D3
    C --> D4
    C --> D5
```

**Interpretation:**
- The top horizontal rail is the **$V_{CC}$ supply**.
- The bottom horizontal rail is the **GND reference**.
- The central block **DRIVER GATE** is the source of the digital signal.
- Each **LOAD GATE** block represents one of the $N$ driven inputs; here five are shown as an illustrative example.

## 4.2 Current Flow Architecture (HIGH vs LOW State)

The Mermaid diagram below distinguishes the two operational states by mapping the current direction between the driver and the loads.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HIGH_STATE["HIGH STATE - CURRENT SOURCING"]
        H1["DRIVER OUTPUT TRANSISTOR"] -- "Sources I_OH" --> H2["N LOADS"]
        H2 -- "Sinks I_IH each" --> H1
    end
    subgraph LOW_STATE["LOW STATE - CURRENT SINKING"]
        L1["DRIVER OUTPUT TRANSISTOR"] -- "Sinks I_OL" --> L2["N LOADS"]
        L2 -- "Sources I_IL each" --> L1
    end
```

**Interpretation:**
- **HIGH_STATE subgraph:** Arrows show current *leaving* the driver and *entering* the loads.
- **LOW_STATE subgraph:** Arrows show current *leaving* the loads and *entering* the driver (which routes it to GND).

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Buffer Insertion Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0["START - Compute N_HIGH and N_LOW"] --> S1["FO_DC equals min of N_HIGH and N_LOW"]
    S1 --> S2{"Is required number of loads M greater than FO_DC?"}
    S2 -- "NO" --> S3["Use driver directly. Design is valid."]
    S2 -- "YES" --> S4["Insert buffer with fan-out FO_BUF"]
    S4 --> S5{"Is M less than or equal to FO_BUF times FO_DC?"}
    S5 -- "NO" --> S4
    S5 -- "YES" --> S6["Use cascaded buffers. Design is valid."]
```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart is the canonical KTU-style decision process for solving any "design a fan-out tree" problem. Each time the fan-out budget is exceeded, a buffer is added to *multiply* the available drive.

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Direct Drive vs Buffered Drive

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph DIRECT["DIRECT DRIVE - FO VIOLATED"]
        C1["CONTROLLER GATE"] -- "Drives 50 loads" --> X["FAIL: Voltage levels collapse"]
    end
    subgraph BUFFERED["BUFFERED DRIVE - FO RESTORED"]
        C2["CONTROLLER GATE"] -- "Drives 10 loads" --> B1["BUFFER STAGE 1 - FO equals 10"]
        B1 -- "Drives 10 loads each" --> B2A["CLUSTER A - 10 loads"]
        B1 -- "Drives 10 loads each" --> B2B["CLUSTER B - 10 loads"]
        B1 -- "Drives 10 loads each" --> B2C["CLUSTER C - 10 loads"]
        B1 -- "Drives 10 loads each" --> B2D["CLUSTER D - 10 loads"]
        B1 -- "Drives 10 loads each" --> B2E["CLUSTER E - 10 loads"]
    end
```

**Interpretation:** A single controller attempting to drive **50** loads directly violates Fan-Out. By inserting **one buffer** (Fan-Out = 10), the same controller can drive **5 clusters of 10 loads each** = **50 total loads**, exactly matching the requirement with margin to spare.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Fan-Out of a digital logic gate. Why is it different for the HIGH and LOW output states?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Fan-Out is the maximum number of standard logic inputs that the output of a single logic gate can reliably drive while still maintaining guaranteed logic-level voltages.

> **HIGH vs LOW difference (2 marks):** The HIGH-state fan-out is limited by the maximum current the output transistor can **source** ($I_{OH}$) divided by the input current each load **draws** in the HIGH state ($I_{IH}$). The LOW-state fan-out is limited by the maximum current the output transistor can **sink** ($I_{OL}$) divided by the input current each load **sources** in the LOW state ($\vert I_{IL} \vert$). These two limits are computed independently and the **smaller** is the binding Fan-Out.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**A standard 74LS TTL gate has $I_{OL(max)} = 8\text{ mA}$ and $\vert I_{IL(max)} \vert = 0.4\text{ mA}$. Calculate the LOW-state fan-out. State the governing formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Formula (1 mark):**
> $$N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{I_{OL(max)}}{\vert I_{IL(max)} \vert} \right\rfloor$$

> **Substitution (1 mark):**
> $$N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 20 \rfloor = 20$$

> **Result (1 mark):** The LOW-state fan-out is **20** standard loads.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 / Model Paper]**

> **(a)** A logic gate has the following DC parameters: $I_{OH} = 0.4\text{ mA}$, $I_{OL} = 16\text{ mA}$, $I_{IH} = 20\text{ μA}$, $\vert I_{IL} \vert = 0.8\text{ mA}$. Compute the **DC Fan-Out**. Explain the engineering significance of taking the **minimum** of the HIGH and LOW limits. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A controller output with $I_{OH} = 0.4\text{ mA}$, $I_{OL} = 8\text{ mA}$ must drive **30 identical loads** with $I_{IH} = 20\text{ μA}$, $\vert I_{IL} \vert = 0.4\text{ mA}$. Design a **buffer tree** to satisfy this requirement. Each available buffer has $FO = 10$. Justify your design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State the governing formula:**
$$N_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \frac{I_{OH}}{I_{IH}} \right\rfloor \quad ; \quad N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \frac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} \right\rfloor \quad ; \quad FO_{DC} = \min(N_{HIGH},\, N_{LOW})$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute $N_{HIGH}$:**
$$N_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 20 \right\rfloor = 20$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute $N_{LOW}$:**
$$N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \frac{16 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 20 \right\rfloor = 20$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final Fan-Out:**
$$FO_{DC} = \min(20,\, 20) = 20$$

**Step 5 (3 marks) — Engineering significance of the minimum:**
- The gate must operate **correctly in BOTH the HIGH and LOW states simultaneously** for the design to be reliable. A logic level that is valid in only one state is useless because digital systems toggle continuously between HIGH and LOW.
- Taking the **minimum** guarantees that the design satisfies the **more restrictive** of the two constraints, ensuring that BOTH $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$ remain within specification regardless of which state the output happens to be in.
- This embodies the **worst-case design philosophy** that is central to digital engineering: we design not for the *typical* case but for the *worst* case, ensuring robustness across manufacturing tolerances, temperature, and supply-voltage variation.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute the gate's own Fan-Out:**
$$N_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor = 20 \quad ; \quad N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = 20$$
$$FO_{controller} = 20$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Recognize the deficit:**
The controller can only drive **20** loads directly, but the requirement is **30 loads**. Deficit = $30 - 20 = 10$ extra loads. Therefore a buffer is required.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Design the buffer tree:**
Since each buffer has $FO = 10$:
- The controller (FO = 20) drives **2 buffers** in the first stage (each buffer consumes 1 unit of fan-out, so $2 \times 1 = 2$ units used, well within the budget of 20).
- Each of those **2 buffers** (FO = 10 each) drives **10 loads** in the second stage.
- Total loads driven: $2 \times 10 = \mathbf{30}$ loads. ✓

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Schematic description (textual):**
```
            ┌──── Buffer 1 (FO=10) ──── 10 Loads
Controller ─┤
            └──── Buffer 2 (FO=10) ──── 10 Loads
```
**Verification of fan-out budget:**
- Controller → 2 buffers: uses 2 of its 20 budget ✓
- Each buffer → 10 loads: uses 10 of its 10 budget ✓
- Total reach: $2 \times 10 = 30$ loads ✓

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
The two-stage buffer tree satisfies the 30-load requirement with full compliance to all DC fan-out constraints.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **current sourcing (HIGH state)** and **current sinking (LOW state)** mechanisms in a TTL totem-pole output stage. Relate each to the corresponding Fan-Out equation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A CMOS gate with $I_{OH} = 4\text{ mA}$, $I_{OL} = 4\text{ mA}$ must drive **8 standard TTL loads** with $I_{IH} = 40\text{ μA}$, $\vert I_{IL} \vert = 1.6\text{ mA}$. Determine whether the direct drive is feasible. If not, propose a remedy and verify your design numerically. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — HIGH-state current sourcing:**
- In the HIGH state, the **upper transistor** of the TTL totem-pole output is ON, the lower is OFF.
- Current flows **from $V_{CC}$ → upper transistor → output pin → load input(s) → load internal circuit → GND**.
- The driver **sources** current $I_{OH}$ to the $N$ loads; each load draws $I_{IH}$.
- Equation: $I_{OH(max)} \geq N \cdot I_{IH(max)} \Rightarrow N_{HIGH} = \lfloor I_{OH}/I_{IH} \rfloor$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — LOW-state current sinking:**
- In the LOW state, the **lower transistor** of the totem-pole is ON, the upper is OFF.
- Current flows **from load $V_{CC}$ → load internal circuit → load input pin → output pin → lower transistor → GND**.
- The driver **sinks** current $I_{OL}$ from the $N$ loads; each load sources $\vert I_{IL} \vert$.
- Equation: $I_{OL(max)} \geq N \cdot \vert I_{IL(max)} \vert \Rightarrow N_{LOW} = \lfloor I_{OL}/\vert I_{IL} \vert \rfloor$.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Neat description of the diagram (textual, since it cannot be rendered):**
```
                VCC
                 │
            ┌────┴────┐
            │  Q3 ON  │  (Upper transistor, sourcing)
            │ (Darlington)│
            └────┬────┘
                 ├──────► Output Pin ────► to N loads (each draws I_IH)
                 │
                 ▼
              [LOW state: Q3 OFF, Q4 ON]
                 │
            ┌────┴────┐
            │  Q4 ON  │  (Lower transistor, sinking)
            │         │
            └────┬────┘
                 │
                GND
```

**Step 4 — Final relation:**
$$FO_{DC} = \min\left(\left\lfloor \frac{I_{OH}}{I_{IH}} \right\rfloor,\; \left\lfloor \frac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} \right\rfloor\right)$$

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute $N_{HIGH}$ for direct drive:**
$$N_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 100 \right\rfloor = 100$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute $N_{LOW}$ for direct drive:**
$$N_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 2.5 \rfloor = 2$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Overall Fan-Out of the CMOS gate:**
$$FO_{CMOS} = \min(100,\, 2) = 2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Feasibility check:**
- Required loads: **8**
- Direct-drive capacity: **2**
- Since $8 > 2$, **direct drive is NOT feasible.** The CMOS gate cannot sink enough current to hold 8 TTL inputs LOW.

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Proposed remedy:**
Insert a **level-translating buffer** between the CMOS controller and the TTL loads. A suitable choice is the **74HCT244** (or 74HCT125), which is a CMOS device with TTL-compatible inputs and high-drive CMOS outputs.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Numerical verification:**
Assume the 74HCT244 buffer has $I_{OH} = 6\text{ mA}$, $I_{OL} = 6\text{ mA}$, $I_{IH} = 1\text{ μA}$, $\vert I_{IL} \vert = 1\text{ μA}$.
- Buffer to 8 TTL loads: $N_{LOW,buf} = \lfloor 6\text{ mA} / 1.6\text{ mA} \rfloor = 3$. Still insufficient for 8!
- **Better:** use **two 74HCT244 buffers in parallel**, each driving **4 TTL loads**:
  - Per-buffer: $N_{LOW} = \lfloor 6 / 1.6 \rfloor = 3 \geq 4$? No, $3 < 4$.
  - **Use three buffers**, each driving $\lceil 8/3 \rceil = 3$ loads: $N_{LOW} = \lfloor 6/1.6 \rfloor = 3 \geq 3$ ✓
- CMOS controller to 3 buffers: $N_{LOW,ctrl} = \lfloor 4\text{ mA} / 1\text{ μA} \rfloor = 4000 \gg 3$ ✓.

**Conclusion:** The CMOS-to-TTL fan-out problem is solved by using a **level-translating CMOS buffer (74HCT244)** configured in a small fan-out tree.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Fan-Out questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the floor function** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ — students write $N = 5.6$ and lose 1 mark; the answer must be an **integer**.
> 2. **Confusing $I_{IL}$ sign convention** — by convention, $I_{IL}$ is the current *flowing OUT* of the input pin when LOW. Some datasheets show it as negative; always use the **magnitude** $\vert I_{IL} \vert$ in the LOW-state formula.
> 3. **Computing only ONE state** — a common error is to compute $N_{HIGH}$ alone and ignore $N_{LOW}$. The KTU answer key ALWAYS expects both, followed by $\min(\cdot)$.
> 4. **Ignoring mixed loads** — when loads are of different types, students often sum them naively without computing the *total* $I_{IH}$ or $I_{IL}$ demand. Always sum **currents**, not gate counts.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — $I_{OH}$, $I_{OL}$, $I_{IH}$, $I_{IL}$ are typically given in mixed units (mA vs μA). **Convert to a common unit (A) BEFORE dividing** to avoid a $1000\times$ error.
> 6. **Missing the buffer-tree design step** — when a 14-mark question asks you to "design a buffer network," failure to draw or describe the **tree structure** with **fan-out verification at each stage** loses at least 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fan-Out (KTU Module 4):**
> - **Fan-Out =** the maximum number of standard gate inputs that ONE gate output can drive reliably.
> - It is a **dimensionless integer**, determined by **DC current limits** in the worst case.
> - Two limits exist: $N_{HIGH} = \lfloor I_{OH} / I_{IH} \rfloor$ and $N_{LOW} = \lfloor I_{OL} / \vert I_{IL} \vert \rfloor$.
> - The **DC Fan-Out** is the **smaller** of the two: $FO_{DC} = \min(N_{HIGH}, N_{LOW})$.
> - Always convert currents to the **same unit (A)** before computing ratios.
> - Always apply the **floor function** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ — Fan-Out is an integer.
> - **TTL Fan-Out** is typically 10 to 20; **CMOS Fan-Out** is very high (50+) for same-family loads but collapses when driving **mixed TTL loads** due to high $I_{IL}$.
> - When Fan-Out is exceeded, **insert buffers** (e.g., 74LS244, 74HCT244) to multiply drive capacity.
> - **Buffer tree design rule:** $FO_{eff} = \prod_{i} FO_{i}$ for cascaded buffers.
> - **AC Fan-Out** is the same concept applied to **propagation delay** and **capacitive loading**; it is important in **high-speed** designs.
> - **Mixed-family interfaces** (CMOS↔TTL) need **level-shifting buffers** like the 74HCT or 74LVC families.
> - Real-world applications: ASIC pin planning, FPGA clock trees, bus drivers, memory-chip select distribution, address-line buffering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propagation Delay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT304 - LOGIC CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Propagation Delay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Propagation Delay ($t_P$)** is formally defined as the time interval between the **50% transition point of the input signal** and the **corresponding 50% transition point of the output signal** when a digital logic gate switches its state. It quantifies the finite time a signal takes to propagate from the input of a logic gate to its output.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Logic Circuit Design*, propagation delay is classified into two distinct directional transitions:

| Parameter | Transition Direction | Alternate Name |
|---|---|---|
| $t_{PHL}$ | **H**igh → **L**ow (output falling) | Fall Delay / $t_{pd,f}$ |
| $t_{PLH}$ | **L**ow → **H**igh (output rising) | Rise Delay / $t_{pd,r}$ |

The **Average Propagation Delay** is the standard KTU-board metric:

$$t_P = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$$

Units: **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)** for high-speed VLSI circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is a direct prerequisite for **Sequential Circuit Design** (Module 5), where propagation delay determines the **clock period**, **setup time**, and **hold time** margins in flip-flops and registers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of propagation delay like **water flowing through a pipe with a narrow constriction**:

- **Input** = Water entering one end of the pipe.
- **Logic Gate** = The narrow constriction in the middle.
- **Output** = Water exiting the other end.
- **Propagation Delay** = The time lag between water entering and leaving the pipe.

The pipe does not transmit water **instantaneously** — there is a measurable delay. In CMOS circuits, this delay is caused by the **resistance of transistors** charging/discharging the **load capacitance**. The narrower the constriction (higher resistance) or the wider the pipe end (larger capacitance), the **slower** the water (signal) emerges.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Context:** A modern 7nm FinFET NAND gate has a $t_P$ of around **10–20 ps**, while a 1970s TTL gate had a $t_P$ of about **10 ns** — a **1000× improvement** over five decades, driven by Moore's Law scaling of capacitance ($C_L$) and ON-resistance ($R_{on}$).

## 1.3 Why Propagation Delay Matters in KTU Examinations

- Determines the **maximum clock frequency**: $f_{max} \le 1 / t_{P,\,critical}$
- Governs **timing violations** (setup/hold) in sequential logic.
- Influences **Power-Delay Product (PDP)**, the key Figure-of-Merit (FoM) for digital gates.
- Establishes the **critical path** in combinational logic synthesis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Delay Generation

The propagation of a digital signal through a CMOS gate follows a strict **RC charging/discharging** mechanism:

### Step 1 — Input Transition Arrives
- The input voltage begins transitioning at $t = 0$.
- The gate's internal node capacitances ($C_{in}$, parasitic $C_{gd}$, $C_{gs}$) start charging/discharging.

### Step 2 — Transistor Network Responds
- The PMOS or NMOS network turns ON in response to the input.
- A low-resistance conducting path ($R_{on,p}$ or $R_{on,n}$) forms between the output node and either $V_{DD}$ or GND.

### Step 3 — Output Capacitance Charges/Discharges
- The output load capacitance $C_L$ (sum of wire + fan-out + intrinsic drain capacitance) is charged or discharged through $R_{on}$.
- Output voltage moves **exponentially** toward $V_{DD}$ or $0V$.

### Step 4 — 50% Threshold Crossing
- The output crosses the **$V_{DD}/2$ threshold**.
- The time elapsed from input's 50% point to this output's 50% point is **$t_P$**.

## 2.2 RC Delay Derivation (First-Order Approximation)

The output node of a CMOS gate behaves as a **first-order RC low-pass network**:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{final} + (V_{initial} - V_{final}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_L$.

Setting $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}/2$, $V_{final} = 0$ (discharge), $V_{initial} = V_{DD}$:

$$0.5 V_{DD} = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t_{PHL}/(R_{on,n} C_L)}$$

Solving for $t_{PHL}$:

$$t_{PHL} = \ln(2) \cdot R_{on,n} \cdot C_L \approx 0.69 \, R_{on,n} \, C_L$$

By symmetry, for the rising transition:

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \, R_{on,p} \, C_L$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all** formulas tested in KTU 2024 ESE/ISE for this topic. Memorize the boxed equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{PHL} \approx 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L$ | Fall (HL) delay through NMOS | seconds |
| 2 | $t_{PLH} \approx 0.69 \, R_p \, C_L$ | Rise (LH) delay through PMOS | seconds |
| 3 | $t_P = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay | seconds |
| 4 | $t_P \approx 0.69 \, C_L \left(\frac{R_n + R_p}{2}\right)$ | Symmetric CMOS delay | seconds |
| 5 | $R_{on} = \frac{1}{\mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_T)}$ | MOSFET ON-resistance | ohms |
| 6 | $C_L = C_{int} + C_{wire} + C_{fanout}$ | Total load capacitance | farads |
| 7 | $f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{P,\,critical}}$ | Maximum clock frequency (pipelined) | Hz |
| 8 | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_P$ | Power-Delay Product (FoM) | joules |
| 9 | $E_{switch} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Switching energy per transition | joules |
| 10 | $t_{P,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{P,i}$ | Cumulative path delay | seconds |
| 11 | $t_{cd}$ (contamination delay) | Minimum delay (fastest transition) | seconds |
| 12 | $t_P \propto L_{gate}^2$ | Logical effort propagation rule | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables above, every absolute value / norm notation was written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX command to **avoid breaking the table pipe syntax** — as required by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol.

## 2.4 Logical Effort Perspective (KTU 2024 Emphasis)

The **Logical Effort (LE)** framework, introduced by Sutherland & Sproull, is heavily tested in KTU 2024. The delay of a single stage is:

$$d = g \cdot h + p$$

where:
- $g$ = logical effort (1 for inverter, $\frac{4}{3}$ for NAND, $\frac{5}{3}$ for NOR)
- $h = C_{out}/C_{in}$ = electrical effort (fan-out)
- $p$ = parasitic delay (1 for inverter, 2 for NAND/NOR)

The **path delay** through $N$ stages:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(g_i h_i + p_i)$$

Minimum delay occurs when each stage has the same **stage effort** $\hat{f} = \sqrt[N]{F}$ where $F = GBH$ is the path's total effort.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPU Design:** Determines instruction cycle time; a 3 GHz CPU requires $t_P \le 333$ ps per stage.
- **FPGA Timing Closure:** Xilinx/Altera tools report $t_{PHL}$, $t_{PLH}$ and slack based on these formulas.
- **VLSI Synthesis:** Critical-path optimization uses LE theory to size transistors for minimum delay.
- **High-Speed Serial Links:** Bit-error rate is directly tied to $t_P$ matching across parallel data lanes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Average Propagation Delay from RC Network

**Problem Setup:** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance $C_L = 50$ fF. The NMOS ON-resistance is $R_n = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and the PMOS ON-resistance is $R_p = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$. Find $t_{PHL}$, $t_{PLH}$, and average $t_P$.

### Step A — Identify the governing equation for fall time

When the input goes HIGH, NMOS turns ON, PMOS turns OFF, and the output capacitor $C_L$ discharges through $R_n$ from $V_{DD}$ toward ground.

The discharge follows:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/(R_n C_L)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The standard form $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ applies with $\tau = R_n C_L$ and initial voltage $V_0 = V_{DD}$.

### Step B — Solve for the 50% threshold crossing point

We require $V_{out}(t_{PHL}) = 0.5 V_{DD}$:

$$0.5 V_{DD} = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t_{PHL}/(R_n C_L)}$$

Dividing both sides by $V_{DD}$:

$$0.5 = e^{-t_{PHL}/(R_n C_L)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** We isolate the exponential by taking the natural logarithm of both sides.

### Step C — Apply natural logarithm

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{R_n C_L}$$

Since $\ln(0.5) = -\ln(2) \approx -0.693$:

$$-0.693 = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{R_n C_L}$$

### Step D — Solve for $t_{PHL}$

$$t_{PHL} = 0.693 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L$$

**Conversion Logic:** The 0.69 factor appears because $\ln(2) = 0.693$ — this is the canonical KTU result.

### Step E — Substitute numerical values

$$t_{PHL} = 0.693 \times 10{,}000 \times 50 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$t_{PHL} = 0.693 \times 5 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$t_{PHL} = 3.465 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{s} = 346.5 \,\text{ps}$$

### Step F — Repeat for $t_{PLH}$ (rising transition)

When input goes LOW, PMOS turns ON, NMOS turns OFF, and $C_L$ charges through $R_p$ from 0V toward $V_{DD}$:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/(R_p C_L)}\right)$$

Setting $V_{out}(t_{PLH}) = 0.5 V_{DD}$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_{PLH}/(R_p C_L)}$$

$$e^{-t_{PLH}/(R_p C_L)} = 0.5$$

$$t_{PLH} = 0.693 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L$$

**Conversion Logic:** Both directions yield the same $0.693$ factor, but the resistance differs.

### Step G — Substitute numerical values for $t_{PLH}$

$$t_{PLH} = 0.693 \times 20{,}000 \times 50 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$t_{PLH} = 0.693 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$t_{PLH} = 6.93 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{s} = 693 \,\text{ps}$$

### Step H — Compute average propagation delay

$$t_P = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2} = \frac{346.5 + 693}{2} \,\text{ps}$$

$$t_P = \frac{1039.5}{2} = 519.75 \,\text{ps} \approx 520 \,\text{ps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Because $R_p = 2 R_n$ (PMOS is weaker than NMOS due to lower hole mobility), the rising transition is **twice as slow** as the falling transition — a hallmark of asymmetric CMOS delay.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Effect of Fan-Out on Delay

**Problem Setup:** An inverter with intrinsic delay $t_{P0} = 100$ ps drives a load capacitance $C_L$. If the fan-out (number of identical inverter loads) increases from 1 to 4, by what factor does the propagation delay change?

### Step A — Express delay in terms of total capacitance

$$t_P \propto C_{L,total} = C_{int} + N \cdot C_{gate}$$

where $C_{int}$ is the inverter's intrinsic output capacitance, $C_{gate}$ is the input capacitance of a single load, and $N$ is the fan-out count.

### Step B — Use the slope-intercept delay model

The standard CMOS delay equation in the presence of fan-out is:

$$t_P = t_{P0}\left(1 + \frac{C_{ext}}{C_{int}}\right)$$

where $C_{ext} = N \cdot C_{gate}$ is the external load, and $C_{int}$ is intrinsic.

For simplicity, assume $C_{int} = C_{gate} = C$ (typical for minimum-sized inverters):

$$t_P = t_{P0}(1 + N)$$

### Step C — Evaluate for N = 1

$$t_P(N=1) = 100(1 + 1) = 200 \,\text{ps}$$

### Step D — Evaluate for N = 4

$$t_P(N=4) = 100(1 + 4) = 500 \,\text{ps}$$

### Step E — Compute the ratio

$$\frac{t_P(N=4)}{t_P(N=1)} = \frac{500}{200} = 2.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A 4× increase in fan-out causes a **2.5×** increase in propagation delay. The delay grows **linearly** with fan-out in the first-order model.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Logical Effort Path Delay (KTU 2024 Favourite)

**Problem Setup:** A 3-stage NAND chain has the following parameters:
- Stage 1: $g_1 = 4/3$, $h_1 = 4$, $p_1 = 2$
- Stage 2: $g_2 = 4/3$, $h_2 = 2$, $p_2 = 2$
- Stage 3: $g_3 = 4/3$, $h_3 = 1$, $p_3 = 2$

Find the **minimum path delay** and the **optimal stage effort**.

### Step A — Compute the path effort

$$F = \prod_{i=1}^{3} g_i h_i$$

**Conversion Logic:** The total path effort $F$ is the product of all stage efforts $f_i = g_i h_i$.

$$F = \left(\frac{4}{3} \cdot 4\right) \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3} \cdot 2\right) \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3} \cdot 1\right)$$

$$F = \frac{16}{3} \cdot \frac{8}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{512}{27} \approx 18.96$$

### Step B — Compute the parasitic delay

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{3} p_i = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6$$

### Step C — Compute the optimal stage effort

The minimum-delay criterion requires equal stage efforts:

$$\hat{f} = \sqrt[N]{F} = \sqrt[3]{18.96} \approx 2.67$$

### Step D — Compute the minimum path delay

$$D_{min} = N \cdot \hat{f} + P = 3 \times 2.67 + 6 = 8.01 + 6 = 14.01$$

**Conversion Logic:** The minimum delay is obtained when each stage is sized such that the stage effort equals $\hat{f}$.

### Step E — Determine the individual stage input capacitances

For optimal sizing, $C_{in,i} = \frac{g_i C_{out,i}}{\hat{f}}$. Assuming the final load $C_{L} = 1$ unit:

$$C_{in,3} = \frac{g_3 C_L}{\hat{f}} = \frac{(4/3)(1)}{2.67} \approx 0.50$$

$$C_{in,2} = \frac{g_2 C_{in,3}}{\hat{f}} = \frac{(4/3)(0.50)}{2.67} \approx 0.25$$

$$C_{in,1} = \frac{g_1 C_{in,2}}{\hat{f}} = \frac{(4/3)(0.25)}{2.67} \approx 0.125$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** The transistor widths must be **scaled geometrically** by a factor of 2 between consecutive stages to achieve the minimum delay. This is the **buffer chain tapering** principle used in clock distribution networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Propagation Delay Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A [Input Source]
        PULSE[Pulse Generator]
    end
    subgraph B [Device Under Test]
        GATE[Logic Gate]
    end
    subgraph C [Measurement]
        OSC[Oscilloscope]
    end
    subgraph D [Analysis]
        T50[50 Percent Threshold]
        TP[Propagation Delay Output]
    end
    PULSE --> GATE
    GATE --> OSC
    OSC --> T50
    T50 --> TP
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Timing Diagram — $t_{PHL}$ and $t_{PLH}$ Definitions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph E [Input Signal]
        I1[Vin starts falling at t0]
        I2[Vin crosses 50 percent at tA]
    end
    subgraph F [Output Signal]
        O1[Vout starts falling at tA plus tPHL]
        O2[Vout crosses 50 percent at tB]
    end
    subgraph G [Timing Relationships]
        R1[tPLH measured on rising edge]
        R2[tPHL measured on falling edge]
        R3[tP average equals tPLH plus tPHL over 2]
    end
    I2 --> O1
    I2 --> O2
    R1 --> R3
    R2 --> R3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture Flow — RC Delay Mechanism in CMOS Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H [Input Stage]
        VIN[Vin Transition]
    end
    subgraph I [Transistor Network]
        PMOS[P Channel Device RONP]
        NMOS[N Channel Device RONN]
    end
    subgraph J [Output Stage]
        CL[Load Capacitance CL]
        VOUT[Vout Node]
    end
    subgraph K [Delay Output]
        DELAY[Propagation Delay tP]
    end
    VIN --> PMOS
    VIN --> NMOS
    PMOS --> VOUT
    NMOS --> VOUT
    CL --> VOUT
    VOUT --> DELAY
```

