<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define propagation delay. Distinguish between $t_{PHL}$ and $t_{PLH}$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Propagation delay** is the time interval between the 50% transition point of the input signal and the corresponding 50% transition point of the output signal of a logic gate.

- **$t_{PHL}$ (High-to-Low delay):** Time measured when the output transitions from logic HIGH to logic LOW. It depends primarily on the **NMOS ON-resistance** $R_n$ and the load capacitance $C_L$.

  $$t_{PHL} \approx 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L$$

- **$t_{PLH}$ (Low-to-High delay):** Time measured when the output transitions from logic LOW to logic HIGH. It depends primarily on the **PMOS ON-resistance** $R_p$ and the load capacitance $C_L$.

  $$t_{PLH} \approx 0.69 \, R_p \, C_L$$

In **symmetric CMOS** inverters, $R_n = R_p$ and $t_{PHL} = t_{PLH}$. In standard CMOS, due to lower hole mobility, $R_p > R_n$, making $t_{PLH} > t_{PHL}$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the Power-Delay Product (PDP)? Why is it used as a Figure-of-Merit (FoM) for digital gates?**

**Model Answer:**

**Power-Delay Product (PDP)** is defined as the product of the average power consumption and the propagation delay of a logic gate.

$$PDP = P_{avg} \times t_P$$

Its unit is **joules (J)**, and it represents the **energy consumed per switching event**.

**Why PDP is a Figure-of-Merit:**
1. It captures the **trade-off** between speed and power — a gate cannot be optimized for both simultaneously.
2. A gate with low power but high delay (or vice-versa) can still have a poor PDP.
3. It is **technology-independent** and allows fair comparison across different logic families (CMOS, BiCMOS, TTL).
4. It is directly related to the battery life in portable devices and the thermal design power (TDP) in processors.

For CMOS, the switching energy per transition is:

$$E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **one full question** from a module. The following provides both **Question A** and **Question B** as the internal-choice alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
A CMOS inverter has $R_n = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ (NMOS ON-resistance) and $R_p = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (PMOS ON-resistance). The load capacitance is $C_L = 100$ fF. Calculate:
1. $t_{PHL}$
2. $t_{PLH}$
3. Average propagation delay $t_P$
4. Maximum operating frequency $f_{max}$ for a chain of 8 such inverters.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $t_{PHL}$**

Using the standard formula:

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L$$

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \times 5 \times 10^{-10} = 3.45 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{s} = 345 \,\text{ps}$$

**Step 2: Compute $t_{PLH}$**

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L$$

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \times 10^{-9} = 6.9 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{s} = 690 \,\text{ps}$$

**Step 3: Compute average $t_P$**

$$t_P = \frac{345 + 690}{2} = \frac{1035}{2} = 517.5 \,\text{ps}$$

**Step 4: Compute total chain delay for 8 inverters**

$$t_{P,chain} = 8 \times 517.5 = 4140 \,\text{ps} = 4.14 \,\text{ns}$$

**Step 5: Compute maximum frequency**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{P,chain}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 4.14 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{8.28 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 120.8 \,\text{MHz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $t_{PHL} = 345$ ps, $t_{PLH} = 690$ ps, $t_P = 517.5$ ps, $f_{max} = 120.8$ MHz.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the **Logical Effort** method for delay optimization. Compute the minimum delay of a 3-stage path with $G = 8$ (path logical effort) and $B = 1$ (branching effort) and $H = 9$ (electrical effort). The parasitic delay is $P = 6$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State the Logical Effort delay equation**

The delay of a single stage is:

$$d_i = g_i h_i + p_i$$

The total path delay is:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (g_i h_i + p_i) = \sum g_i h_i + P$$

**Step 2: Define path effort $F$**

$$F = G \cdot B \cdot H = 8 \times 1 \times 9 = 72$$

**Step 3: Compute optimal stage effort $\hat{f}$**

$$\hat{f} = \sqrt[N]{F} = \sqrt[3]{72} = 4.16$$

**Step 4: Compute minimum path delay**

$$D_{min} = N \cdot \hat{f} + P = 3 \times 4.16 + 6 = 12.48 + 6 = 18.48$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result**

The minimum delay is **18.48 $\tau$** (in units of the inverter delay $\tau \approx 3RC$), achieved when each stage is sized so that the input capacitance scales by $\hat{f} = 4.16$ per stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $D_{min} = 18.48\,\tau$. The optimal sizing factor is $4.16$ per stage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **first-order RC delay model** of a CMOS inverter. Derive the expression for $t_{PHL}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Describe the RC model**

When the input of a CMOS inverter transitions, the output node is modelled as a **Thevenin equivalent circuit**: a resistance $R_{on}$ (either $R_n$ or $R_p$) in series with the load capacitance $C_L$ driven by a step voltage source ($V_{DD}$ or GND).

**Step 2: Draw the equivalent circuit for fall transition**

During the HIGH-to-LOW transition, NMOS is ON and PMOS is OFF. The equivalent circuit is:

$$V_{DD} \rightarrow R_n \rightarrow C_L \rightarrow \text{GND}$$

with $V_{out}$ measured across $C_L$.

**Step 3: Write the differential equation**

Using KVL: $V_{DD} = i(t) R_n + V_{out}(t)$, and $i(t) = C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$:

$$V_{DD} = R_n C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out}(t)$$

**Step 4: Solve the ODE with initial condition $V_{out}(0) = V_{DD}$**

Standard first-order solution:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/(R_n C_L)}$$

**Step 5: Set $V_{out}(t_{PHL}) = V_{DD}/2$**

$$\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t_{PHL}/(R_n C_L)}$$

**Step 6: Solve for $t_{PHL}$**

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_{PHL}/(R_n C_L)}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{R_n C_L}$$

$$t_{PHL} = \ln(2) \cdot R_n \cdot C_L = 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L$. The derivation holds for $t \ge 0$ and assumes the input is a perfect step.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A digital system has a critical path consisting of 4 gates with the following delays: 250 ps, 400 ps, 300 ps, and 350 ps. Determine:
1. Total critical path delay.
2. Maximum clock frequency.
3. What happens to $f_{max}$ if the gate with 400 ps delay is replaced by one with 250 ps delay?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute total critical path delay**

$$T_{clk} \ge t_{P,total} = 250 + 400 + 300 + 350 = 1300 \,\text{ps} = 1.3 \,\text{ns}$$

**Step 2: Compute maximum clock frequency**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk}} = \frac{1}{1.3 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 769.2 \,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 3: Recompute after replacing 400 ps with 250 ps**

$$T_{clk}' = 250 + 250 + 300 + 350 = 1150 \,\text{ps} = 1.15 \,\text{ns}$$

$$f_{max}' = \frac{1}{1.15 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 869.6 \,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 4: Compute percentage improvement**

$$\Delta f = \frac{869.6 - 769.2}{769.2} \times 100\% = 13.05\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $T_{clk} = 1.3$ ns, $f_{max} = 769.2$ MHz. After replacement, $f_{max} = 869.6$ MHz (a **13% improvement**).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor:** Many students write $t_P = R C_L$ instead of $t_P = 0.69 R C_L$. The 0.69 comes from $\ln(2)$ and is **mandatory**. Loss: **2 marks** per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Confusing $t_{PHL}$ with $t_{PLH}$:** $t_{PHL}$ uses $R_n$ (NMOS) and $t_{PLH}$ uses $R_p$ (PMOS). Swapping them yields wrong answers. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion:** $C_L$ in **femtofarads** ($10^{-15}$ F) must be converted to farads before multiplication. KTU board examiners are strict on SI units. Loss: **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Omitting the path delay summation:** For multi-stage circuits, students often quote a single gate's delay and forget to **sum** all stages. Loss: **2–3 marks**.
>
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state "assuming input is an ideal step" and "ignoring internal capacitances" in derivations. Loss: **1 mark** for missing key assumptions.
>
> 6. **Misinterpreting Logical Effort:** $g = 1$ for inverter, **not** for NAND/NOR. The parasitic delay $p$ is also gate-dependent. Loss: **2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Propagation Delay:**

- ✅ **Two delays exist:** $t_{PHL}$ (fall) and $t_{PLH}$ (rise). The average is $t_P = (t_{PHL} + t_{PLH})/2$.
- ✅ **The 0.69 factor** is non-negotiable — it originates from $\ln(2) = 0.693$ when solving the RC discharge equation.
- ✅ **Fall delay uses $R_n$, rise delay uses $R_p$** — never mix them up.
- ✅ **PMOS is slower** than NMOS due to ~2.5× lower hole mobility; $t_{PLH} > t_{PHL}$ in standard CMOS.
- ✅ **Propagation delay is proportional to load capacitance $C_L$** and ON-resistance $R_{on}$.
- ✅ **Total path delay = sum of all individual stage delays** in the critical path.
- ✅ **Maximum clock frequency** for a synchronous system: $f_{max} = 1/T_{clk} \ge 1/t_{P,critical}$.
- ✅ **Logical Effort** gives optimal sizing: stage effort $\hat{f} = \sqrt[N]{F}$ where $F = GBH$.
- ✅ **Parasitic delay** $p$ is 1 (inverter), 2 (NAND/NOR), and 3 (XOR/AOI) — measured in units of $\tau \approx 3RC$.
- ✅ **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** is the standard FoM — lower is better; it is **energy per switching event**.
- ✅ **Switching energy** $E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is the dominant dynamic power term.
- ✅ **Contamination delay $t_{cd}$** is the *fastest* possible delay; it sets the **hold-time** constraint.
- ✅ **Fan-out scaling** is linear: $t_P(N) = t_{P0}(1 + N)$ in the first-order model.
- ✅ **Buffer insertion** reduces delay on long wires — the optimal number of buffers is $\sqrt{C_{wire}/C_{gate}}$.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Trend:** Expect at least **one 7-mark derivation** and **one 3-mark definition** on this topic per exam cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transition Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT304 - LOGIC CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transition Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Transition Time** in digital logic circuit design refers to the finite duration required for a logic signal (voltage or current) to change from one stable logic level to another (i.e., from logic **0** to logic **1**, or from logic **1** to logic **0**). It is a critical **dynamic (switching) performance parameter** of a logic gate, defining how *fast* the output of a gate can change state when the input changes.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBECT304 – Module 4), transition time is studied under the family of **dynamic characteristics** of logic gates, alongside propagation delay, contamination delay, rise time, fall time, and power-delay product.

There are **four standard transition time metrics** that KTU examiners routinely test:

1. **Rise Time ($t_r$)** — Time taken by the output signal to transition from **10 % to 90 %** of its final steady-state logic HIGH value.
2. **Fall Time ($t_f$)** — Time taken by the output signal to transition from **90 % to 10 %** of its initial steady-state logic HIGH value (i.e., while falling to LOW).
3. **Low-to-High Propagation Delay ($t_{PLH}$)** — Time delay measured from the **50 % point of the input transition** to the **50 % point of the corresponding output LOW → HIGH transition**.
4. **High-to-Low Propagation Delay ($t_{PHL}$)** — Time delay measured from the **50 % point of the input transition** to the **50 % point of the corresponding output HIGH → LOW transition**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Transition time is *not* the same as propagation delay. Propagation delay measures *when* the output reaches its 50 % point relative to the input. Transition time (rise/fall) measures *how long* the output itself takes to swing from one rail to the other. Both are essential for timing analysis in sequential and combinational circuits.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are **pouring water from one glass into another** through a narrow funnel:

- The **propagation delay** is the *waiting time* between when you start pouring (input changes) and when the receiving glass (output) starts to actually fill up noticeably.
- The **transition time (rise/fall)** is the *time it takes the water level in the receiving glass to climb from near-empty to near-full* — limited by how fast water can flow through the funnel.

If the funnel is wide (low resistance, high drive strength), the glass fills quickly — short transition time. If the funnel is narrow (high resistance, weak driver) or the glass is huge (large load capacitance), filling takes longer — long transition time.

In a logic gate, the "funnel" is the **transistor's ON-resistance**, and the "glass size" is the **load capacitance** ($C_L$) the gate must charge or discharge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight for KTU:** Transition time is fundamentally an **RC (Resistor–Capacitor) charging phenomenon**. Every logic gate output can be modeled as a switch (resistance $R$) charging or discharging a load capacitor ($C_L$). Therefore, transition times are direct functions of the **RC time constant** of the output node.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Measurement Points | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rise Time | $t_r$ | 10 % → 90 % of $V_{OH}$ | **ns / ps** |
| Fall Time | $t_f$ | 90 % → 10 % of $V_{OH}$ | **ns / ps** |
| Low-to-High Delay | $t_{PLH}$ | 50 % input → 50 % output (rising) | **ns / ps** |
| High-to-Low Delay | $t_{PHL}$ | 50 % input → 50 % output (falling) | **ns / ps** |
| Average Propagation Delay | $t_{pd}$ | $(t_{PLH} + t_{PHL})/2$ | **ns / ps** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Transition Time

Every logic gate output node has a **parasitic load capacitance** $C_L$ (sum of gate–source, gate–drain, and interconnect capacitances of the next stage). The output driver transistor (NMOS for pull-down, PMOS for pull-up in CMOS) acts as a **non-linear resistor** when ON.

When the input of the gate switches:

- The **output transistor** either turns ON (connecting output to $V_{DD}$ or GND) to **charge** or **discharge** $C_L$.
- The voltage across $C_L$ cannot change instantaneously — it must follow an **exponential RC curve** governed by the equation:

$$V_C(t) = V_{\text{final}} - (V_{\text{final}} - V_{\text{initial}}) \cdot e^{-t/(RC)}$$

This exponential charging/discharging is precisely what creates the finite **rise and fall times**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Why is the output of a logic gate unable to change instantaneously?"* The answer: **because the output node capacitance must be charged/discharged through a finite ON-resistance**, leading to an exponential RC transient. A perfect, instant transition would require **infinite drive current**, which is physically impossible.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

**Step 1 — Identify the Switching Event**
- The input to the gate crosses the switching threshold ($V_{th} \approx V_{DD}/2$).
- The output transistor begins to conduct.

**Step 2 — Determine the Charging/Discharging Path**
- **LOW → HIGH output transition:** PMOS transistor ON, charging $C_L$ through PMOS ON-resistance $R_p$ toward $V_{DD}$.
- **HIGH → LOW output transition:** NMOS transistor ON, discharging $C_L$ through NMOS ON-resistance $R_n$ toward GND.

**Step 3 — Apply the RC Charging Model**
- The output node voltage $V_{out}(t)$ rises (or falls) exponentially with time constant $\tau = R \cdot C_L$.

**Step 4 — Extract the 10–90 % (or 50 %) Points**
- For rise time: $V_{out}(t_1) = 0.1 V_{DD}$ and $V_{out}(t_2) = 0.9 V_{DD}$.
- For propagation delay: $V_{out}(t_d) = 0.5 V_{DD}$.

**Step 5 — Compute the Time Interval**
- $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$ gives $t_r$ or $t_f$.
- $t_d = t(\text{50 % output}) - t(\text{50 % input})$ gives $t_{PLH}$ or $t_{PHL}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_r = 2.2 \cdot R \cdot C_L$ | Rise time (10–90 %) for RC charging | Step input, single RC pole |
| 2 | $t_f = 2.2 \cdot R \cdot C_L$ | Fall time (90–10 %) for RC discharge | Step input, single RC pole |
| 3 | $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L$ | L→H propagation delay in CMOS | PMOS drives $C_L$ |
| 4 | $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L$ | H→L propagation delay in CMOS | NMOS drives $C_L$ |
| 5 | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay | Standard KTU definition |
| 6 | $t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ | Approx. average delay where $R_{eq} = (R_n + R_p)/2$ | Symmetric CMOS |
| 7 | $\tau = R \cdot C_L$ | Time constant of output node | Universal RC model |
| 8 | $PDP = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Power-Delay Product (energy per switch) | CMOS dynamic energy |
| 9 | $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Capacitor charging equation | Rising edge |
| 10 | $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | Capacitor discharging equation | Falling edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the magic number 0.69?**  
> It comes from $\ln(2) = 0.693$. When a capacitor charges from 0 to **50 %** of $V_{DD}$ through a resistor $R$, the time taken is $t = R C \ln(2) \approx 0.69 RC$. This is exactly the **50 % point** used to measure propagation delay. The factor **2.2** in rise time comes from $\ln(9) = \ln(0.9/0.1) \approx 2.197$, which is the time between the 10 % and 90 % points on an exponential curve.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transition time directly determines:

- **Maximum Clock Frequency** in synchronous digital systems: $f_{max} \le 1 / (t_r + t_f + \text{setup time})$.
- **Signal Integrity & Skew** in long interconnects (rise/fall time comparable to propagation delay ⇒ transmission-line effects).
- **Dynamic Power Dissipation** in CMOS: $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$, where the activity factor $\alpha$ depends on how often transitions occur.
- **Setup & Hold Timing Margins** in flip-flops — slow rise times can cause metastability.
- **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)** — sharper transitions (shorter $t_r$, $t_f$) generate more high-frequency harmonics.

In **high-speed processor design**, controlling transition times to within tight bounds (often **50–200 ps** for modern 7 nm CMOS) is one of the most critical jobs of a digital VLSI engineer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rise Time $t_r$ from First Principles

**Given:** A capacitor $C_L$ is being charged from $0$ V toward $V_{DD}$ through a resistor $R$ (the ON-resistance of the PMOS pull-up transistor). The input is a perfect step (instantaneous).

**Goal:** Find the time elapsed between the moment the capacitor voltage reaches **10 % of $V_{DD}$** and the moment it reaches **90 % of $V_{DD}$**.

**Step 1 — Write the charging equation for the capacitor voltage.**

For a simple RC circuit with a step input of magnitude $V_{DD}$, the capacitor voltage as a function of time is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/(R C_L)}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Find the time $t_1$ at which the output reaches 10 % of $V_{DD}$.**

We set $V_{out}(t_1) = 0.1 \cdot V_{DD}$:

$$0.1 \cdot V_{DD} = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_1/(R C_L)}\right)$$

Divide both sides by $V_{DD}$:

$$0.1 = 1 - e^{-t_1/(R C_L)}$$

Rearrange:

$$e^{-t_1/(R C_L)} = 0.9$$

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\frac{t_1}{R C_L} = \ln(0.9)$$

Therefore:

$$t_1 = -R C_L \cdot \ln(0.9) = R C_L \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{0.9}\right) = R C_L \cdot \ln(1.111\ldots)$$

**Step 3 — Find the time $t_2$ at which the output reaches 90 % of $V_{DD}$.**

We set $V_{out}(t_2) = 0.9 \cdot V_{DD}$:

$$0.9 \cdot V_{DD} = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_2/(R C_L)}\right)$$

Rearrange:

$$e^{-t_2/(R C_L)} = 0.1$$

Take natural logarithm:

$$-\frac{t_2}{R C_L} = \ln(0.1)$$

Therefore:

$$t_2 = -R C_L \cdot \ln(0.1) = R C_L \cdot \ln(10)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the rise time as the difference $t_r = t_2 - t_1$.**

$$t_r = t_2 - t_1 = R C_L \cdot \ln(10) - R C_L \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{0.9}\right)$$

Use the logarithm identity $\ln(a) - \ln(b) = \ln(a/b)$:

$$t_r = R C_L \cdot \ln\left(\frac{10}{1/0.9}\right) = R C_L \cdot \ln(9)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerical constant.**

We know that $\ln(9) = \ln(3^2) = 2 \ln(3) \approx 2 \times 1.0986 = 2.1972$.

Therefore, rounding to two decimal places:

$$t_r = 2.2 \cdot R \cdot C_L$$

This is the **standard textbook result** for the 10 %–90 % rise time of a first-order RC system. The **identical derivation** applies to fall time, since the discharging curve is just a mirror image.

$$\boxed{\,t_r = t_f \approx 2.2 \cdot R \cdot C_L\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Propagation Delay $t_{PLH}$ (Low-to-High) in CMOS

**Given:** A CMOS inverter output node with load capacitance $C_L$ is being charged by the PMOS pull-up transistor with equivalent ON-resistance $R_p$. Assume the input switches instantaneously at $t = 0$ and we want the time for the output to reach **50 % of $V_{DD}$** (i.e., the switching threshold).

**Step 1 — Initial condition.** The output was previously LOW, so $V_{out}(0) = 0$ V.

**Step 2 — Charging equation.** Once the input goes LOW, the PMOS turns ON and charges $C_L$:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/(R_p C_L)}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Set the output to 50 % of $V_{DD}$.**

$$0.5 \cdot V_{DD} = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_{PLH}/(R_p C_L)}\right)$$

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_{PLH}/(R_p C_L)}$$

$$e^{-t_{PLH}/(R_p C_L)} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_{PLH}$.**

$$-\frac{t_{PLH}}{R_p C_L} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln(2)$$

$$t_{PLH} = R_p C_L \cdot \ln(2)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the constant.**

Since $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931$:

$$\boxed{\,t_{PLH} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L\,}$$

By the same logic, the high-to-low delay is:

$$\boxed{\,t_{PHL} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L\,}$$

**Step 6 — Average propagation delay.** Adding and dividing by 2:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2} = 0.69 \cdot \frac{R_p + R_n}{2} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **[Example Problem — Typical KTU Numerical]**
> A CMOS logic gate drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 50$ fF. The equivalent ON-resistance of the PMOS pull-up is $R_p = 2 \,\text{k}\Omega$ and of the NMOS pull-down is $R_n = 1 \,\text{k}\Omega$. The supply voltage is $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V. Compute: (i) $t_{PLH}$, (ii) $t_{PHL}$, (iii) $t_{pd}$, and (iv) $t_r$.

**Solution:**

**(i) Low-to-High propagation delay:**

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 2000 \,\Omega \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \,\text{F}$$

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{s} = 6.9 \times 10^{-11} \,\text{s} = \mathbf{69 \,\text{ps}}$$

**(ii) High-to-Low propagation delay:**

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 1000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot 5.0 \times 10^{-11} = 3.45 \times 10^{-11} \,\text{s} = \mathbf{34.5 \,\text{ps}}$$

**(iii) Average propagation delay:**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2} = \frac{69 + 34.5}{2} = \frac{103.5}{2} = \mathbf{51.75 \,\text{ps}}$$

**(iv) Rise time (using average R):**

Using $R_{eq} = (R_p + R_n)/2 = 1.5 \,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$t_r = 2.2 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L = 2.2 \cdot 1500 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} = \mathbf{165 \,\text{ps}}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice the **asymmetry** between $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$. This happens because in CMOS, NMOS transistors are typically **2–3 times stronger** (lower ON-resistance) than PMOS transistors of the same size, since electron mobility $\mu_n$ is about 2–3× higher than hole mobility $\mu_p$. To equalize the delays, designers make PMOS transistors **wider** than NMOS — typically a **2 : 1** (PMOS : NMOS) width ratio in standard cell design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RC Output Model of a Logic Gate (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram captures the **functional architecture** of how a logic gate's transition time arises from the interaction of the driver transistor, its ON-resistance, and the load capacitance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal Vin] -->|Switching Event at t = 0| B[Driver Transistor acts as Switch]
    B -->|ON Resistance R| C[Output Node]
    C -->|Charging or Discharging| D[Load Capacitance C_L]
    D -->|Exponential Transient| E[Output Signal Vout t]
    F[Supply Rail V_DD or GND] -->|Connected via R| C
    G[Parasitic Capacitance C_parasitic] --> C
    H[Interconnect Capacitance C_interconnect] --> C
    D -.includes.-> G
    D -.includes.-> H
    E -->|Measured at 10% and 90%| I[Transition Time t_r or t_f]
    A -->|Measured at 50% point| J[Reference for Propagation Delay]
    E -->|Measured at 50% point| J
    J --> K[Propagation Delay t_PLH or t_PHL]
```

### 4.2 CMOS Inverter Transition — Pull-Up vs Pull-Down Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LOW_TO_HIGH["LOW to HIGH Transition t_PLH"]
        L1[Input goes LOW] --> L2[PMOS Transistor turns ON]
        L2 --> L3[Charging Path: V_DD to R_p to C_L to Output]
        L3 --> L4[Output rises from 0 to V_DD]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_TO_LOW["HIGH to LOW Transition t_PHL"]
        H1[Input goes HIGH] --> H2[NMOS Transistor turns ON]
        H2 --> H3[Discharging Path: Output to R_n to GND]
        H3 --> H4[Output falls from V_DD to 0]
    end
```

### 4.3 Timing Waveform — Annotated Signal Transitions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Input Voltage Waveform] --> W1[50% point of rising input]
    W --> W2[50% point of falling input]
    X[Output Voltage Waveform] --> X1[10% point Vout_rise]
    X --> X2[50% point of rising output]
    X --> X3[90% point Vout_rise]
    X --> X4[90% point Vout_fall]
    X --> X5[50% point of falling output]
    X --> X6[10% point Vout_fall]
    W1 -. t_PLH measurement .-> X2
    W2 -. t_PHL measurement .-> X5
    X1 -. t_r interval .-> X3
    X4 -. t_f interval .-> X6
```

### 4.4 State Diagram — Transition Time Computation Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IdentifySwitchingEvent
    IdentifySwitchingEvent --> DetermineDriver: Input crosses V_th
    DetermineDriver --> PMOSpath: LOW to HIGH Output
    DetermineDriver --> NMOSpath: HIGH to LOW Output
    PMOSpath --> RCChargeModel: t_PLH equals 0.69 R_p C_L
    NMOSpath --> RCDischargeModel: t_PHL equals 0.69 R_n C_L
    RCChargeModel --> ComputeRiseTime: t_r equals 2.2 R C_L
    RCDischargeModel --> ComputeFallTime: t_f equals 2.2 R C_L
    ComputeRiseTime --> AverageDelay: t_pd average of t_PLH and t_PHL
    ComputeFallTime --> AverageDelay
    AverageDelay --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The Mermaid `flowchart` blocks above use only **alphanumeric node identifiers** (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `LOW_TO_HIGH`) and **plain-text labels** (no markdown formatting inside the quoted labels). This complies with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> *Define the terms (i) rise time, (ii) fall time, and (iii) propagation delay of a logic gate. State the standard measurement points used for each.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Rise time ($t_r$):** It is the time taken by the output of a logic gate to transition from **10 % to 90 %** of its final steady-state HIGH value ($V_{OH}$) when the output changes from LOW to HIGH. It indicates the *speed of the rising edge* and is determined by the RC time constant at the output node: $t_r \approx 2.2 R C_L$.

(ii) **Fall time ($t_f$):** It is the time taken by the output to transition from **90 % to 10 %** of $V_{OH}$ when the output changes from HIGH to LOW. It characterizes the *speed of the falling edge* and follows the same RC model: $t_f \approx 2.2 R C_L$.

(iii) **Propagation delay ($t_{pd}$):** It is the time interval between the **50 % point of the input transition** and the **50 % point of the corresponding output transition**. There are two variants — $t_{PLH}$ (LOW to HIGH output) and $t_{PHL}$ (HIGH to LOW output). The average propagation delay is $t_{pd} = (t_{PLH} + t_{PHL})/2$.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> *Why can the output of a logic gate never change instantaneously? Explain the role of load capacitance and transistor ON-resistance.*

**Model Answer:**

The output of a logic gate cannot change instantaneously because every output node has a **parasitic load capacitance** $C_L$ (due to gate capacitances of the next stage, interconnect capacitance, and drain capacitances of the driver transistor itself). A capacitor's voltage cannot change in zero time — it must be charged or discharged through a finite current.

The current is supplied (or sunk) by the **driver transistor** operating in its linear (triode) region, which behaves as a non-linear resistor with ON-resistance $R$ (typically $R_n$ for NMOS pull-down, $R_p$ for PMOS pull-up).

The output voltage therefore follows the **RC charging/discharging equation**:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{\text{final}} - (V_{\text{final}} - V_{\text{initial}}) \cdot e^{-t/(R C_L)}$$

This **exponential transient** is the fundamental physical reason for finite rise/fall times and propagation delays. An instant transition would require **infinite drive current**, which is physically impossible.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **rise time $t_r$** of the output of a CMOS logic gate driving a load capacitance $C_L$ through an equivalent ON-resistance $R$. State clearly the measurement points used. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A CMOS gate has $R_p = 4 \,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_n = 2 \,\text{k}\Omega$, and drives a load of $C_L = 100$ fF with $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V. Compute $t_{PLH}$, $t_{PHL}$, average propagation delay $t_{pd}$, and rise time $t_r$. Comment on the asymmetry between $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps:

1. Write the RC charging equation:
   $$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/(R C_L)}\right)$$
2. Solve for the 10 % crossing time: $t_1 = R C_L \ln(1/0.9)$.
3. Solve for the 90 % crossing time: $t_2 = R C_L \ln(10)$.
4. Subtract: $t_r = t_2 - t_1 = R C_L \ln(9) \approx 2.2 R C_L$.

**Measurement points:** $t_r$ is measured from **10 % to 90 %** of the final HIGH output voltage $V_{OH}$ (or $V_{DD}$). The same RC model applies, with $R$ being the equivalent ON-resistance of the conducting transistor.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Low-to-High propagation delay:**

$$t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 4000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} = 2.76 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{s} = \mathbf{276 \,\text{ps}}$$

**(ii) High-to-Low propagation delay:**

$$t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 2000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} = 1.38 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{s} = \mathbf{138 \,\text{ps}}$$

**(iii) Average propagation delay:**

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2} = \frac{276 + 138}{2} = \mathbf{207 \,\text{ps}}$$

**(iv) Rise time:**

$$R_{eq} = \frac{R_p + R_n}{2} = \frac{4000 + 2000}{2} = 3000 \,\Omega$$

$$t_r = 2.2 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L = 2.2 \cdot 3000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} = 6.6 \times 10^{-10} \,\text{s} = \mathbf{660 \,\text{ps}}$$

**Comment on Asymmetry:** $t_{PLH}$ (276 ps) is **twice** $t_{PHL}$ (138 ps). This asymmetry arises because in standard CMOS processes, **NMOS transistors have approximately 2–3× higher carrier mobility** than PMOS transistors of identical dimensions. As a result, an NMOS pull-down has **lower ON-resistance** than a PMOS pull-up of the same width, so the HIGH-to-LOW transition (driven by NMOS) is *faster* than the LOW-to-HIGH transition (driven by PMOS). To symmetrize the delays, designers make the PMOS transistor **wider** (typically 2× the NMOS width), which lowers $R_p$ and equalizes the drive strengths.

---

> **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat RC equivalent model of a logic gate output, explain how **propagation delay** $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ arise. Derive the expressions for $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ in terms of ON-resistance and load capacitance. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the dynamic performance of **CMOS and TTL** logic families with respect to transition time, propagation delay, and power dissipation. Which family is preferred for high-density, low-power VLSI applications and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**RC Equivalent Model:** The output of any logic gate can be modeled as a **switch (driver transistor) + resistor $R$ + load capacitor $C_L$** connected to a rail ($V_{DD}$ for pull-up, GND for pull-down). The load capacitor represents the **sum of all capacitances** at the output node: gate capacitance of the fan-out gates, drain capacitance of the driver, and interconnect capacitance.

**LOW → HIGH transition ($t_{PLH}$):**
- Input goes LOW → PMOS turns ON → $C_L$ charges through $R_p$ from 0 V toward $V_{DD}$.
- $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}(1 - e^{-t/(R_p C_L)})$.
- Time to reach 50 % of $V_{DD}$: setting $V_{out} = 0.5 V_{DD}$ and solving gives $\boxed{t_{PLH} = 0.69 R_p C_L}$.

**HIGH → LOW transition ($t_{PHL}$):**
- Input goes HIGH → NMOS turns ON → $C_L$ discharges through $R_n$ from $V_{DD}$ to 0 V.
- $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/(R_n C_L)}$.
- Time to fall to 50 % of $V_{DD}$: $\boxed{t_{PHL} = 0.69 R_n C_L}$.

These finite delays arise because **the capacitor voltage cannot change instantaneously** — it must follow the exponential RC curve.

**Model Solution to (b):**

| Parameter | **CMOS** | **TTL (Bipolar)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Rise time ($t_r$)** | Moderate (depends on PMOS $R_p$, scaling improves with tech) | Fast (active pull-up gives strong drive) |
| **Fall time ($t_f$)** | Fast (NMOS is strong) | Fast (BJT has high drive) |
| **Propagation delay ($t_{pd}$)** | 10–100 ps (modern nanometer CMOS); older CMOS ≈ 1–10 ns | 1–10 ns (74LS, 74AS series) |
| **Static power dissipation** | **Extremely low** (nW range) — no DC path from $V_{DD}$ to GND | **High** (mW per gate) — bipolar transistors draw continuous current |
| **Dynamic power dissipation** | $P_d = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$; scales with $V_{DD}^2$ | Higher due to cross-conduction currents |
| **Noise margin** | Excellent (≈ 40 % of $V_{DD}$) | Moderate |
| **Fan-out** | Very high (DC input current ≈ 0) | Limited (input current drawn from previous stage) |
| **Integration density** | **Very high** (single transistor + poly gate per logic cell) | Lower (multiple BJTs + resistors per gate) |
| **Scalability with technology** | Excellent — Moore's law driver | Poor — bipolar devices do not scale well below ~100 nm |

**Conclusion:** For **high-density, low-power VLSI applications** (microprocessors, mobile SoCs, ASICs, FPGAs), **CMOS is overwhelmingly preferred** because:
1. **Near-zero static power** allows billions of gates on a single chip.
2. **$V_{DD}$ scaling** directly reduces dynamic power quadratically.
3. **High integration density** — one CMOS gate uses only 2 transistors (NMOS + PMOS), and as the technology node shrinks, both area and delay shrink together.
4. **Excellent scalability** with each new process node (180 nm → 65 nm → 7 nm → 3 nm).

TTL is now largely confined to **legacy industrial applications** and educational labs where its higher noise immunity and predictable analog behavior are useful.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing rise time with propagation delay.** Rise time = output's own 10–90 % swing time. Propagation delay = input 50 % to output 50 %. Both are different concepts, and examiners often give partial credit only if the student distinguishes them clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.69 vs 2.2 distinction.** The 0.69 factor is for the 50 % point (propagation delay). The 2.2 factor is for the 10–90 % interval (rise/fall time). Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Unit mistakes.** Capacitance in fF or pF, resistance in $\Omega$ or k$\Omega$ — always convert to **SI base units (F and $\Omega$)** before multiplying. Many students lose marks by writing "fF · k$\Omega$ = ns" without verifying.
> 4. **Not commenting on CMOS asymmetry.** When asked to compute $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$, simply presenting the numbers without explaining *why* $t_{PLH} \neq t_{PHL}$ (carrier mobility difference) loses the "design insight" marks.
> 5. **Using $R$ instead of $R_p$ or $R_n$.** Always specify which transistor's ON-resistance is being used — pull-up uses $R_p$, pull-down uses $R_n$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Transition time** is the time taken for a logic signal to change between two valid logic levels, and is fundamentally governed by the **RC time constant** at the output node of a logic gate.
- **Rise time ($t_r$)** is measured from **10 % to 90 %** of the output's final HIGH value; **fall time ($t_f$)** is measured from **90 % to 10 %** of the output's initial HIGH value.
- The **standard rise time formula** is $t_r = 2.2 R C_L$, derived from the RC exponential charging curve using $\ln(9) \approx 2.197$.
- **Propagation delay** $t_{pd}$ is measured from the **50 % point of the input** to the **50 % point of the output**, and equals $0.69 R C_L$ per transition. The magic number is $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$.
- **Average propagation delay** is $t_{pd} = (t_{PLH} + t_{PHL})/2 = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ where $R_{eq} = (R_p + R_n)/2$.
- In **CMOS**, $t_{PLH} = 0.69 R_p C_L$ and $t_{PHL} = 0.69 R_n C_L$. Since NMOS is stronger than PMOS, $t_{PHL} < t_{PLH}$ unless the PMOS is intentionally widened (typically **2 : 1 W/L ratio**).
- **Transition time cannot be zero** because load capacitance must be charged/discharged through a finite ON-resistance — perfect, instant switching would require infinite current.
- Transition times directly limit the **maximum clock frequency** of a digital system: $f_{max} \approx 1/(t_r + t_f + t_{setup})$.
- **Dynamic power** in CMOS scales as $P_d = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — lower $V_{DD}$ and lower $C_L$ reduce power quadratically/linearly.
- **CMOS** is preferred over **TTL** for modern VLSI due to **near-zero static power, high density, and excellent scalability**.
- For KTU numerical problems, **always convert to SI units** (Farads, Ohms) before computing, and **show the substitution step explicitly** to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT304 - LOGIC CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power-Delay Product) and TTL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Power-Delay Product (PDP) & TTL Logic Family

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Power-Delay Product (PDP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Power-Delay Product (PDP)**, also known as the *switching energy* or *energy-delay product*, is defined as the product of the average power dissipation ($P_{avg}$) of a logic gate and its average propagation delay ($t_{pd}$). It represents the **energy consumed by the gate per one logic transition (switching event)**.

Mathematically:

$$PDP = P_{avg} \times t_{pd}$$

The standard SI unit of PDP is the **Joule (J)**, but in modern VLSI and digital IC design, it is almost universally expressed in **picojoules (pJ)** because each switching event in CMOS/TTL consumes only trillionths of a joule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PDP is called the "Figure of Merit" of a Logic Family:**
> Power alone is not enough to judge a logic family (low power may mean slow speed). Delay alone is not enough (fast speed may mean high power). The **PDP combines both into a single energy number**, making it the most honest metric for comparing logic families such as TTL, CMOS, ECL, and BiCMOS.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Fuel Economy" of Logic Gates

Think of a logic gate as a **car engine**:
- **Power dissipation** ($P_{avg}$) is like the *fuel consumption rate* (liters per hour).
- **Propagation delay** ($t_{pd}$) is like the *time taken to complete a trip*.
- The product **Power × Delay = Energy per trip** (e.g., liters consumed to go from A to B).

A car that uses less fuel *per trip* is more efficient — exactly the same way a logic gate with lower PDP is more efficient. Just like we compare cars using **km/liter**, digital designers compare logic families using **picojoules per switching event**.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Insight:** A *good* logic family has **low PDP**. TTL standard has PDP ≈ 100 pJ, while modern CMOS can drop below 1 pJ — a 100× improvement over four decades.

---

### 1.3 TTL — Transistor-Transistor Logic (Formal Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **TTL Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Transistor-Transistor Logic (TTL)** is a class of digital logic circuits built using **bipolar junction transistors (BJTs)** and resistors, in which the logical function is performed entirely by transistors (replacing the diodes used in the earlier DTL family). It became the dominant logic family from the late 1960s through the 1990s and forms the historical backbone of the famous **74xx series** of integrated circuits.

**Key Identity of TTL:**
- Uses **multi-emitter transistor** at the input (a defining innovation by James L. Buie of TRW, 1961).
- Uses a **totem-pole / pull-up pull-down** output stage.
- Operates from a single **+5 V supply**.
- Has **standardized noise margins of ≈ 0.4 V** (both LOW and HIGH).

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — TTL as a "Water-Tap System"

Imagine the TTL output stage as a **two-valve water tap**:
- **Q3 (upper transistor)** = the "cold water" valve (pulls output **HIGH**).
- **Q5 (lower transistor)** = the "hot water" valve (pulls output **LOW**).
- The two valves are **never opened simultaneously** — this is the role of the phase-splitter transistor Q2.
- The result: water (current) flows either **up from Vcc** to the output, or **down from the output to ground** — but never both at once. This is why TTL is fast and symmetric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The totem-pole arrangement gives TTL its **low output impedance in both states**, allowing it to drive capacitive loads quickly — which is the secret behind TTL's relatively short propagation delay (~10 ns for standard TTL).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power-Delay Product — Theoretical Framework

The PDP is computed for the **worst-case static power plus dynamic switching power**, multiplied by the **average propagation delay**.

### 2.1.1 Components of Power Dissipation

A logic gate dissipates power in **two regimes**:

1. **Static Power Dissipation ($P_{static}$):**
   Power consumed when the output is sitting in a steady HIGH or LOW state.
   - In TTL: caused by the totem-pole current path and the biasing resistors.
   - In CMOS: ideally zero (only leakage), practically small.

2. **Dynamic Power Dissipation ($P_{dynamic}$):**
   Power consumed when the output switches — charging/discharging load and parasitic capacitances.
   - Dominant in CMOS.
   - Formula: $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f$, where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor.

$$P_{avg} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic}$$

### 2.1.2 Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$)

The **average propagation delay** is defined as:

$$t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$

where:
- $t_{pLH}$ = delay when output goes **LOW → HIGH**.
- $t_{pHL}$ = delay when output goes **HIGH → LOW**.

### 2.1.3 Energy per Switching Event

Since **Energy = Power × Time**, the PDP physically represents the energy drawn from the supply for *one* logic transition:

$$E_{switch} = PDP = P_{avg} \times t_{pd} \;\; [\text{Joules}]$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of PDP Calculation

1. **Measure** $V_{CC}$ (supply voltage, typically 5 V).
2. **Measure** $I_{CC}$ (supply current for the gate) under worst-case conditions.
3. **Compute** $P_{avg} = V_{CC} \times I_{CC}$.
4. **Measure** $t_{pd}$ from the 50% input to 50% output transition on an oscilloscope.
5. **Multiply** $P_{avg} \times t_{pd}$ to obtain PDP in joules.
6. **Convert** to picojoules: $PDP_{pJ} = PDP_{J} \times 10^{12}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Critical)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Typical Unit | Boundary / Range |
|---|----------|--------|---------------------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | Average Power Dissipation | $P_{avg}$ | $V_{CC} \times I_{CC}$ | mW | 1 – 25 mW (TTL) |
| 2 | Propagation Delay (HIGH→LOW) | $t_{pHL}$ | Measured at 50% points | ns | 4 – 22 ns (TTL) |
| 3 | Propagation Delay (LOW→HIGH) | $t_{pLH}$ | Measured at 50% points | ns | 5 – 15 ns (TTL) |
| 4 | Average Propagation Delay | $t_{pd}$ | $(t_{pLH} + t_{pHL})/2$ | ns | 5 – 15 ns (TTL std.) |
| 5 | Power-Delay Product | $PDP$ | $P_{avg} \times t_{pd}$ | pJ | 10 – 200 pJ |
| 6 | Dynamic Power (CMOS) | $P_{dyn}$ | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | W | Dominant in CMOS |
| 7 | Fan-Out of TTL | $N$ | $I_{OL(max)}/I_{IL(max)}$ | unitless | 10 (standard TTL) |
| 8 | Noise Margin LOW | $NM_L$ | $V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | V | 0.4 V (TTL) |
| 9 | Noise Margin HIGH | $NM_H$ | $V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | V | 0.4 V (TTL) |
| 10 | TTL Supply Voltage | $V_{CC}$ | Fixed by spec | V | **+5 V ± 0.25 V** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The single most-forgotten number is **$V_{CC} = +5\,\text{V}$** for TTL. Writing $\pm 5\,\text{V}$ or $\pm 12\,\text{V}$ will cost you marks — TTL is **strictly +5 V**.

---

## 2.4 TTL — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.4.1 Evolution of TTL Sub-Families

TTL is not one monolithic family — it has seven sub-families, each a trade-off between speed and power.

| Sub-Family | Prefix | $t_{pd}$ (ns) | $P_{avg}$ (mW) | PDP (pJ) | Comment |
|------------|--------|---------------|----------------|----------|---------|
| Standard TTL | 74xx | 10 | 10 | 100 | Original 1965 |
| Low-Power TTL | 74Lxx | 33 | 1 | 33 | Slow, low power |
| High-Speed TTL | 74Hxx | 6 | 22 | 132 | Fast, hungry |
| Schottky TTL | 74Sxx | 3 | 19 | 57 | Anti-saturation |
| Low-Power Schottky | 74LSxx | 9 | 2 | 18 | Sweet spot |
| Advanced Schottky | 74ASxx | 1.7 | 8 | 14 | Fast + Schottky |
| Advanced Low-P Schottky | 74ALSxx | 4 | 1 | 4 | Modern preference |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Insight:** 74LS (Low-power Schottky) was the *industry workhorse* of the 1980s and is still widely cited in KTU question papers.

### 2.4.2 The Five-Transistor Building Block of Standard TTL

Every TTL gate has **five functional blocks** built around five transistors (Q1 to Q5):

| Transistor | Function | Mode of Operation |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Q1** | Multi-emitter input transistor | Performs AND logic on inputs |
| **Q2** | Phase-splitter transistor | Splits signal into two anti-phase outputs |
| **Q3** | Upper totem-pole transistor | Pulls output HIGH (active pull-up) |
| **Q4** | Bias/diode transistor | Steers Q3 / Q5 base current |
| **Q5** | Lower totem-pole transistor | Pulls output LOW (active pull-down) |

### 2.4.3 Operational Modes of TTL NAND Gate

The most commonly analyzed TTL gate is the **2-input NAND (74xx00)**.

**Mode 1: Both inputs HIGH (A = B = 1)**
- All base-emitter junctions of Q1 are reverse-biased.
- Current from $R_1$ flows through Q1's **base-collector** junction into Q2's base.
- Q2 turns ON → drives Q5 ON, Q4 OFF.
- **Output = LOW** ✓ (NAND behavior)

**Mode 2: Any input LOW (A = 0 or B = 0)**
- The corresponding base-emitter junction of Q1 is forward-biased.
- Current from $R_1$ is shunted *out* through the low input (to ground).
- Q2 receives no base current → Q2 OFF.
- Q5 OFF, Q3/Q4 conduct via $R_2$ → Q4 acts as emitter follower.
- **Output = HIGH** ✓ (NAND behavior)

### 2.4.4 Why TTL Uses a Multi-Emitter Transistor

The multi-emitter transistor is **TTL's defining innovation**. It replaces the **input diode AND-gate** of the older DTL family. Advantages:
- Fewer components → higher integration density.
- Faster turn-off because stored charge in the base can be drained rapidly.
- Performs logic directly with transistors → gives the family its name.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often call TTL "Transistor-Transistor Logic" and then describe diodes. **TTL does NOT use input diodes.** The AND function is performed by the **multiple emitters of Q1**.

### 2.4.5 The Totem-Pole Output Stage — Why It Matters

The totem-pole (Q3 + Q4 + Q5) gives TTL two huge benefits:
1. **Low output impedance in both states** → fast charging/discharging of load capacitance.
2. **High fan-out capability** (can drive ≥ 10 standard TTL loads).

**However**, the totem-pole has one limitation: outputs **cannot be connected together** (no wired-OR). To overcome this, **open-collector TTL** and **tri-state TTL** variants were developed.

### 2.4.6 Tri-State TTL

Tri-state TTL adds a **diode (D2)** and a **control transistor** to the basic totem-pole. When the enable input is LOW, both Q3 and Q5 are forced OFF, producing a **high-impedance (Hi-Z) output**. This allows multiple outputs to share a common bus — the foundation of microprocessor data buses.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-Delay Product** is the headline metric used by every logic-family datasheet (TI, ON Semi, NXP) and is the parameter chip designers minimize in modern FinFET/CMOS processes.
- **TTL** dominated the **minicomputer era (PDP-11, VAX)** and **early microprocessors (8086, Z80)**. The 74LS244, 74LS245, 74LS374 bus drivers you find on legacy boards are all TTL.
- Even today, **TTL-compatible input thresholds** are designed into CMOS ICs (e.g., 74HCT, 74ACT) so that CMOS chips can be interfaced directly with TTL logic.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power-Delay Product from First Principles

### Step 1: Start with the definition of electrical power

$$P_{avg} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC}}{1}$$

This is just Ohm's law applied to the supply rail — the average power drawn by the gate equals supply voltage multiplied by average supply current.

### Step 2: Multiply both sides by the average propagation delay

Multiplying both sides of the power equation by the time $t_{pd}$:

$$P_{avg} \cdot t_{pd} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC} \cdot t_{pd}$$

### Step 3: Recognize the right-hand side as energy

By definition, **Energy = Power × Time**. Therefore:

$$E_{switch} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC} \cdot t_{pd}$$

### Step 4: Substitute back to PDP

$$\boxed{PDP = P_{avg} \times t_{pd} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC} \cdot t_{pd} \;\;\;[\text{Joules}]}$$

### Step 5: Numerical evaluation (Worked Example)

Given a standard 74LS00 gate:
- $V_{CC} = +5\,\text{V}$
- $I_{CC} = 0.8\,\text{mA}$ (worst-case)
- $t_{pd} = 9\,\text{ns}$

$$P_{avg} = V_{CC} \times I_{CC} = 5 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{W} = 4\,\text{mW}$$

$$PDP = P_{avg} \times t_{pd} = (4 \times 10^{-3}) \times (9 \times 10^{-9}) = 36 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{J}$$

$$\boxed{PDP = 36\;\text{pJ}}$$

This matches the published 74LS00 datasheet value of ≈ **18 – 36 pJ** (the lower bound assumes per-gate power, not package power).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TTL Voltage-Transfer Characteristic (VTC) — Three Regions

The TTL NAND gate's voltage-transfer curve has three identifiable regions:

### Step 1: Cut-off Region (Input $V_{in} < 0.4$ V)

When $V_{in}$ is small, Q1's base-emitter junction connected to that input is forward-biased, shunting base current away from Q2. Q2 is in **cut-off**, so Q5 is OFF. Q3 is ON, output is HIGH ≈ $V_{OH}$.

### Step 2: Transition Region (0.4 V $< V_{in} <$ 1.3 V)

As $V_{in}$ rises, Q1's input emitter junction turns OFF. Q2 begins to conduct, its collector voltage falls, Q3 begins to turn OFF, Q5 begins to turn ON. The output swings from HIGH to LOW.

### Step 3: Saturation Region ($V_{in} > 1.3$ V)

Q2 is fully ON, its collector voltage is low enough that Q3/Q4 are OFF. Q5 is **saturated**, pulling output to $V_{OL} \approx 0.2$ V.

Mathematically, the critical switching voltage is:

$$V_{IL} \approx 0.8\,\text{V}, \quad V_{IH} \approx 2.0\,\text{V}, \quad V_{OL} \approx 0.4\,\text{V}, \quad V_{OH} \approx 2.4\,\text{V}$$

Therefore:

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4\,\text{V}$$

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 2.4 - 2.0 = 0.4\,\text{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Both noise margins are 0.4 V for standard TTL** — a value to be memorized for KTU boards.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fan-Out Calculation for Standard TTL

### Step 1: Define Fan-Out

Fan-out is the maximum number of standard loads a gate can drive while maintaining guaranteed voltage levels.

$$N = \frac{I_{OL(max)}}{I_{IL(max)}}$$

### Step 2: Insert typical TTL specifications
- $I_{OL(max)} = 16\,\text{mA}$ (max sink current, output LOW).
- $I_{IL(max)} = 1.6\,\text{mA}$ (max input current, input LOW).

### Step 3: Compute

$$N = \frac{16\,\text{mA}}{1.6\,\text{mA}} = 10$$

$$\boxed{\text{Fan-Out of Standard TTL} = 10}$$

This matches every datasheet — **TTL fan-out is 10** (and is one of the most frequently-asked KTU values).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — TTL Sub-Families

| Parameter | 74xx (Std) | 74Lxx (LP) | 74Hxx (HS) | 74Sxx (Sch) | 74LSxx (LS) | 74ASxx | 74ALSxx |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------|------------|--------|---------|
| $t_{pd}$ (ns) | 10 | 33 | 6 | 3 | 9 | 1.7 | 4 |
| $P_{avg}$ (mW) | 10 | 1 | 22 | 19 | 2 | 8 | 1 |
| PDP (pJ) | 100 | 33 | 132 | 57 | 18 | 14 | 4 |
| Fan-Out | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 20 | 40 | 20 |
| Speed-Power ratio | 1× | 0.33× | 1.32× | 0.57× | 0.18× | 0.14× | 0.04× |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Conclusion:** As you move down the table, PDP *decreases* — meaning each newer sub-family is more energy-efficient per switching event.

---

## 3.5 Comparison of TTL with CMOS (KTU Hot Topic)

| Property | TTL (74LS00) | CMOS (74HC00) |
|----------|--------------|---------------|
| Active Device | BJT | MOSFET |
| Power Dissipation | ~2 mW / gate | ~10 nW static |
| Propagation Delay | ~9 ns | ~8 ns (HC) |
| PDP | ~18 pJ | < 1 pJ |
| Fan-Out | 10 – 20 | ≥ 50 |
| Supply Voltage | +5 V (fixed) | 2 – 6 V |
| Input current | High (1.6 mA LOW) | Essentially 0 |
| Noise Margin | 0.4 V | ≈ 1 V (HC) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Verdict:** CMOS has *vastly* better PDP because of near-zero static power. TTL's historical advantage was **speed at low voltage** in the era before fine-line CMOS existed.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TTL NAND Gate — Circuit-Level Schematic

> *A physical schematic requires exact BJT symbols with multiple emitters, diodes, and resistors — beyond Mermaid's syntax. We therefore provide a faithful **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping every transistor's role.*

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT A"] --> ME1["EMITTER 1 OF Q1"]
    B["INPUT B"] --> ME2["EMITTER 2 OF Q1"]
    ME1 --> Q1["Q1 MULTI EMITTER TRANSISTOR\nAND FUNCTION ON INPUTS"]
    ME2 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2["Q2 PHASE SPLITTER\nSPLITS SIGNAL INVERTED AND NON INVERTED"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Q3 TOTEM POLE UPPER\nPULLS OUTPUT TO VCC"]
    Q2 --> Q4["Q4 BIAS TRANSISTOR\nSTEERS Q3 AND Q5"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Q5 TOTEM POLE LOWER\nPULLS OUTPUT TO GROUND"]
    Q3 --> OUT["OUTPUT Y"]
    Q5 --> OUT
    VCC["VCC PLUS 5V"] --> R1["R1 4 KILOHM"] --> Q1
    VCC --> R2["R2 1.6 KILOHM"] --> Q3
    VCC --> R3["R3 130 OHM"] --> Q4
    D1["D1 INPUT CLAMP DIODE\nPROTECTS AGAINST ESD"] --> ME1
    D1 --> ME2
    OUT --> Y["FINAL OUTPUT Y"]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Left side:** Inputs A and B enter Q1's multiple emitters.
- **D1:** Clamp diode — protects against negative electrostatic discharge.
- **R1:** Base resistor — feeds bias current to Q1.
- **Q2:** Phase-splitter — produces two anti-phase signals driving the totem-pole.
- **Right side:** Q3 (top), Q5 (bottom) form the totem-pole output; Q4 is the bias/steering element.
- **Bottom rail:** Ground reference for Q5 emitter.

---

## 4.2 Power-Delay Product Trade-off Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRADEOFF["SPEED POWER TRADEOFF IN LOGIC FAMILIES"]
        direction LR
        HP["HIGH POWER\nHIGH SPEED\nEXAMPLE ECL 74Hxx"] --> BAL["BALANCED\nEXAMPLE STD TTL 74xx"] --> LP["LOW POWER\nLOW SPEED\nEXAMPLE 74Lxx"]
    end
    BAL --> MET["METRIC OF CHOICE"]
    LP --> MET
    HP --> MET
    MET["POWER DELAY PRODUCT PDP\nLOWER IS BETTER\nUNIT pJ"] --> WIN["WINNER\n74ALSxx PDP APPROX 4 pJ"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The diagram shows that **no single parameter (power alone or delay alone) suffices** — only PDP captures the joint optimization. This is the central message of the topic.

---

## 4.3 TTL Output Stage — Totem-Pole State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> HiZ
    HiZ --> HIGH: ENABLE LOW AND INPUT CONDITION MET
    HIGH --> LOW: BOTH INPUTS BECOME HIGH
    LOW --> HIGH: ANY INPUT GOES LOW
    HIGH --> HiZ: ENABLE HIGH TRI STATE
    LOW --> HiZ: ENABLE HIGH TRI STATE
    HiZ --> [*]
```

### Interpretation

- **HIGH state:** Q3 ON, Q5 OFF → output ≈ $V_{CC} - V_{BE} \approx 3.4$ V.
- **LOW state:** Q5 ON (saturated), Q3 OFF → output ≈ $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V.
- **Hi-Z (Tri-state):** Both Q3 and Q5 forced OFF — output floats.

---

## 4.4 TTL Sub-Family Evolution Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BASE["STD TTL 74xx 1965\nPDP 100 pJ"] --> LP["LOW POWER 74Lxx\nPDP 33 pJ"]
    BASE --> HS["HIGH SPEED 74Hxx\nPDP 132 pJ"]
    BASE --> SCH["SCHOTTKY 74Sxx 1971\nANTI SATURATION\nPDP 57 pJ"]
    SCH --> AS["ADVANCED SCHOTTKY 74ASxx\nPDP 14 pJ"]
    SCH --> LS["LOW POWER SCHOTTKY 74LSxx 1976\nPDP 18 pJ"]
    LS --> ALS["ADVANCED LOW POWER SCHOTTKY 74ALSxx\nPDP 4 pJ"]
    ALS --> FAST["FAST 74Fxx\nFAIRCHILD"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU boards:** "**S**chottky **S**aves **S**aturation" — Schottky diodes clamp the BJT collector-base junction, preventing it from saturating deeply, which dramatically reduces storage time and propagation delay.
