# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Power-Delay Product. Why is it considered the figure of merit of a logic family?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** of a logic gate is defined as the product of its average power dissipation ($P_{avg}$) and its average propagation delay ($t_{pd}$):
>
> $$PDP = P_{avg} \times t_{pd} \;\; [\text{Joules, typically pJ}]$$
>
> It is called the **figure of merit** because neither power alone nor delay alone gives a complete picture of a logic family's performance. A gate could have low power but be unacceptably slow (e.g., 74Lxx), or be fast but consume unacceptable power (e.g., 74Hxx). PDP combines both into a single **energy-per-transition** number — the most honest single-number metric. The lower the PDP, the more efficient the logic family. **Standard TTL** has PDP ≈ 100 pJ, whereas **modern CMOS** achieves < 1 pJ, a 100× improvement.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**What is the role of the multi-emitter transistor in a TTL gate?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **multi-emitter transistor (Q1)** at the input of a TTL gate performs the **AND function** on the input signals and acts as the input stage. Each emitter corresponds to one logic input. When **all** emitters are HIGH, the base-emitter junctions are reverse-biased, and base current flows into Q2, causing the output to go LOW. When **any** emitter is LOW, that base-emitter junction is forward-biased, shunting base current to ground, turning Q2 OFF, and causing the output to go HIGH.
>
> This implementation:
> 1. Replaces the input diode AND-gate of the older DTL family.
> 2. Reduces the number of components and improves integration density.
> 3. Provides fast turn-off of stored base charge, improving speed.
>
> The use of multiple transistors in the logic function is precisely what gives TTL its name — **Transistor-Transistor Logic**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> KTU Pattern: Each 14-mark question has two 7-mark sub-parts. We provide **TWO alternative full-questions** — students may answer either.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**A. (a) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 2-input TTL NAND gate. Discuss the totem-pole output stage and the role of the multi-emitter input transistor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A standard TTL 2-input NAND gate (e.g., 74xx00) consists of five transistors Q1–Q5, three resistors R1, R2, R3, and an input clamp diode D1, all powered from a single +5 V supply.

**Input Stage — Q1 (Multi-emitter transistor):**
- Q1 has two emitters — one for each input A and B.
- R1 (4 kΩ) supplies base current to Q1 from $V_{CC}$.
- **Case 1 — Both inputs HIGH:** Both base-emitter junctions of Q1 are reverse-biased. Current from R1 flows through Q1's **base-collector junction** into Q2's base.
- **Case 2 — Either input LOW:** The corresponding base-emitter junction is forward-biased. Base current is shunted to ground through the LOW input. Q2 receives no base drive.

**Phase-Splitter — Q2:**
- When Q2 receives base current, it turns ON.
- Its **collector voltage falls**, which turns OFF the upper totem-pole transistors Q3 and Q4.
- Its **emitter voltage rises**, which turns ON Q5 (lower totem-pole).
- Output is pulled **LOW** via Q5 saturation.

**Totem-Pole Output Stage — Q3, Q4, Q5:**
- Q3 + Q4 form the active pull-up (HIGH driver).
- Q5 forms the active pull-down (LOW driver).
- Only one side conducts at a time — Q2 ensures this anti-phase operation.
- **Benefit:** Low output impedance in both states, allowing fast charging of load capacitances.

**Diode D1:**
- Clamp diode at the input protects against negative-going electrostatic discharge (ESD) below −0.7 V.

**Truth Table Summary:**

| A | B | Q1's state | Q2 | Q3, Q4 | Q5 | Output Y |
|---|---|------------|----|----|----|----------|
| 0 | 0 | Both EB ON | OFF | ON | OFF | **1 (HIGH)** |
| 0 | 1 | One EB ON | OFF | ON | OFF | **1 (HIGH)** |
| 1 | 0 | One EB ON | OFF | ON | OFF | **1 (HIGH)** |
| 1 | 1 | Both EB OFF | ON | OFF | ON | **0 (LOW)** |

The output $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$, confirming **NAND** operation.

---

**A. (b) Define Power-Delay Product. A standard TTL gate has $V_{CC} = 5$ V, $I_{CC} = 4$ mA, $t_{pd} = 10$ ns. Calculate (i) average power dissipation, (ii) Power-Delay Product in pJ, and (iii) compare its PDP with 74LSxx which has PDP = 18 pJ. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Average Power Dissipation:**

$$P_{avg} = V_{CC} \times I_{CC} = 5\,\text{V} \times 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A} = 20 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = 20\,\text{mW}}$$

**(ii) Power-Delay Product:**

$$PDP = P_{avg} \times t_{pd} = (20 \times 10^{-3}) \times (10 \times 10^{-9}) = 200 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{J}$$

$$\boxed{PDP = 200\,\text{pJ}}$$

**(iii) Comparison with 74LSxx:**

The standard TTL gate has PDP = 200 pJ, while 74LSxx has PDP = 18 pJ.

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{PDP_{std}}{PDP_{LS}} = \frac{200}{18} \approx 11.11$$

The 74LSxx gate consumes approximately **11 times less energy per switching event** than standard TTL. This is because 74LSxx uses:
- Schottky diodes (anti-saturation) → faster switching → lower $t_{pd}$.
- Higher-value resistors → lower static current → lower $P_{avg}$.

> **Conclusion:** 74LSxx is far superior in terms of energy efficiency, which is why it became the dominant TTL sub-family in the 1980s.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**B. (a) Explain with necessary diagrams the totem-pole output stage of TTL. Why are open-collector and tri-state TTL outputs needed? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Totem-Pole Output Stage:**
The totem-pole is the signature output stage of TTL, consisting of three transistors Q3, Q4, Q5 arranged in a stack from $V_{CC}$ to ground:
- **Q3** is a Darlington pair with **Q4** acting as an emitter follower for active pull-up.
- **Q5** is a switching transistor for active pull-down.
- **130 Ω resistor R3** limits current spikes during the brief overlap when both Q4 and Q5 conduct simultaneously.

**Why Totem-Pole is used:**
1. **Low output impedance in both states** — about 10–20 Ω in HIGH state (via emitter follower) and 5–10 Ω in LOW state (via saturated Q5).
2. **Symmetric rise/fall times** — leads to nearly equal $t_{pLH}$ and $t_{pHL}$.
3. **High drive capability** — fan-out of 10 (standard TTL) to 40 (74AS).
4. **Fast charging of capacitive loads** — critical for high-speed operation.

**Why Open-Collector TTL is needed:**
The totem-pole **cannot be connected in parallel** — if two totem-poles drive the same wire with opposite states, they will short $V_{CC}$ to ground, destroying the IC. **Open-collector** output removes Q3 and Q4, leaving only Q5 with its collector floating. An **external pull-up resistor** to $V_{CC}$ is required. Benefits:
- Allows **wired-AND** connection of multiple outputs.
- Allows level shifting to a different supply voltage.
- Allows driving high-current loads (relays, LEDs).

**Why Tri-State TTL is needed:**
For **bidirectional buses** (used in microprocessors), only one device at a time can drive the bus, but all devices must be able to read from it. Tri-state TTL adds an **enable input** that, when HIGH, forces both Q3 and Q5 OFF, producing a **high-impedance (Hi-Z)** state that effectively disconnects the gate from the bus. This allows multiple outputs to share a common bus safely.

---

**B. (b) Compare TTL and CMOS logic families in terms of PDP, fan-out, supply voltage, and noise margin. State two applications where TTL is still preferred. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | TTL (74LS00) | CMOS (74HC00) |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| Active device | BJT | MOSFET |
| Supply Voltage | +5 V (fixed) | 2 V – 6 V (variable) |
| Power Dissipation | ~2 mW / gate | ~10 nW static (negligible) |
| Propagation Delay | ~9 ns | ~8 ns (HC) |
| PDP | ~18 pJ | < 1 pJ |
| Fan-Out | 20 (LS) | ≥ 50 |
| Noise Margin LOW | 0.4 V | ≈ 0.9 V |
| Noise Margin HIGH | 0.4 V | ≈ 0.9 V |
| Input Current | 1.6 mA (LOW) | ≈ 0 (very high input impedance) |

**Comparison Commentary:**
- **PDP:** CMOS is dramatically better because static power is essentially zero.
- **Fan-out:** CMOS wins due to near-zero input current.
- **Supply voltage:** CMOS is more flexible (anywhere from 2 to 6 V); TTL is locked at +5 V.
- **Noise margin:** CMOS has roughly twice the noise margin of TTL.

**Applications where TTL is still preferred:**
1. **Legacy industrial control systems** — older CNC machines, PLCs, and instrumentation are built entirely around 74LS and 74AS series. Replacing them with CMOS would require board redesign.
2. **High-speed, low-voltage digital signal processing interfaces** — TTL-compatible signal levels (e.g., 74HCT family) are still used in bus interfaces (PCI, GTLP) because of their well-defined thresholds and predictable AC behavior at moderate clock rates.

> **Modern verdict:** For all new designs, CMOS (HC, HCT, AC, ACT) or BiCMOS (LVT, ALVT) is preferred. TTL survives in legacy, educational, and certain interface contexts.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Writing "TTL uses input diodes"** — Wrong. TTL uses the **multi-emitter transistor** Q1. This alone loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the supply voltage** — TTL is **+5 V only**. Writing ±5 V or 12 V costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Stating PDP unit as Watts** — Wrong. PDP is **Joules (or pJ)** because it is energy, not power.
> 4. **Not mentioning the totem-pole** — A TTL answer without the totem-pole is incomplete; lose up to 3 marks.
> 5. **Confusing $t_{pLH}$ and $t_{pHL}$** — These are *different*; the average is $t_{pd} = (t_{pLH}+t_{pHL})/2$.
> 6. **Skipping the truth table** — Every KTU NAND-gate answer **must include a truth table** for full marks.
> 7. **Stating fan-out of TTL as "10" universally** — 10 is for **standard TTL**. Other sub-families differ (74LS = 20, 74AS = 40).
> 8. **Using "fan-in" wrongly** — Fan-in is the *loading* a gate presents to its driver, not a property of the gate itself.

---

## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Exam Quick-Fire):**

- [x] **PDP definition:** $PDP = P_{avg} \times t_{pd}$, unit **Joules (pJ)**.
- [x] PDP is the **figure of merit** — lower is better.
- [x] **TTL** = Transistor-Transistor Logic, introduced **1965**, supply **+5 V**.
- [x] **Multi-emitter Q1** performs the AND function — *not* diodes.
- [x] **Q2** is the phase-splitter; **Q3+Q4** are the totem-pole upper, **Q5** is lower.
- [x] **Totem-pole** = low output impedance both states → fast and symmetric.
- [x] **Noise margins** of standard TTL = **0.4 V (LOW) and 0.4 V (HIGH)**.
- [x] **Fan-out** of standard TTL = **10**; of 74LS = **20**.
- [x] **Schottky TTL (74S/74LS)** uses **Schottky diodes** to prevent deep saturation, reducing storage time and improving speed.
- [x] **Open-collector TTL** needs an **external pull-up resistor**; allows wired-AND.
- [x] **Tri-state TTL** has an **enable** input that produces **Hi-Z** for bus sharing.
- [x] **Standard TTL** PDP ≈ 100 pJ; **74ALSxx** PDP ≈ 4 pJ; **modern CMOS** PDP < 1 pJ.
- [x] TTL's main advantage over early CMOS: **speed at low V**. Its main disadvantage: **high static power**.
- [x] Today, **74HCT / 74ACT** CMOS families are **TTL-compatible** — combining TTL's voltage levels with CMOS's low power.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Power-Delay Product & TTL Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS, Circuit Description and Working of TTL and CMOS Inverter
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT304 - LOGIC CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CMOS, Circuit Description and Working of TTL and CMOS Inverter

# 🔌 CMOS, Circuit Description and Working of TTL and CMOS Inverter

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Inverter (NOT Gate)?

An **inverter** is the most fundamental building block of all digital logic families. It is a single-input, single-output combinational circuit that produces the logical complement (opposite) of its input. If the input is a logic **HIGH (1)**, the output is a logic **LOW (0)**, and vice versa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *An inverter is a single-input logic gate whose output is the Boolean complement of its input, mathematically expressed as **F = A'** (or NOT A). It serves as the basic voltage amplification stage in both bipolar (TTL) and unipolar (CMOS) logic families.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of an inverter as a **light switch with a twist**. Imagine a staircase where the switch at the bottom of the stairs turns **OFF** the upstairs light, and the switch at the top of the stairs turns it **ON**. Each switch inverts the state of the bulb. A digital inverter does the same thing with voltage levels: a HIGH voltage at the input forces the output to LOW, and a LOW input forces the output to HIGH.

### 1.2 What is CMOS?

**CMOS** stands for **Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor**. It is a digital logic family constructed using **two complementary MOSFET transistors** — one **NMOS** (N-channel) and one **PMOS** (P-channel) — arranged in a push-pull configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core CMOS Principle:** In steady state, ONE of the two transistors is always OFF. This means CMOS draws virtually **zero static (DC) current** from the power supply, making it the lowest-power digital technology in existence today.

**Real-world Analogy:**
A CMOS inverter is like a **two-person rowing boat in a narrow canal**. One rower (PMOS) pulls the boat forward when the water level is low (input LOW), while the other rower (NMOS) pulls the boat backward when the water level is high (input HIGH). They never both pull at the same time, so the boat (output) glides with minimal effort.

**Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):**

- **Threshold Voltage of NMOS ($V_{TN}$):** typically **0.5 V to 1.0 V**
- **Threshold Voltage of PMOS ($V_{TP}$):** typically **-0.5 V to -1.0 V**
- **Standard Supply Voltage ($V_{DD}$):** typically **+5 V (legacy)** or **+3.3 V / +1.8 V (modern)**
- **Noise Margin (NM):** typically **> 1.0 V** for robust operation
- **Static Power Dissipation:** ideally **0 W** (in steady state)
- **Technology Node:** modern CMOS scaled down to **5 nm and below**

### 1.3 What is TTL?

**TTL** stands for **Transistor-Transistor Logic**. It is a digital logic family built using **bipolar junction transistors (BJTs)** and resistors, where multiple emitter-coupled or multi-emitter transistors perform the logic function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *TTL is a class of digital logic gates constructed using bipolar junction transistors, where the input stage uses a multi-emitter transistor to perform the AND logic function, and a totem-pole output stage provides active pull-up and pull-down drive capability.*

**Real-world Analogy:**
A TTL inverter is like a **two-handled water tap**. To get water out, you must push down one handle and release the other — both hands (transistors) coordinate to direct the flow. Unlike CMOS, both transistors can conduct briefly during switching, causing a momentary current spike (called *shoot-through* or *crowbar current*).

**Key TTL Physical Constants:**

- **Supply Voltage ($V_{CC}$):** **+5 V** (industry standard)
- **Logic LOW Voltage ($V_{OL}$):** typically **≤ 0.4 V**
- **Logic HIGH Voltage ($V_{OH}$):** typically **≥ 2.7 V**
- **Propagation Delay:** typically **5–10 ns** (standard TTL)
- **Power Dissipation per gate:** typically **10 mW**
- **Fan-out:** typically **10** (can drive 10 standard TTL inputs)

### 1.4 Why These Two Families Matter

| Family | Active Device | Power | Speed | Modern Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TTL** | BJT (Bipolar) | Medium (mW range) | Fast (ns) | Legacy industrial, glue logic |
| **CMOS** | MOSFET (Unipolar) | Ultra-low (μW range) | Slower (ns) but improving | **Universal** — microprocessors, ASICs, SoCs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favorite Question:** *"Compare TTL and CMOS logic families."* This comparison table is the single most repeated question structure in KTU university exams for this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CMOS Inverter — Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Circuit Structure

A CMOS inverter consists of:
- **One PMOS transistor** (top device) — source connected to $V_{DD}$, drain connected to output
- **One NMOS transistor** (bottom device) — source connected to GND (0 V), drain connected to output
- **Both gates tied together** to form the input node
- **Both drains tied together** to form the output node

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**When $V_{in} = 0$ V (Logic LOW):**
- The gate-to-source voltage of the **PMOS** = $V_{GS,p} = V_{in} - V_{DD} = 0 - 5 = -5$ V
- Since $-5 \text{ V} < V_{TP} (\approx -1 \text{ V})$, the **PMOS turns ON**
- The gate-to-source voltage of the **NMOS** = $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} - 0 = 0$ V
- Since $0 \text{ V} < V_{TN} (\approx 1 \text{ V})$, the **NMOS remains OFF**
- **Result:** Output node is pulled up to $V_{DD}$ through the conducting PMOS → $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD} = +5$ V

**When $V_{in} = V_{DD} = 5$ V (Logic HIGH):**
- The gate-to-source voltage of the **PMOS** = $V_{GS,p} = 5 - 5 = 0$ V
- Since $0 \text{ V} > V_{TP} (\approx -1 \text{ V})$, the **PMOS turns OFF**
- The gate-to-source voltage of the **NMOS** = $V_{GS,n} = 5 - 0 = 5$ V
- Since $5 \text{ V} > V_{TN} (\approx 1 \text{ V})$, the **NMOS turns ON**
- **Result:** Output node is pulled down to GND through the conducting NMOS → $V_{out} = V_{OL} = 0$ V

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind CMOS Efficiency:** In both steady states (HIGH and LOW output), exactly ONE transistor conducts and ONE is OFF. Since the OFF transistor has effectively infinite resistance, the leakage current is in the **nanoampere** range. This is why CMOS is the **dominant technology** for battery-powered devices, smartphones, laptops, and IoT sensors.

### 2.1.3 CMOS Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) — The Five Regions

The VTC of a CMOS inverter is divided into **five operating regions** as $V_{in}$ sweeps from 0 to $V_{DD}$:

| Region | $V_{in}$ Range | NMOS State | PMOS State | Output Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Region A** | $0 \le V_{in} < V_{TN}$ | Cutoff | Triode (Linear) | $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ |
| **Region B** | $V_{TN} \le V_{in} < V_{inv}$ | Saturation | Triode | $V_{out}$ falls steeply |
| **Region C** | $V_{in} = V_{inv}$ | Saturation | Saturation | **Switching Threshold** point |
| **Region D** | $V_{inv} < V_{in} \le V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert$ | Triode | Saturation | $V_{out}$ continues to fall |
| **Region E** | $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert$ | Triode | Cutoff | $V_{out} = V_{OL} = 0$ V |

**Switching Threshold Voltage ($V_{inv}$):** The point where $V_{in} = V_{out}$, and both transistors are in saturation. This is the **inversion** point of the VTC.

### 2.1.4 CMOS Power Dissipation

The total power dissipation in a CMOS inverter has **three components:**

$$P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic} + P_{short-circuit}$$

**1. Static Power ($P_{static}$):** Power consumed when the circuit is NOT switching.
$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$$
For ideal CMOS, $I_{leakage} \approx 0$, so $P_{static} \approx 0$.

**2. Dynamic Power ($P_{dynamic}$):** Power consumed to charge and discharge the load capacitance.
$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$
where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (probability of a 0→1 transition)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance at the output node
- $f$ = clock frequency

**3. Short-Circuit Power ($P_{SC}$):** Power dissipated during the brief moment when both transistors conduct during switching.

---

## 2.2 TTL Inverter (NOT Gate) — Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 Standard TTL Inverter Circuit Stages

A standard TTL inverter (such as the **7404** hex inverter IC) consists of **three distinct stages:**

**Stage 1: Input Stage (Multi-emitter Transistor Q1)**
- A multi-emitter NPN transistor with one base resistor $R_B$ connected to $V_{CC}$
- Each emitter acts as a logic input
- Performs the **AND function** (all inputs HIGH → base current flows)

**Stage 2: Phase-Splitting Stage (Transistor Q2)**
- An NPN transistor that splits the signal into two complementary outputs
- One output drives Q3 (push-pull upper), the other drives Q4 (push-pull lower)

**Stage 3: Output Stage (Totem-Pole Configuration: Q3 + Q4 + Diode)**
- **Q3** (upper NPN) — provides active pull-up to HIGH
- **Q4** (lower NPN) — provides active pull-down to LOW
- **Diode D1** — ensures Q3 and Q4 never conduct simultaneously
- This is the famous **"totem-pole"** output

### 2.2.2 TTL Operational Logic

**When $V_{in} = LOW$ (0 to 0.8 V):**
- The base-emitter junction of Q1 is **forward biased**
- Current flows from $V_{CC}$ through $R_B$ → out of the emitter → into the previous stage
- Q1 is in **saturation**; Q2 receives no base current → Q2 is **OFF**
- Q2 OFF means Q4 has no base drive → Q4 is **OFF**
- Q3 receives base current through $R_2$ → Q3 is **ON** (and diode D1 is ON)
- **Result:** Output is pulled HIGH → $V_{out} = V_{OH} \approx 3.6$ V

**When $V_{in} = HIGH$ (2.0 V to $V_{CC}$):**
- The base-emitter junction of Q1 is **reverse biased**
- The base-collector junction of Q1 is **forward biased** (current flows from $V_{CC}$ through $R_B$ → through Q1's base-collector → into Q2's base)
- Q2 turns **ON** (saturation)
- Q2 ON pulls Q4's base LOW → Q4 turns **ON** (saturation)
- Q3 and D1 are **OFF** because Q2's collector voltage is pulled low
- **Result:** Output is pulled LOW → $V_{out} = V_{OL} \approx 0.2$ V

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key TTL Feature — Active Pull-Up:** Unlike early resistor-transistor logic (RTL), TTL uses an **active transistor (Q3)** for pull-up. This gives TTL its characteristic **low output impedance in both states**, enabling faster switching and higher fan-out than passive-pull-up logic families.

### 2.2.3 TTL Power Dissipation

TTL power is dominated by the **constant current flowing through $R_B$** even in static states:

$$P_{TTL} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC(avg)}$$

For a standard 7400-series TTL gate:
- $I_{CCH}$ (current with output HIGH) $\approx 1$ mA
- $I_{CCL}$ (current with output LOW) $\approx 3$ mA
- Average $I_{CC} \approx 2$ mA
- $P_{TTL} \approx 5 \text{ V} \times 2 \text{ mA} = 10$ mW

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Memorize the following table. These equations appear in **at least one question** in every KTU end-semester exam for this module.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{inv} = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}(V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$ | CMOS switching threshold | Volts (V) | CMOS Inverter |
| 2 | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High-state noise margin | Volts (V) | All logic families |
| 3 | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low-state noise margin | Volts (V) | All logic families |
| 4 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | CMOS dynamic power | Watts (W) | CMOS |
| 5 | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | CMOS static leakage power | Watts (W) | CMOS |
| 6 | $t_p = \dfrac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay | Seconds (s) | All families |
| 7 | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | Power-Delay Product (figure of merit) | Joules (J) | All families |
| 8 | $Fan\text{-}out = \min\left(\dfrac{I_{OH}}{\vert I_{IH}\vert}, \dfrac{I_{OL}}{\vert I_{IL}\vert}\right)$ | Maximum load capacity | Dimensionless | TTL, CMOS |
| 9 | $R_{out,H} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OH}}{I_{OH}}$ | Output resistance (HIGH) | Ohms ($\Omega$) | TTL totem-pole |
| 10 | $I_{IL} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE1} - V_{IL}}{R_B}$ | TTL input current (LOW) | Amperes (A) | TTL input stage |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why CMOS/TTL Matters |
|---|---|
| **Microprocessor Design** | Billions of CMOS inverters form flip-flops, ALUs, cache memory |
| **Industrial Control** | TTL used in legacy 5 V PLCs, sensor interfacing due to noise immunity |
| **Wearable / IoT Devices** | CMOS enables months-long battery life via sub-threshold operation |
| **Space Electronics** | Rad-hardened CMOS used in satellites; TTL used in older space-grade systems |
| **Mixed-Signal ASICs** | CMOS inverters used as analog amplifiers (in weak-inversion region) |
| **FPGA Implementation** | Every LUT (look-up table) is built from CMOS inverters and pass transistors |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of CMOS Switching Threshold Voltage ($V_{inv}$)

The switching threshold is the input voltage $V_{in} = V_{out}$ at which both the NMOS and PMOS transistors are in the **saturation region**. At this point, the currents through both transistors are equal in magnitude.

### Step 1: Write the drain current equations in saturation

For the **NMOS** in saturation:
$$I_{DN,sat} = \frac{k_n}{2} (V_{GS,n} - V_{TN})^2$$

For the **PMOS** in saturation:
$$I_{DP,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2} (V_{GS,p} - V_{TP})^2$$

### Step 2: Substitute the gate-source voltages

Since the source of NMOS is at GND:
$$V_{GS,n} = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$$

Since the source of PMOS is at $V_{DD}$:
$$V_{GS,p} = V_{in} - V_{DD}$$

### Step 3: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law at the output node

At the switching point, the current flowing into the output node from PMOS must equal the current flowing out of the output node through NMOS:
$$I_{DN,sat} = I_{DP,sat}$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{k_n}{2} (V_{in} - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} (V_{in} - V_{DD} - V_{TP})^2$$

### Step 4: Take the square root of both sides

$$\sqrt{k_n} \cdot (V_{in} - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{k_p} \cdot (V_{in} - V_{DD} - V_{TP})$$

### Step 5: Expand and collect $V_{in}$ terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{in} - \sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{TN} &= \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{in} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{TP}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Isolate $V_{in}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} \cdot (\sqrt{k_n} - \sqrt{k_p}) &= \sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{TN} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{TP}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Solve for $V_{inv}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{inv} &= \frac{\sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{TN} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{TP}}{\sqrt{k_n} - \sqrt{k_p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8: Final simplified form (the standard textbook expression)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{inv} &= \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case — Symmetric Inverter:** If $k_n = k_p$ and $V_{TN} = -V_{TP}$, then the switching threshold simplifies to $V_{inv} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$. This is the **ideal design goal** — it gives maximum noise margin in both states and the sharpest VTC transition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Noise Margins

The noise margin is the **maximum noise voltage** that can be superimposed on a logic signal without causing a logic error at the input of the next gate.

### Step 1: Define the four critical voltages from the VTC

- $V_{OL}$ = output voltage in the LOW state (when input is HIGH)
- $V_{OH}$ = output voltage in the HIGH state (when input is LOW)
- $V_{IL}$ = maximum input voltage recognized as LOW
- $V_{IH}$ = minimum input voltage recognized as HIGH

These are found from the VTC at the points where the **slope = -1** (the unity-gain points).

### Step 2: High-state noise margin

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$$

This represents how much noise can be **subtracted** from a HIGH output before it is misinterpreted as something other than HIGH.

### Step 3: Low-state noise margin

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$

This represents how much noise can be **added** to a LOW output before it is misinterpreted as something other than LOW.

### Step 4: Numerical Example (Standard 74HC00 CMOS)

Given:
- $V_{OH} = 4.9$ V
- $V_{OL} = 0.1$ V
- $V_{IH} = 3.5$ V
- $V_{IL} = 1.5$ V

Calculate:
$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_H &= V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 4.9 - 3.5 = 1.4 \text{ V} \\
NM_L &= V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 1.5 - 0.1 = 1.4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetric noise margins** ($NM_H = NM_L$) indicate a **well-designed symmetric inverter**. KTU questions often ask to "verify" the noise margin symmetry for a CMOS inverter with matched NMOS/PMOS sizing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of CMOS Dynamic Power Dissipation

### Step 1: Energy stored in a capacitor

When a load capacitor $C_L$ is charged from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the energy stored in the capacitor is:
$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 2: Energy delivered by the power supply

Simultaneously, the power supply $V_{DD}$ delivers charge $Q = C_L V_{DD}$ at voltage $V_{DD}$, supplying energy:
$$E_{supply} = Q \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 3: Energy dissipated in the PMOS transistor

By conservation of energy, the energy dissipated in the PMOS (charging path) is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{dissipated,charge} &= E_{supply} - E_{stored} \\
&= C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Energy dissipated during discharge

When the capacitor discharges from $V_{DD}$ to 0 through the NMOS, all the stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated in the NMOS transistor.

### Step 5: Total energy per switching cycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= E_{dissipated,charge} + E_{dissipated,discharge} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 \\
&= C_L V_{DD}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Average power dissipation

If the gate switches at frequency $f$ (cycles per second), and the activity factor is $\alpha$ (fraction of clock cycles that produce a 0→1 transition):
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= \alpha \cdot E_{total} \cdot f \\
&= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why modern low-power design aggressively **reduces $V_{DD}$** — the power scales as $V_{DD}^2$, so halving the supply voltage reduces dynamic power by a factor of **4**. This is the key reason for the industry's shift from 5 V → 3.3 V → 1.8 V → 0.9 V supply rails.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CMOS vs TTL Comparison

| Parameter | CMOS (74HC00) | TTL (74LS00) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Power** | ~10 nW | ~10 mW | **CMOS** (by 10⁶) |
| **Dynamic Power** | Depends on $f$ | Independent of $f$ | CMOS at low $f$; TTL at very high $f$ |
| **Propagation Delay** | ~7 ns | ~10 ns | CMOS (slight edge) |
| **Noise Margin** | ~1.4 V (symmetric) | ~0.4 V (asymmetric) | **CMOS** |
| **Fan-out** | ~50 (DC); lower at high $f$ | ~20 | **CMOS** (DC) |
| **Input Impedance** | ~10¹² $\Omega$ (capacitive) | ~4 k$\Omega$ | **CMOS** |
| **Supply Voltage** | 2 V – 6 V | Fixed 5 V $\pm$ 5% | **CMOS** (flexible) |
| **Cost per Gate** | Lower (higher density) | Higher | **CMOS** |
| **Output Drive Current** | $\pm$ 25 mA (HC) | $\pm$ 0.4 mA (LS) | **CMOS** (HC) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: CMOS Inverter with $k_n \neq k_p$

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter is fabricated with $k_n = 50 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $k_p = 20 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $V_{TN} = 0.8$ V, $V_{TP} = -0.7$ V, and $V_{DD} = 5$ V. Calculate the switching threshold $V_{inv}$.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Compute the ratio $\sqrt{k_p / k_n}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} &= \sqrt{\frac{20}{50}} = \sqrt{0.4} \approx 0.632
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the switching threshold formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{inv} &= \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}} \\
&= \frac{0.8 + 0.632 \cdot (5 + (-0.7))}{1 + 0.632} \\
&= \frac{0.8 + 0.632 \cdot 4.3}{1.632} \\
&= \frac{0.8 + 2.718}{1.632} \\
&= \frac{3.518}{1.632} \\
&\approx 2.156 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpret the result

Since $V_{inv} \approx 2.16$ V (which is less than $V_{DD}/2 = 2.5$ V), the VTC transition occurs **slightly below the midpoint**. This indicates that the **NMOS is stronger** than the PMOS ($k_n > k_p$). To symmetrize the VTC, the designer should **increase the W/L ratio of the PMOS** to make $k_p \approx k_n$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Transistor-Level Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Vin] --> B[Gate of PMOS Q1]
    A --> C[Gate of NMOS Q2]
    D[VDD Supply Rail] --> E[Source of PMOS Q1]
    B --> F[Drain of PMOS Q1]
    C --> G[Drain of NMOS Q2]
    H[Ground GND] --> I[Source of NMOS Q2]
    F --> J[Output Node Vout]
    G --> J
    J --> K[Load Capacitance CL]
    
    style A fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#4caf50,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#333,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style J fill:#03a9f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style K fill:#ff9800,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.1.1 CMOS Inverter Functional Block Representation

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Vin Logic Input] --> INV{CMOS Inverter}
    INV --> OUT[Vout = NOT Vin]
    VDD[VDD Power] --> INV
    GND[GND Reference] --> INV
    
    style IN fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#000
    style OUT fill:#03a9f4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style INV fill:#e91e63,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style VDD fill:#4caf50,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style GND fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.1.2 CMOS Inverter Operation State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State_High_Check
    State_High_Check --> Output_LOW: Vin = HIGH
    State_High_Check --> Output_HIGH: Vin = LOW
    Output_LOW: NMOS ON, PMOS OFF, Vout = 0V
    Output_HIGH: NMOS OFF, PMOS ON, Vout = VDD
    Output_LOW --> [*]
    Output_HIGH --> [*]
```

---

## 4.2 Standard TTL Inverter — Three-Stage Block Architecture


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Explain the static power dissipation of a CMOS inverter. Why is it considered negligible in the ideal case?**

**Model Answer:**

The **static power dissipation** of a CMOS inverter is the power consumed when the circuit is in a steady state (i.e., the input is not changing).

In steady state, either $V_{in} = 0$ V (logic LOW) or $V_{in} = V_{DD}$ (logic HIGH). In **both** cases, exactly **one transistor is OFF** and the other is ON.

- When $V_{in} = 0$: PMOS is ON, NMOS is OFF
- When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$: NMOS is ON, PMOS is OFF

Since the OFF transistor has a near-infinite drain-to-source resistance (in the **giga-ohm** range), the current flowing from $V_{DD}$ to GND is limited to the **leakage current** of the OFF device.

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$$

For an **ideal CMOS inverter**, $I_{leakage} \to 0$, which makes $P_{static} \approx 0$. This is the fundamental reason why CMOS is the **lowest-power digital logic family** and is preferred for battery-operated and portable devices.

> [!NOTE]
> In **real (non-ideal)** CMOS, sub-threshold leakage and reverse-bias junction leakage cause $I_{leakage}$ to be in the **nanoampere** range, making $P_{static}$ non-zero but still extremely small.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**What is a totem-pole output stage in TTL? Why is it used instead of a passive pull-up resistor?**

**Model Answer:**

A **totem-pole output stage** is the standard output configuration used in TTL logic gates, consisting of **two NPN transistors (Q3 and Q4) stacked in series** between $V_{CC}$ and GND, along with a **level-shifting diode (D1)**.

The configuration looks like a "totem pole" with the transistors stacked vertically:

- **Q3 (upper transistor):** Provides **active pull-up** to drive the output HIGH
- **Q4 (lower transistor):** Provides **active pull-down** to drive the output LOW
- **D1 (diode):** Ensures Q3 and Q4 **never conduct simultaneously**, preventing a direct short from $V_{CC}$ to GND

**Why active pull-up is preferred over a passive resistor:**

| Aspect | Passive Resistor (RTL) | Active Totem-Pole (TTL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Output impedance (HIGH)** | High (R = few kΩ) | Low (Q3 in saturation, $\sim$ 50 $\Omega$) |
| **Switching speed** | Slow (RC time constant large) | Fast (low R allows rapid charging of $C_L$) |
| **Power dissipation (LOW output)** | High (current flows through R to ground) | Low (Q3 OFF, no current path) |
| **Noise immunity** | Poor | Good |

The totem-pole configuration gives TTL its characteristic **low output impedance in both logic states**, enabling **high fan-out** and **fast switching speeds** of 5–10 ns.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the construction and working of a **CMOS inverter**. Derive the expression for the **switching threshold voltage** $V_{inv}$. (7 marks)

**b)** For a CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 5$ V, $V_{TN} = 1$ V, $V_{TP} = -1$ V, $k_n = 40 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2$, and $k_p = 20 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2$, calculate the switching threshold $V_{inv}$ and comment on the symmetry of the VTC. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Construction:**
A CMOS inverter consists of two enhancement-mode MOSFETs:
- **PMOS (Q1):** Source at $V_{DD}$, drain at output, gate at input
- **NMOS (Q2):** Source at GND, drain at output, gate at input
- Gates are tied together to form the input; drains are tied together to form the output

**Working:**

| Input | $V_{GS,p}$ | $V_{GS,n}$ | PMOS | NMOS | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{in} = 0$ V (LOW) | $-V_{DD} = -5$ V | $0$ V | ON | OFF | $V_{OH} = V_{DD} = 5$ V |
| $V_{in} = V_{DD} = 5$ V (HIGH) | $0$ V | $+V_{DD} = 5$ V | OFF | ON | $V_{OL} = 0$ V |

**Derivation of $V_{inv}$:**

At the switching threshold, $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_{inv}$, and **both transistors are in saturation**.

The drain currents must be equal:
$$I_{DN,sat} = I_{DP,sat}$$

Expanding:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_{TN})^2 &= \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{in} - V_{DD} - V_{TP})^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the square root and solving (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{inv} = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_{DD} = 5$ V
- $V_{TN} = 1$ V
- $V_{TP} = -1$ V
- $k_n = 40 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2$
- $k_p = 20 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2$

**Step 1: Compute $\sqrt{k_p/k_n}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} &= \sqrt{\frac{20}{40}} = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.707
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the switching threshold formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{inv} &= \frac{1 + 0.707 \times (5 + (-1))}{1 + 0.707} \\
&= \frac{1 + 0.707 \times 4}{1.707} \\
&= \frac{1 + 2.828}{1.707} \\
&= \frac{3.828}{1.707} \\
&\approx 2.243 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comment on symmetry**

The ideal symmetric switching threshold would be $V_{DD}/2 = 2.5$ V. The calculated $V_{inv} \approx 2.24$ V is **less than 2.5 V**, which means the **NMOS is stronger** than the PMOS (since $k_n > k_p$).

To **symmetrize the VTC**, the designer must **increase the W/L ratio of the PMOS** to make $k_p \approx k_n$. Specifically, since $k_n/k_p = 2$, the PMOS should be made **twice as wide** as the NMOS (i.e., $(W/L)_p \approx 2 \cdot (W/L)_n$).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Internal Choice)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a)** Describe the **circuit construction and working of a standard TTL inverter** with a neat diagram. Identify the role of the multi-emitter transistor in the input stage. (7 marks)

**b)** Compare **TTL and CMOS** logic families in terms of **power dissipation, noise margin, fan-out, propagation delay, and input impedance**. State two situations where each is preferred. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Construction of Standard TTL Inverter:**

A standard TTL inverter (e.g., 7404 IC) has **three stages:**

**Stage 1 — Input Stage (Q1):**
- A **multi-emitter NPN transistor** Q1 with base resistor $R_B$ connected to $V_{CC}$
- For an inverter, there is only **one emitter** connected to the input
- Function: Implements the **input AND logic**; in a multi-input gate, all inputs must be HIGH to enable the next stage

**Stage 2 — Phase-Splitter (Q2):**
- An NPN transistor with base connected to Q1's collector
- Emitter connected through a resistor to GND
- Collector connected through $R_2$ to $V_{CC}$
- Function: Splits the phase — its collector output drives the upper output transistor, its emitter output drives the lower output transistor

**Stage 3 — Totem-Pole Output (Q3, Q4, D1):**
- Q3 (upper NPN) with collector at $V_{CC}$, emitter connected to output through diode D1
- Q4 (lower NPN) with collector at output node, emitter at GND
- Q3 and Q4 operate in **complementary fashion** — only one conducts at a time

**Working:**

**Case 1: $V_{in} = LOW$ (≤ 0.8 V)**

- Q1's base-emitter junction is **forward-biased** (BE voltage = $V_{in} - 0$ is small and positive)
- Current flows from $V_{CC} \to R_B \to$ Q1's base $\to$ Q1's emitter $\to$ input source
- Q1 enters **saturation**; no base current is delivered to Q2
- Q2 is **OFF**
- Q2 OFF → Q4's base has no drive → Q4 is **OFF**
- Q2 OFF → Q3's base is pulled HIGH through $R_2$ → Q3 is **ON** and D1 is **ON**
- **Output:** $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{CC} - V_{CE3,sat} - V_{D1} \approx 5 - 0.2 - 0.7 \approx 3.6$ V

**Case 2: $V_{in} = HIGH$ (≥ 2.0 V)**

- Q1's base-emitter junction is **reverse-biased** (Q1's base is at a higher voltage than its emitter)
- Q1's base-collector junction is **forward-biased**; current flows from $V_{CC} \to R_B \to$ Q1's B-C junction $\to$ Q2's base
- Q2 enters **saturation**; its collector voltage drops to $\sim$ 0.2 V
- Q2's emitter drives Q4's base; Q4 enters **saturation**
- Q3 and D1 are **OFF** (Q2's collector is too low to forward-bias Q3's BE and D1)
- **Output:** $V_{out} = V_{OL} = V_{CE4,sat} \approx 0.2$ V

**Role of Multi-Emitter Transistor Q1:**

In a multi-input TTL gate (e.g., 7400 NAND), Q1 has **multiple emitters**, one per input. The transistor performs an **AND function** on the inputs: 
- If **any** input is LOW, the corresponding base-emitter junction is forward-biased, Q1 saturates, and the output of Q1 is LOW, keeping Q2 OFF and the final output HIGH.
- Only when **all** inputs are HIGH are **all** base-emitter junctions reverse-biased; Q1's base-collector junction becomes forward-biased, and Q2 turns ON, producing a LOW output.

Thus, Q1 essentially implements a **low-cost AND function** at the input, which (combined with the phase-splitter inversion) yields the desired gate function.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | TTL (74LS00) | CMOS (74HC00) |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Device** | BJT (NPN multi-emitter) | MOSFET (NMOS + PMOS) |
| **Static Power Dissipation** | ~10 mW (constant) | ~10 nW (idle) |
| **Dynamic Power** | $P = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC}$ (constant) | $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ (frequency-dependent) |
| **Noise Margin ($NM_H$, $NM_L$)** | 0.4 V, 0.4 V (asymmetric) | 1.4 V, 1.4 V (symmetric) |
| **Fan-out** | ~20 (limited by current) | ~50 DC (limited by capacitance at high $f$) |
| **Propagation Delay** | ~10 ns | ~7 ns (HC), 1–2 ns (AC/AHC) |
| **Input Impedance** | ~4 k$\Omega$ (resistive) | ~10¹² $\Omega$ (capacitive) |
| **Supply Voltage** | 5 V $\pm$ 5% (strict) | 2 V – 6 V (flexible) |
| **Power-Delay Product** | ~100 pJ | ~10 pJ (HC), ~1 pJ (modern) |

**When to Prefer Each:**

**TTL is preferred when:**
1. The system operates in a **high-noise industrial environment** and needs guaranteed 5 V noise margins with **current-mode (rather than voltage-mode) signaling** that is more robust against voltage spikes.
2. The design must interface with **legacy 5 V industrial equipment** (PLCs, motor drivers, sensor interfaces) where TTL has been the de facto standard for decades.

**CMOS is preferred when:**
1. The application is **battery-powered** (smartphone, IoT sensor, wearable), where CMOS's near-zero static power dramatically extends battery life.
2. The design requires **high integration density** (microprocessor, memory, ASIC), since CMOS transistors are 5–10× smaller than BJTs and can be fabricated at nanometer scale.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Mermaid diagram restriction in exam:** Never draw Mermaid code on your answer sheet! KTU expects **hand-drawn circuit diagrams** with proper transistor symbols (circle for BJT, arrow for MOSFET body). Using any computational notation will cost you marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $V_{TP}$ sign conventions:** The threshold voltage of a PMOS is **negative** in standard notation. Students frequently write $V_{TP} = +1$ V instead of $V_{TP} = -1$ V, leading to a **completely wrong switching threshold**. Always check the sign.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the level-shift diode D1 in TTL:** Many students draw the totem-pole as just Q3 and Q4 without the diode D1. This is worth **1 mark** in the diagram and shows the examiner you understand *why* Q3 and Q4 cannot conduct simultaneously.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$ definitions:** $V_{OH}$ is the output voltage when the output is HIGH, and $V_{OL}$ is the output voltage when the output is LOW. Some students reverse these, leading to negative noise margins. **Always label clearly.**
> 
> 5. **Omitting the assumption of saturation region in $V_{inv}$ derivation:** The switching threshold formula is valid **only when both transistors are in saturation**. If you don't state this assumption explicitly, the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Not mentioning 'fan-out' in the comparison question:** This is the most commonly missed parameter. Even if you don't have the exact number, write "TTL fan-out $\sim$10–20; CMOS fan-out $\sim$50" to show awareness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam!**

- ✅ **Inverter (NOT gate):** $F = \bar{A}$; single input, single output; output is the Boolean complement of the input.
- ✅ **CMOS = Complementary MOS:** Two MOSFETs (PMOS top, NMOS bottom) in series between $V_{DD}$ and GND; gates tied to input, drains tied to output.
- ✅ **CMOS steady-state rule:** One transistor ON, one OFF, always. This gives **near-zero static power**.
- ✅ **CMOS dynamic power formula:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$. Power scales with $V_{DD}^2$.
- ✅ **Switching threshold formula:** $V_{inv} = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n} \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$ — valid only when both transistors are in saturation.
- ✅ **Symmetric CMOS design goal:** $V_{inv} = V_{DD}/2$ (achieved when $k_n = k_p$ and $V_{TN} = -V_{TP}$).
- ✅ **Noise margin formulas:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$, $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$. Higher NM = better noise immunity.
- ✅ **TTL = Transistor-Transistor Logic:** Uses BJTs; input stage has a **multi-emitter** transistor for AND function; output stage is the **totem-pole** (Q3 + Q4 + D1).
- ✅ **Totem-pole purpose:** Active pull-up and pull-down; low output impedance; fast switching; prevents shoot-through via D1.
- ✅ **TTL static power:** ~10 mW per gate; **constant**, does not depend on switching frequency.
- ✅ **TTL operating voltages:** $V_{OH} \approx 3.6$ V, $V_{OL} \approx 0.2$ V, $V_{IL} \leq 0.8$ V, $V_{IH} \geq 2.0$ V.
- ✅ **TTL fan-out:** ~10 for standard TTL (limited by $I_{OL}$ of the driving gate and $I_{IL}$ of the load gates).
- ✅ **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$; the universal **figure of merit** for digital logic families; lower is better.
- ✅ **MOSFET vs BJT:** MOSFETs are voltage-controlled, BJTs are current-controlled; MOSFETs have ~10¹² $\Omega$ input impedance, BJTs have ~4 k$\Omega$ input impedance.
- ✅ **Modern dominance:** CMOS is the **universal technology** for VLSI, microprocessors, memory, and SoCs; TTL is relegated to **legacy industrial glue logic** and 5 V interface circuits.
- ✅ **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC):** Has 5 regions; the **steep transition region** is where both transistors are in saturation; the slope at $V_{inv}$ is the **small-signal gain** of the inverter.
- ✅ **The diode D1 in TTL:** Drops one diode voltage (~0.7 V) so that Q3's base-emitter junction (which needs 0.7 V to turn on) is **not** forward-biased when Q4 is ON — this prevents **simultaneous conduction** (shoot-through).

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 CMOS Logic Family — The Foundation of Modern VLSI

**CMOS** stands for **Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor**. It is the dominant digital logic family used in virtually every modern integrated circuit (microprocessors, microcontrollers, FPGAs, ASICs) because of its **near-zero static power dissipation**, excellent noise immunity, and high packing density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CMOS (Complementary MOS) uses **two complementary networks** — a **Pull-Up Network (PUN)** made of **PMOS** transistors and a **Pull-Down Network (PDN)** made of **NMOS** transistors. The output is always connected to **VDD** through PUN and to **GND** through PDN, but **never both at the same time**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of CMOS logic as a **two-person rowing system**:
- The **PMOS team (PUN)** pulls the boat UP toward VDD (like a rope attached to the ceiling).
- The **NMOS team (PDN)** pulls the boat DOWN toward GND (like a rope attached to the floor).
- For any given input combination, **only one team pulls** — never both. This means in steady state, **no current flows from VDD to GND**, which is why CMOS consumes almost no static power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Design Rule of CMOS:**
> - **PMOS** transistors conduct when input = **0** (gate low).
> - **NMOS** transistors conduct when input = **1** (gate high).
> - **PUN (PMOS) → ON when output should be 1.**
> - **PDN (NMOS) → ON when output should be 0.**

---

## 1.2 CMOS NAND Gate — Definition

A **CMOS NAND gate** is a two-input universal logic gate implemented in CMOS technology that produces the logical NAND function: **F = (A · B)'**. It uses **2 PMOS transistors in parallel** (PUN) and **2 NMOS transistors in series** (PDN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series vs Parallel Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **NMOS in series** implements **AND** in the pull-down network.
> - **NMOS in parallel** implements **OR** in the pull-down network.
> - **PMOS uses the dual** — series in PUN implements OR, parallel implements AND.

### Conceptual Analogy — CMOS NAND as a Switch Box

Imagine a tap connected to a water tank (VDD):
- **Two upper switches (PMOS)** are placed in **parallel** — EITHER switch closed lets water flow to the output.
- **Two lower switches (NMOS)** are placed in **series** — BOTH must close to drain water to ground.

So:
- If **both inputs = 0**: PMOS parallel ON (water flows down) → Output = **1**
- If **any input = 0**: At least one PMOS ON → Output = **1**
- Only when **both inputs = 1**: All PMOS OFF, both NMOS ON → Output = **0**

This is exactly the NAND truth table.

---

## 1.3 CMOS NOR Gate — Definition

A **CMOS NOR gate** is a two-input universal logic gate that produces the logical NOR function: **F = (A + B)'**. It uses **2 PMOS transistors in series** (PUN) and **2 NMOS transistors in parallel** (PDN) — the **dual** of the NAND structure.

### Conceptual Analogy — CMOS NOR as a Switch Box (Dual of NAND)

Continuing the tap analogy:
- **Two upper switches (PMOS)** are now in **SERIES** — BOTH must close to allow current from VDD.
- **Two lower switches (NMOS)** are now in **PARALLEL** — EITHER switch closed drains current to ground.

So:
- Only when **both inputs = 0**: Both PMOS ON, both NMOS OFF → Output = **1**
- If **any input = 1**: At least one NMOS pulls output to **0** → Output = **0**

This is the NOR truth table.

---

## 1.4 Course Project Using Verilog HDL — PBL (Project-Based Learning) Overview

**PBL** is a KTU-mandated pedagogical methodology where students learn by doing — they select a real-world problem, design a digital circuit, simulate it, and deploy it on hardware. The deliverables include a working **Verilog HDL model**, **simulation waveforms**, and **FPGA-based or discrete-component hardware realization**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Verilog HDL?**
> Verilog is a **Hardware Description Language (HDL)** — NOT a software programming language. It **describes the structure and behavior of digital hardware** (gates, flip-flops, adders, ALUs) and is synthesized by tools like **Xilinx Vivado**, **Intel Quartus**, or **Yosys** into a netlist that can be programmed onto an **FPGA**.

### The Four Canonical Steps of PBL in Logic Circuit Design

| Step | Activity | Tools/Output |
|------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Selection of Relevant Topic** | Real-world problem statement, specifications |
| 2 | **Design and Optimization of Digital Circuit** | Verilog RTL code, logic synthesis report |
| 3 | **Simulation** | Testbench, waveform (`.vcd`/`.wlf`), functional verification |
| 4 | **Hardware Implementation** | FPGA bitstream OR discrete IC implementation on breadboard |

### Conceptual Analogy — PBL as Building a House

Think of the project workflow like constructing a house:
1. **Topic Selection** = Choosing the **blueprint** (what will the house do? A residence? A hospital?).
2. **Design and Optimization** = Creating the **architectural drawing** (floor plan, materials, energy efficiency).
3. **Simulation** = The **scale model** — testing weather resistance, structural integrity before construction.
4. **Hardware Implementation** = The **actual construction** — pouring concrete, wiring electricity, opening the doors.

Skipping any step leads to a failed project — exactly like skipping foundation work in construction.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CMOS NAND Gate — Deep Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Transistor Structure

A 2-input CMOS NAND gate consists of **exactly 4 transistors**:
- **2 PMOS** (M_p1, M_p2) — Source connected to **VDD**, drains tied together, gates connected to inputs A and B.
- **2 NMOS** (M_n1, M_n2) — Sources connected to **GND**, drains tied together, gates connected to inputs A and B.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Topology Rule:**
> - PMOS are in **PARALLEL** between VDD and output node.
> - NMOS are in **SERIES** between output node and GND.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operational Truth Table Analysis

| Case | A | B | M_p1 (PMOS-A) | M_p2 (PMOS-B) | M_n1 (NMOS-A) | M_n2 (NMOS-B) | PUN Path | PDN Path | Output F |
|------|---|---|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|----------|----------|----------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | VDD → F (closed) | Open | **1 (VDD)** |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | VDD → F (through M_p1) | Open | **1 (VDD)** |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | VDD → F (through M_p2) | Open | **1 (VDD)** |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | Open | F → GND (closed) | **0 (GND)** |

### 2.1.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Case (A=0, B=0):** Both PMOS gates are LOW → both PMOS turn ON. Both NMOS gates are LOW → both NMOS OFF. Output is pulled to VDD through the parallel PMOS network. → **F = 1**
- **Case (A=0, B=1):** PMOS-A sees gate=0 → ON. PMOS-B sees gate=1 → OFF. Still at least one PMOS path is active (parallel redundancy). NMOS-A OFF, NMOS-B ON, but the series chain is broken. → **F = 1**
- **Case (A=1, B=0):** Symmetric to Case 2. → **F = 1**
- **Case (A=1, B=1):** Both PMOS OFF (PUN disconnected). Both NMOS ON (series path complete) → Output discharged to GND. → **F = 0**

### 2.1.4 Why Parallel PMOS for NAND?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output should be **1 if ANY input is 0** (NAND logic). The PUN is responsible for pulling HIGH. So we need the PUN to conduct if **A=0 OR B=0**. PMOS turns ON when input=0. So PMOS must conduct if **A=0 OR B=0** → **Parallel** (OR in pull-up).

### 2.1.5 Why Series NMOS for NAND?

The output should be **0 only if BOTH inputs are 1**. The PDN is responsible for pulling LOW. So PDN must conduct if **A=1 AND B=1** → **Series** (AND in pull-down).

---

## 2.2 CMOS NOR Gate — Deep Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 Transistor Structure

A 2-input CMOS NOR gate also uses **4 transistors** but with the **dual topology**:
- **2 PMOS** in **SERIES** between VDD and output.
- **2 NMOS** in **PARALLEL** between output and GND.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Truth Table Analysis

| Case | A | B | PMOS-A | PMOS-B | NMOS-A | NMOS-B | PUN Path | PDN Path | Output F |
|------|---|---|--------|--------|--------|--------|----------|----------|----------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | VDD → F (series closed) | Open | **1 (VDD)** |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Open (PMOS-B OFF) | F → GND (through NMOS-B) | **0 (GND)** |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Open (PMOS-A OFF) | F → GND (through NMOS-A) | **0 (GND)** |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | Open | F → GND (parallel) | **0 (GND)** |

### 2.2.3 The "Why" Behind Series PMOS in NOR

The output should be **1 ONLY if BOTH inputs are 0**. So PUN must conduct if **A=0 AND B=0**. PMOS turns ON when input=0. Therefore **PMOS must be in SERIES** to require both conditions.

### 2.2.4 The "Why" Behind Parallel NMOS in NOR

The output should be **0 if A=1 OR B=1**. PDN must conduct if **A=1 OR B=1**. NMOS turns ON when input=1. Therefore **NMOS must be in PARALLEL**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** for CMOS gate analysis and PBL project planning.

### 2.3.1 CMOS Gate Design Rules

| Parameter | CMOS NAND (2-input) | CMOS NOR (2-input) | CMOS Inverter |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Transistor Count** | 4 (2P + 2N) | 4 (2P + 2N) | 2 (1P + 1N) |
| **PUN Topology** | PMOS in Parallel | PMOS in Series | Single PMOS |
| **PDN Topology** | NMOS in Series | NMOS in Parallel | Single NMOS |
| **Logic Function** | F = (A·B)' | F = (A+B)' | F = A' |
| **Worst-case Output HIGH** | 1 PMOS conducting | 2 PMOS in series | 1 PMOS |
| **Worst-case Output LOW** | 2 NMOS in series | 1 NMOS conducting | 1 NMOS |
| **Static Power** | ≈ 0 (no DC path) | ≈ 0 (no DC path) | ≈ 0 |
| **Dynamic Power** | $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ | Same | Same |

### 2.3.2 Performance Metrics Formulas

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Static Power Dissipation:} \quad & P_{static} = I_{leakage} \cdot V_{DD} \\
\text{Dynamic Power Dissipation:} \quad & P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f \\
\text{Propagation Delay (avg):} \quad & t_p = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2} \\
\text{Noise Margin High:} \quad & NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} \\
\text{Noise Margin Low:} \quad & NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (probability of output transition)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance
- $f$ = clock frequency
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage

### 2.3.3 CMOS vs TTL — KTU Comparison Snapshot

| Feature | CMOS | TTL |
|---------|------|-----|
| Static Power | Near zero | High (mW range) |
| Fan-out | > 50 | ~10 |
| Noise Immunity | Excellent | Moderate |
| Power Supply | 3–15 V (TTL compat 5V) | Fixed 5 V |
| Input Impedance | Very high | Low |

### 2.3.4 PBL Project Effort Distribution (KTU Marks)

| PBL Phase | Typical Marks Weight | Deliverable |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------|
| Topic Selection & Problem Definition | 10% | Proposal, specifications |
| Design & Verilog Coding | 30% | `.v` source files, RTL schematic |
| Simulation & Verification | 25% | Testbench, waveforms, coverage report |
| Hardware Implementation (FPGA/Discrete) | 25% | Bitstream / working breadboard |
| Report & Viva | 10% | PDF report, presentation |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMOS NAND/NOR**: Every standard cell in a 7nm/5nm SoC (Snapdragon, Apple M-series, AMD Ryzen) is built from these primitive gates. NAND is preferred over NOR in modern designs because **PMOS in parallel conducts faster** than PMOS in series (lower resistance path).
- **Fast Adders (CLA)**: Used in GPU shaders, CPU ALUs — Carry Look-Ahead eliminates the ripple delay.
- **Hamming Code Checker**: Used in ECC memory (DDR5 RAM), satellite communication, QR codes.
- **Traffic Light Controller**: Direct application in smart city IoT, embedded traffic systems.
- **ALU on FPGA**: Educational precursor to RISC-V soft-core processors (PicoRV32, VexRiscv).
- **LFSR-based Random Sequence Generator**: Cryptographic key generation, built-in self-test (BIST) in ASICs.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Static Power Dissipation in CMOS

We rigorously derive why CMOS consumes negligible static power.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DC Current Path Condition:} \quad & I_{DC} = 0 \text{ in steady state} \\
\text{Steady State Definition:} \quad & \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = 0, \quad \text{inputs are constant} \\
\text{By KCL at output node:} \quad & I_{PUN} = I_{PDN} + I_{Load} \\
\text{Since output is settled:} \quad & I_{Load} = 0 \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad & I_{PUN} = I_{PDN} \\
\text{But PUN and PDN cannot both be ON simultaneously:} \quad & \\
& \text{PUN ON} \Rightarrow \text{PDN OFF} \Rightarrow I_{PDN} = 0 \\
& \text{PDN ON} \Rightarrow \text{PUN OFF} \Rightarrow I_{PUN} = 0 \\
\therefore I_{DC} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad & P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{DC} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why CMOS is called a "**ratioless**" logic family — the output voltage is independent of the transistor W/L ratios in steady state. This also makes the design **highly robust** to process variations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Power Dissipation (Charging a Capacitor)

When a CMOS gate output switches from 0 → 1, the load capacitor $C_L$ is charged from 0 to $V_{DD}$. The energy drawn from the supply is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{drawn} &= \int_0^\infty V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt = V_{DD} \cdot \int_0^\infty i(t) \, dt \\
&= V_{DD} \cdot \int_0^{V_{DD}} C_L \, dV = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} \\
&= C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The energy stored in the capacitor is:

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

The energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{dissipated} &= E_{drawn} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

When the output switches 1 → 0, the capacitor discharges through the NMOS (no energy drawn from VDD, all stored energy dissipated in NMOS as heat = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$).

**Total energy per switching cycle:**

$$
E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Power with switching activity $\alpha$ at frequency $f$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= \alpha \cdot E_{cycle} \cdot f = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Dynamic power is **proportional to $V_{DD}^2$**. This is why modern CPUs (Intel, AMD) use **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** — reducing $V_{DD}$ by 50% reduces power by 75%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CMOS NAND Propagation Delay (Worst Case)

The worst-case pull-down delay for a 2-input NAND occurs when **only one NMOS is in the path** (e.g., A=0, B=1 → output=1, no pull-down), and the worst-case pull-up delay is when **only one PMOS** drives the output high.

The effective resistance of $n$ transistors in series is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{series} &= n \cdot R_{unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

The effective resistance of $n$ transistors in parallel is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{R_{parallel}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_{i}} \\
R_{parallel} &= \frac{R_{unit}}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For 2-input NAND:**
- Pull-up path: 2 PMOS in **parallel** → $R_{PU} = R_p / 2$ (fast, half resistance)
- Pull-down path: 2 NMOS in **series** → $R_{PD} = 2 R_n$ (slow, double resistance)

Propagation delay (using RC model):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{PLH} &= 0.69 \cdot R_{PU} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot \frac{R_p}{2} \cdot C_L \\
t_{PHL} &= 0.69 \cdot R_{PD} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 2 R_n \cdot C_L
\end{aligned}
$$

To make $t_{PLH} \approx t_{PHL}$, designers **upsize the NMOS** by 2x relative to PMOS (since electron mobility $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \mu_p$). This is the **W/L sizing principle** in CMOS standard cell design.

> [!NOTE]
> For a 2-input NOR: PUN is series (slow), PDN is parallel (fast) — the **opposite** problem. NOR gates are intrinsically slower than NAND for the same transistor sizes — this is **why NAND is the preferred primitive** in modern CMOS libraries.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: LFSR for Random Sequence Generator (4-bit Example)

A 4-bit Linear Feedback Shift Register generates a **pseudo-random sequence** using XOR taps at specific bit positions. The maximal-length sequence is $2^n - 1$ for an n-bit LFSR.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LFSR recurrence relation:} \quad & s_{n+1} = s_n \oplus s_{k} \\
\text{4-bit LFSR (taps at bit 4 and 3):} \quad & \text{feedback} = Q[3] \oplus Q[2] \\
\text{Next state shift:} \quad & Q_{next}[3:0] = \{ Q[2:0], \text{feedback} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample sequence** (starting from 0001):
`0001 → 1000 → 0100 → 0010 → 1001 → 1100 → 0110 → 1011 → 0101 → 1010 → 1101 → 1110 → 1111 → 0111 → 0011 → 0001` (15 states, then repeats)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "random" sequence is **deterministic and reproducible** — it is therefore called **pseudo-random**. True randomness requires analog noise sources (thermal noise, avalanche diodes).

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Hamming Code Parity Check (7,4)

For a 4-bit data word $D = d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0$, the 7-bit Hamming codeword is $H = p_2 d_3 p_1 d_2 d_1 d_0 p_0$ with parity bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_0 &= d_0 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_3 \\
p_1 &= d_0 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3 \\
p_2 &= d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Syndrome calculation at receiver** (detects single-bit error and pinpoints its position):

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_0 &= p_0 \oplus d_0 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_3 \\
s_1 &= p_1 \oplus d_0 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3 \\
s_2 &= p_2 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Syndrome value (binary $s_2 s_1 s_0$) directly gives the **position of the erroneous bit** (1-indexed).

> [!NOTE]
> The Hamming distance of the (7,4) code is **3**, meaning it can correct **all 1-bit errors** and detect (but not correct) all 2-bit errors.
