# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: CMOS NAND Gate Topology `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Draw the circuit diagram of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate and explain its operation. State why PMOS transistors are placed in parallel and NMOS in series.

**Model Answer:**

A 2-input CMOS NAND gate consists of **2 PMOS** (M_p1, M_p2) in **parallel** between VDD and output F, and **2 NMOS** (M_n1, M_n2) in **series** between output F and GND. The gates of M_p1 and M_n1 are tied to input A; the gates of M_p2 and M_n2 are tied to input B.

**Operation (4 cases):**
- **A=0, B=0:** Both PMOS ON, both NMOS OFF → F = VDD (logic 1)
- **A=0, B=1:** PMOS-A ON, PMOS-B OFF, NMOS-A OFF, NMOS-B ON (series broken) → F = 1
- **A=1, B=0:** Symmetric → F = 1
- **A=1, B=1:** Both PMOS OFF, both NMOS ON (series closed) → F = GND (logic 0)

**Why parallel PMOS:** Output should be 1 if **A=0 OR B=0**. PMOS turns ON when input=0. So PUN must conduct on OR condition → **parallel**.

**Why series NMOS:** Output should be 0 only if **A=1 AND B=1**. NMOS turns ON when input=1. So PDN must conduct on AND condition → **series**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often claim "PMOS in parallel because of NAND" without explaining the underlying **OR-condition for PUN conduction**. Always state both the **logic condition** and the **PMOS/NMOS gate behavior**.

---

### Question 2: PBL Project Steps `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** List the four steps involved in a Project-Based Learning (PBL) course project using Verilog HDL. Briefly explain the simulation step.

**Model Answer:**

The four canonical steps of PBL in Verilog HDL design are:

1. **Selection of Relevant Topic:** Identify a real-world digital design problem (e.g., traffic light controller, ALU) and define specifications, inputs, outputs, and constraints.

2. **Design and Optimization of Digital Circuit:** Translate specifications into **Verilog RTL code** (behavioral or structural). Use logic synthesis tools (Vivado, Quartus) to optimize for area, power, and delay.

3. **Simulation:** Write a **testbench** in Verilog that instantiates the Design Under Test (DUT), applies stimulus vectors, and verifies outputs against expected values. Tools: **ModelSim, Vivado Simulator, Icarus Verilog**. Output: **waveform (VCD/WLF)** confirming functional correctness before hardware realization.

4. **Hardware Implementation Using FPGA and Discrete Components:** Two approaches:
   - **FPGA-based:** Synthesize Verilog into a bitstream, program onto FPGA (Basys 3, Nexys A7, Zybo Z7), verify using on-board LEDs, switches, and 7-segment displays.
   - **Discrete components:** Implement using 74xx series ICs (7400 NAND, 7402 NOR, 7474 D-FF) on a breadboard with LEDs and switches.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students skip the **simulation step** and jump directly to FPGA programming. If the design is flawed, it manifests as incorrect hardware behavior that is hard to debug. **Always simulate first.**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — CMOS Gates Analysis (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: CMOS NOR Gate Design

**Q:** Design a 2-input CMOS NOR gate. Draw the circuit diagram, derive the truth table, and explain the operation for all four input combinations. Justify the placement of PMOS in series and NMOS in parallel.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (Verbal Description):**
- **PMOS M_p1** with gate = A, drain = output F, source = drain of M_p2.
- **PMOS M_p2** with gate = B, drain = source of M_p1, source = VDD.
- **NMOS M_n1** with gate = A, drain = output F, source = GND.
- **NMOS M_n2** with gate = B, drain = output F, source = GND.
- (M_n1 and M_n2 sources and drains are both at F and GND, hence in parallel.)

**Truth Table:**

| A | B | M_p1 | M_p2 | M_n1 | M_n2 | F |
|---|---|------|------|------|------|---|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | **1** |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | **0** |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | **0** |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | **0** |

**Operation:**

- **Case A=0, B=0:** Both PMOS gates low → both PMOS ON. Series PUN path is complete → F pulled to VDD = 1. Both NMOS OFF. → **F = 1**.
- **Case A=0, B=1:** PMOS-A ON, PMOS-B OFF → PUN series path broken. NMOS-B ON → PDN parallel path active → F = 0. → **F = 0**.
- **Case A=1, B=0:** PMOS-A OFF → PUN open. NMOS-A ON → F = 0. → **F = 0**.
- **Case A=1, B=1:** Both PMOS OFF, both NMOS ON → F = 0. → **F = 0**.

**Justification for PMOS in Series:** Output should be 1 only if **A=0 AND B=0** (NOR logic requires both inputs low for HIGH output). PMOS turns ON when input=0. So PUN must conduct on AND condition → **series**.

**Justification for NMOS in Parallel:** Output should be 0 if **A=1 OR B=1**. NMOS turns ON when input=1. So PDN must conduct on OR condition → **parallel**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the topology. Remember the mnemonic: **"NOR has the OR"** in the **NMOS** (parallel = OR). The PMOS then follows the **dual** of NMOS.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparison + PBL Topic Selection

**Q:** (i) Compare CMOS NAND and NOR gates in terms of transistor count, speed, and noise margin. (ii) As a PBL project, you are asked to design a "Multiplexer Based Person Priority Check System at Airport." Outline the problem statement, inputs, outputs, and the Verilog approach.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) CMOS NAND vs NOR Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | CMOS NAND | CMOS NOR |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Transistor Count | 4 (2P+2N) | 4 (2P+2N) |
| PUN Topology | PMOS parallel | PMOS series |
| PDN Topology | NMOS series | NMOS parallel |
| Pull-up resistance | $R_p/2$ (parallel) | $2R_p$ (series) |
| Pull-down resistance | $2R_n$ (series) | $R_n/2$ (parallel) |
| Speed (t_PD) | Fast HIGH-to-LOW transition | Fast LOW-to-HIGH transition |
| Preferred in VLSI | **Yes** (faster overall) | Less preferred |
| Noise Margin | Symmetric ≈ 1.5V at 5V | Similar |

**Reason NAND is faster:** PMOS parallel has lower resistance than PMOS series, and modern processes use NMOS upsizing to balance the slow NMOS series path. NOR has the **opposite** problem — slow PMOS series path is harder to optimize.

**(ii) PBL Project Outline (3 marks):**

**Problem Statement:** Design a system that prioritizes persons entering an airport terminal (e.g., **VIP > Senior Citizen > Frequent Flyer > General**) using a **4:1 Multiplexer** controlled by a 2-bit priority code.

**Inputs:**
- 4 candidate data lines: `D0` (VIP), `D1` (Senior), `D2` (Frequent Flyer), `D3` (General)
- 2-bit select line: `S[1:0]` (driven by a priority encoder based on sensor/ID input)

**Outputs:**
- `Y` — selected person's data (e.g., 8-bit ID)
- `LED_priority` — indicates which class is being served

**Verilog Approach (Dataflow/Structural):**

```verilog
module airport_priority_mux (
    input  wire [7:0] D0, D1, D2, D3,
    input  wire [1:0] S,
    output reg  [7:0] Y,
    output wire [3:0] LED
);
    // 4:1 Multiplexer using case statement
    always @(*) begin
        case (S)
            2'b00: Y = D0;  // VIP
            2'b01: Y = D1;  // Senior Citizen
            2'b10: Y = D2;  // Frequent Flyer
            2'b11: Y = D3;  // General
        endcase
    end
    assign LED = (S == 2'b00) ? 4'b0001 :
                 (S == 2'b01) ? 4'b0010 :
                 (S == 2'b10) ? 4'b0100 : 4'b1000;
endmodule
```

**Hardware Implementation:** On **Basys 3 FPGA**, switches map to D0–D3 (8-bit each via switch expansion), buttons to S[1:0], output Y to 7-segment or LED array.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Forgetting to mention **testbench development** and **functional simulation** in the Verilog approach loses 2–3 marks. A complete PBL answer must include: **specification → RTL → testbench → simulation → FPGA bitstream**.

---

### QUESTION B — Course Project with Verilog HDL (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Sample Project — Hamming Code Parity Checker

**Q:** As part of your PBL course project, design a **Hamming Code (7,4) Parity Checker** using Verilog HDL. Show the Verilog code, explain the encoder and syndrome calculator, and describe the simulation testbench.

**Model Solution:**

**Background (1 mark):** A (7,4) Hamming code adds **3 parity bits** to **4 data bits** to enable single-bit error detection and correction. Codeword format: `[p2 d3 p1 d2 d1 d0 p0]`.

**Encoder Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_0 &= d_0 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_3 \\
p_1 &= d_0 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3 \\
p_2 &= d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verilog Encoder (2 marks):**

```verilog
module hamming_encoder (
    input  wire [3:0] data,    // d3 d2 d1 d0
    output wire [6:0] codeword // p2 d3 p1 d2 d1 d0 p0
);
    assign codeword[0] = data[0] ^ data[1] ^ data[3]; // p0
    assign codeword[1] = data[0];                      // d0
    assign codeword[2] = data[1] ^ data[2] ^ data[3]; // p1
    assign codeword[3] = data[1];                      // d1
    assign codeword[4] = data[2];                      // d2
    assign codeword[5] = data[3];                      // d3
    assign codeword[6] = data[1] ^ data[2] ^ data[3]; // p2
endmodule
```

**Syndrome Calculator / Decoder (2 marks):**

```verilog
module hamming_decoder (
    input  wire [6:0] received, // possibly corrupted
    output reg  [3:0] corrected_data,
    output wire [2:0] syndrome,
    output reg        error_flag
);
    assign syndrome[0] = received[0] ^ received[1] ^ received[3] ^ received[5];
    assign syndrome[1] = received[2] ^ received[1] ^ received[4] ^ received[5];
    assign syndrome[2] = received[6] ^ received[3] ^ received[4] ^ received[5];

    always @(*) begin
        corrected_data = received[5:1]; // extract d3 d2 d1 d0 (no parity)
        error_flag = |syndrome;         // non-zero syndrome = error
        if (syndrome != 0) begin
            corrected_data[syndrome - 1] = ~corrected_data[syndrome - 1];
        end
    end
endmodule
```

**Testbench (1 mark):**

```verilog
module tb_hamming;
    reg  [3:0] data;
    wire [6:0] codeword;
    reg  [6:0] received;
    wire [3:0] corrected;
    wire [2:0] syndrome;
    wire       error_flag;

    hamming_encoder enc (.data(data), .codeword(codeword));
    hamming_decoder dec (.received(received), .corrected_data(corrected), 
                         .syndrome(syndrome), .error_flag(error_flag));

    initial begin
        data = 4'b1010; #10;
        received = codeword; #10;          // no error
        received = codeword ^ 7'b0000100; #10; // inject error at bit 3
        $finish;
    end
endmodule
```

**Simulation Outcome:** Waveform shows `error_flag` going HIGH, `syndrome = 011` (binary 3 → error at position 3), and `corrected_data` matching original `1010`.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Hardware Implementation using FPGA and Discrete Components

**Q:** Describe in detail the **hardware implementation step** of a PBL project. Compare **FPGA-based** and **discrete-component (74xx ICs)** implementation approaches with their advantages and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 4: Hardware Implementation (7 marks)**

This is the final PBL step where the simulated Verilog design is realized on real hardware. Two parallel approaches are accepted by KTU.

**Approach A: FPGA-Based Implementation (3.5 marks)**

1. **Synthesis:** Use Xilinx Vivado / Intel Quartus to convert Verilog RTL into a **gate-level netlist** (.edf/.ngc).
2. **Place & Route:** Tool maps netlist onto FPGA's logic blocks (LUTs), flip-flops, BRAM, DSP slices. Generates **bitstream file** (.bit).
3. **Programming:** Load bitstream onto FPGA via JTAG/USB (e.g., Basys 3 board uses microUSB).
4. **In-system Verification:** Test inputs using on-board **switches and buttons**, observe outputs on **LEDs and 7-segment displays**. Optionally use **ChipScope / Integrated Logic Analyzer (ILA)** for internal signal probing.

**FPGA Board Examples:**
- **Digilent Basys 3** (Artix-7 XC7A35T) — 33K logic cells, 16 switches, 16 LEDs, 4-digit 7-seg.
- **Digilent Nexys A7** (Artix-7 XC7A100T) — 158K logic cells, suitable for ALU/RISC-V projects.
- **Xilinx Zybo Z7** (Zynq-7000 SoC) — includes ARM Cortex-A9 + FPGA fabric.

**Advantages of FPGA:**
- Reconfigurable (reprogram for new projects in seconds)
- High integration (one chip replaces hundreds of ICs)
- Real-time performance (nanosecond delays)
- Industry-standard tools (Vivado, Quartus)

**Limitations of FPGA:**
- Higher per-unit cost (not suitable for mass production)
- Higher power consumption than ASIC
- Limited by on-chip resources (BRAM, DSP, IO pins)

**Approach B: Discrete Components (74xx ICs) (3.5 marks)**

1. **Logic Mapping:** Translate the Verilog design into **74xx series ICs**:
   - 7400 (Quad 2-input NAND)
   - 7402 (Quad 2-input NOR)
   - 7408 (Quad 2-input AND)
   - 7432 (Quad 2-input OR)
   - 7486 (Quad 2-input XOR)
   - 7474 (Dual D-FF), 74161 (4-bit counter), 74151 (8:1 MUX)
2. **Breadboard Assembly:** Mount ICs on a solderless breadboard, connect VCC (+5V) and GND rails.
3. **Wiring:** Use jumper wires for interconnections per the circuit schematic.
4. **Verification:** Apply inputs via toggle switches / logic switches, observe outputs on LEDs or logic probe.

**Advantages of Discrete Implementation:**
- Low cost (each IC ≈ ₹20–50)
- Educational — students see the **gate-level reality**
- No synthesis tool dependency
- Easy to debug with multimeter/logic probe

**Limitations:**
- **Propagation delay accumulates** — a 4-bit ripple counter using 4× 7474 ICs is far slower than FPGA.
- **Wiring complexity grows exponentially** with circuit size.
- **Power and noise issues** for large circuits.
- **Not practical for > 50 gates** — FPGA is the only feasible option for complex designs like ALU or Hamming code.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | FPGA | Discrete ICs |
|-----------|------|--------------|
| Speed | MHz–GHz | kHz–MHz (delay-limited) |
| Density | 100K+ gates in one chip | ~5–10 gates per IC |
| Reconfigurability | Full (reprogrammable) | Fixed wiring |
| Cost per project | ₹5,000–50,000 (board) | ₹500–2,000 (IC kit) |
| Educational value | High (industry tools) | Very high (see physical gates) |
| Power consumption | 0.5–5 W | Depends on IC count |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often describe only **one** implementation approach. KTU examiners expect **both FPGA AND discrete** discussion for full 7 marks. Always include the comparison table.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### CMOS Gates — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **CMOS = Complementary MOS** (PMOS PUN + NMOS PDN)
- ✅ **Static power = 0** in CMOS (PUN and PDN never conduct simultaneously)
- ✅ **NAND:** PMOS parallel + NMOS series → Output = 1 if any input is 0
- ✅ **NOR:** PMOS series + NMOS parallel → Output = 1 only if both inputs are 0
- ✅ **Rule of thumb:** PUN conducts when output should be 1; PDN conducts when output should be 0
- ✅ **NMOS in series = AND**, **NMOS in parallel = OR** in the pull-down network
- ✅ **NAND is preferred over NOR** in modern CMOS due to faster pull-up
- ✅ **Dynamic power** = $\alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ — proportional to $V_{DD}^2$
- ✅ **Worst-case delay** of NAND: $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot 2R_n \cdot C_L$ (series NMOS)

### PBL Project Steps — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Step 1:** Topic selection + problem specification
- ✅ **Step 2:** Design and Verilog coding + logic optimization
- ✅ **Step 3:** Simulation (testbench + waveform verification) — **never skip**
- ✅ **Step 4:** Hardware implementation via **FPGA** (Basys 3 / Nexys) OR **discrete 74xx ICs** on breadboard

### Verilog HDL — KTU Key Constructs

- ✅ `module ... endmodule` — every design
- ✅ `assign` for **dataflow** (combinational)
- ✅ `always @(*)` for **behavioral combinational**
- ✅ `always @(posedge clk)` for **sequential** (flip-flops, FSMs)
- ✅ `case` for multiplexers, FSMs, decoders
- ✅ `generate ... for` for **replicated hardware** (e.g., N-bit adders)
- ✅ Testbench: `initial begin ... end` with `$display`, `$monitor`, `$finish`

### Sample Project Key Concepts

| Project | One-line Definition |
|---------|---------------------|
| Random Sequence Generator | LFSR using XOR feedback, $2^n - 1$ states |
| Traffic Light Controller | Moore FSM with timed state transitions |
| Airport Priority MUX | Priority encoder → 4:1 MUX selecting class |
| Waveform Generator | LUT-based sine/triangle output to DAC |
| Object/Visitor Counter | Sensor-debounced up/down counter + 7-seg |
| Fast Adders | CLA with parallel carry generation |
| Hamming Code | (7,4) code with syndrome-based error correction |
| ALU on FPGA | Combinational core (ADD/SUB/AND/OR/XOR) + flags + register file |

### KTU Exam Day Pointers

- ✅ Always draw **clean circuit diagrams** with PMOS/NMOS symbols (circle for PMOS bubble at gate).
- ✅ Mention **why** a topology is chosen, not just **what** it is — examiners reward the "because" clause.
- ✅ For PBL questions, cover **all 4 steps** explicitly — even brief mention scores marks.
- ✅ Include **Verilog code snippets** in answers when the question asks for "Verilog HDL approach" — pure theory loses 2–3 marks.
- ✅ State the **FPGA board name** (Basys 3 / Nexys A7) and **discrete IC numbers** (7400, 7474, 74151) in implementation answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** "**PUN and PDN are mutually exclusive. NAND → parallel PMOS. NOR → series PMOS. Always simulate before hardware.**" Master this trinity and Module 4 is locked down.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Outcomes and Evaluation: Skills to Design Medium Complexity Digital Circuits Using Verilog HDL and Implementation on Programmable Logic Devices Like FPGA; Evaluation Parameters (Relevance of the Topic, HDL Coding and FPGA Implementation, Presentation Skills)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT304 - LOGIC CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Outcomes and Evaluation: Skills to Design Medium Complexity Digital Circuits Using Verilog HDL and Implementation on Programmable Logic Devices Like FPGA; Evaluation Parameters (Relevance of the Topic, HDL Coding and FPGA Implementation, Presentation Skills)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Project Outcomes and Evaluation** in the context of Logic Circuit Design refers to the structured assessment framework that measures a student's ability to apply **Hardware Description Language (HDL)** — specifically **Verilog HDL** — to design, simulate, synthesize, and implement **medium complexity digital circuits** on **Programmable Logic Devices (PLDs)** such as **Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs)**. The evaluation framework tests three core competencies aligned with **Programme Outcome (PO)** mapping under the **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** model: *Engineering Knowledge (PO1)*, *Design/Development of Solutions (PO3)*, *Modern Tool Usage (PO5)*, and *Communication (PO10)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Complexity Digital Circuit** — A digital system containing between **20 and 200 equivalent logic gates**, typically combining sequential elements (flip-flops, registers, counters, FSMs) with combinational logic (encoders, decoders, multiplexers, ALUs). Examples include stopwatches, vending machines, traffic light controllers, UART transceivers, and simple CPU datapaths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programme Outcomes (POs) Mapped in Project Evaluation:**
> - **PO1 – Engineering Knowledge** : Verilog syntax, digital design principles
> - **PO3 – Design/Development of Solutions** : Architectural design, FSM modeling
> - **PO5 – Modern Tool Usage** : Xilinx Vivado / Intel Quartus / ModelSim / ISim
> - **PO10 – Communication** : Presentation, documentation, viva-voce

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are an **architect building a house**. You do not lay bricks randomly; you first draw a **blueprint** (this is the **Verilog HDL code**), get it **reviewed by engineers** (this is **simulation and testbench verification**), then hand it to the **construction crew** who use **prefabricated modular units** (these are the **Configurable Logic Blocks / CLBs** inside an FPGA) to assemble the building. Finally, the **inspector** checks the finished house against the blueprint (this is the **on-board hardware verification** and **evaluation**).

In this analogy:
- **Verilog HDL** = The architectural blueprint written in a language the silicon "construction crew" understands.
- **FPGA** = An empty plot of land filled with millions of prefabricated, configurable logic bricks (LUTs, flip-flops, routing switches).
- **Synthesis Tool** = The construction foreman who reads your blueprint and decides how to arrange the bricks.
- **Bitstream** = The final instruction set uploaded to the FPGA that configures every brick to perform the designed function.
- **Evaluation** = The independent quality audit of your blueprint, brick-arrangement skill, and final inspection report.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Verilog HDL as a "software program that describes hardware."** It is *not* executed sequentially like C; every `always` block, `assign` statement, and module instance represents **hardware that operates in parallel**.

## 1.3 Standard Evaluation Metrics (KTU 2024 Project Rubric)

| Parameter | Weightage (Typical) | What is Measured |
|---|---|---|
| **Relevance of the Topic** | 15–20% | Real-world applicability, novelty, alignment with course outcomes |
| **HDL Coding (Verilog)** | 30–35% | Syntax correctness, modularity, synthesis-friendly style, testbench coverage |
| **FPGA Implementation** | 30–35% | Synthesis report analysis, pin assignment, resource utilization, hardware demo |
| **Presentation Skills** | 15–20% | Clarity, slide design, viva-voce response, time management |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student mistake is to assume "more features = more marks." KTU examiners **penalize incomplete, un-simulated, or non-functional** projects far more heavily than simpler projects that are **fully working and well-tested**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analysis Sheet

## 2.1 Skills to Design Medium Complexity Digital Circuits Using Verilog HDL

The cognitive skill set required to design a medium-complexity Verilog project can be decomposed into **five hierarchical levels**, mirroring **Bloom's Taxonomy** as adopted by KTU under NEP 2020.

### Level 1 — *Remembering & Understanding* (Foundational)
- Verilog syntax: `module`, `always`, `assign`, `initial`, `wire`, `reg`.
- Data types: `wire` (combinational connection), `reg` (storage in procedural blocks).
- Modeling styles: **Dataflow** (`assign`), **Behavioral** (`always`), **Structural** (gate-level / module instantiation).
- Number representations: binary `'b`, hex `'h`, decimal `'d` with explicit bit-width (e.g., `4'b1010`).

### Level 2 — *Applying* (Implementation)
- Writing **synthesizable** Verilog — avoiding `initial` blocks (except in testbenches), `#` delays, and `display` statements in design code.
- Constructing **clocked always blocks** for sequential logic:

```verilog
always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n)
    if (!rst_n) q <= 1'b0;
    else        q <= d;
```

- Building **Finite State Machines (FSMs)** in **Mealy** and **Moore** forms.

### Level 3 — *Analyzing* (Verification)
- Writing **self-checking testbenches** using `$random`, `$display`, `$monitor`, `$finish`.
- Generating waveform dumps (`.vcd` files) for ModelSim / Vivado Simulator.
- Calculating **propagation delay**, **setup/hold time** violations, and **fan-out** concerns from synthesis reports.

### Level 4 — *Evaluating* (Design Trade-offs)
- **Area vs. Speed** trade-offs — choosing between ripple-carry and carry-lookahead adders.
- **Resource utilization** analysis — LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, DSP slices.
- **Power estimation** — clock gating, operand isolation.

### Level 5 — *Creating* (System Integration)
- Hierarchical design — top-level module instantiating sub-modules (`alu`, `regfile`, `controller`).
- IP integration — instantiating vendor IP (e.g., Xilinx Clocking Wizard, FIFO Generator).
- **Design for Testability (DFT)** — adding scan chains, JTAG boundary scan.

## 2.2 Implementation on Programmable Logic Devices (FPGA)

### FPGA Internal Architecture (Conceptual)

A modern FPGA contains six primary programmable resources:

1. **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)** — The basic logic unit containing **Look-Up Tables (LUTs)**, flip-flops, and multiplexers. A k-input LUT can implement any Boolean function of k variables. Modern Xilinx 7-series uses **6-input LUTs**.
2. **Programmable Interconnect** — A matrix of wire segments and programmable switches that route signals between CLBs.
3. **Block RAM (BRAM)** — Dedicated dual-port memory blocks (typically 36 Kb each) for data storage.
4. **DSP Slices** — Hardened multipliers, accumulators, and adders for high-speed arithmetic.
5. **Clock Management Tiles (CMT)** — PLLs and MMCMs for clock generation, de-skew, and frequency synthesis.
6. **I/O Blocks (IOBs)** — Configurable pads supporting standards like LVCMOS, LVDS, SSTL, and HDMI.

### FPGA Design Flow (The Canonical 7-Step Process)

The industry-standard implementation pipeline — strictly evaluated by KTU examiners — is:

1. **Specification** — Functional and timing requirements, interface definitions.
2. **RTL Design** — Writing Verilog/VHDL code (Register Transfer Level).
3. **Functional Simulation** — Behavioral verification using testbenches (no timing yet).
4. **Synthesis** — Converting RTL to a **gate-level netlist** mapped to FPGA primitives.
5. **Implementation** — Place and Route (P&R) on the target FPGA device.
6. **Timing Analysis** — Static Timing Analysis (STA) to confirm setup/hold constraints are met.
7. **Bitstream Generation & Programming** — Creating a `.bit` file loaded onto the FPGA via JTAG.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FPGAs vs. Microcontrollers — The Critical Distinction:**
> - An **MCU** executes a **program sequentially** (von Neumann / Harvard architecture).
> - An **FPGA** is **reconfigured hardware** — the Verilog code literally becomes the silicon circuit (spatial computing). This is why FPGAs achieve **massive parallelism** and deterministic latency.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Analysis Sheet — Verilog Constructs vs. Hardware Inference

| Verilog Construct | Hardware Inferred | Synthesizable? | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| `assign y = a & b;` | AND gate (combinational) | ✅ Yes | None — safest style |
| `always @* y = a + b;` | Combinational adder | ✅ Yes | Missing sensitivity → latch inference |
| `always @(posedge clk) q <= d;` | D flip-flop | ✅ Yes | Forgetting async reset |
| `initial q = 0;` | Not synthesizable | ❌ No | Allowed **only** in testbench |
| `#5 y = a;` | Delay element | ❌ No | Use clock division instead |
| `always @(posedge clk) q = d;` | D flip-flop (race-prone) | ⚠ Risky | Use non-blocking `<=` for FF |
| `case (sel) 2'b00: ... endcase` | Multiplexer | ✅ Yes | Missing `default` → latch |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The skill set evaluated in this project is the **exact workflow** used by:
- **Semiconductor companies** (Intel, AMD/Xilinx, Qualcomm) for ASIC prototyping.
- **Aerospace & Defense** (NASA, ISRO) for radiation-hardened reconfigurable computing.
- **Telecommunications** (5G base stations use FPGAs for real-time signal processing).
- **Automotive** (ADAS, ECU prototyping on Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+).
- **AI/ML Edge Inference** (Xilinx Versal AI Edge, Lattice CertusPro-NX).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: Mapping Project Requirements to Verilog Implementation

Because this topic is **design-oriented rather than mathematically derivable**, the "analytical matrix" here is a **systematic design methodology** that KTU examiners expect students to articulate during the viva.

### Step 1 — Requirement Decomposition

**Objective:** Translate an abstract problem statement into **concrete, testable specifications**.

**Procedure:**
1. Read the problem statement (e.g., "Design a digital stopwatch with start, stop, reset, and lap functions").
2. Identify **inputs** (push-buttons, clock, mode select) and **outputs** (7-segment display, LEDs, buzzer).
3. Create a **truth table** or **state diagram**.
4. Define **timing constraints** (clock frequency, debounce time, refresh rate).

**Example Specification Table:**

| Signal Name | Direction | Width | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `clk` | Input | 1 | 100 MHz system clock |
| `rst_n` | Input | 1 | Active-low reset |
| `start` | Input | 1 | Start/Pause toggle |
| `lap` | Input | 1 | Lap-time capture |
| `seg` | Output | 8 | 7-segment data lines |
| `an` | Output | 4 | Digit anode select |

### Step 2 — Architectural Partitioning

**Objective:** Decompose the design into **sub-modules** following the **divide-and-conquer** principle.

**Example — Stopwatch Architecture:**

```
TOP MODULE: stopwatch_top
   ├── clk_divider         (100 MHz → 1 kHz, 100 Hz, 1 Hz)
   ├── debouncer           (button debounce for start, lap, reset)
   ├── counter_core        (hours, minutes, seconds, centiseconds)
   ├── lap_register        (captures current count on lap press)
   ├── display_mux         (multiplexes time and lap values)
   └── seven_seg_decoder   (BCD to 7-segment)
```

**Viva Question Anticipation:**
> *"Why did you choose a 1 kHz clock for multiplexing and not 1 MHz?"*
> *Answer:* Human eye persistence is ~16 ms (≈60 Hz refresh). A 1 kHz refresh divided across 4 digits yields 250 Hz per digit — well above the flicker threshold, balancing **display stability** and **power consumption**.

### Step 3 — Coding Discipline Checklist

Before submission, each Verilog file must satisfy:

- [ ] **Header comment** with author, date, module name, brief description.
- [ ] **All ports declared** with correct direction and width.
- [ ] **No `initial` blocks** in design files.
- [ ] **All `always` blocks** have complete sensitivity lists (`@*` for combinational, `@(posedge clk)` for sequential).
- [ ] **Non-blocking assignments (`<=`)** used in sequential `always` blocks.
- [ ] **Blocking assignments (`=`)** used in combinational `always` blocks.
- [ ] **No latches** in combinational logic — every `if`/`case` has an `else`/`default`.
- [ ] **Synchronous resets** preferred (or well-justified asynchronous resets).
- [ ] **Meaningful signal names** — `cnt_enable`, not `x123`.
- [ ] **Testbench included** with ≥ 90% functional coverage.

### Step 4 — Synthesis Report Interpretation

A standard Xilinx Vivado synthesis report contains:

| Report Section | What It Tells the Examiner |
|---|---|
| **Primitive & Black Box Usage** | Whether vendor IP was used appropriately |
| **Register Summary** | Number of flip-flops used vs. available |
| **LUT as Logic** | Combinational complexity of the design |
| **LUT as Memory** | Distributed RAM (LUTRAM) usage |
| **DSP Blocks** | Multiplier/accumulator count |
| **Block RAM** | Memory tile usage |
| **I/O** | Pin count and voltage standard |
| **Timing Summary** | Worst Negative Slack (WNS), Total Negative Slack (TNS) |

**Sample Interpretation:**

> *"The design uses **412 LUTs (1.2% of Artix-7 XC7A35T)**, **89 FFs (0.3%)**, and **0 DSP blocks**, achieving a **WNS of +2.34 ns at 100 MHz** — meaning the design comfortably meets the 10 ns clock period with positive slack."*

## 3.2 Worked Example — Traffic Light Controller (FSM Design)

**Problem:** Design a Verilog FSM for a 4-way traffic light with NS-green, NS-yellow, EW-green, EW-yellow, plus a pedestrian crossing mode triggered by an input `ped`.

**State Encoding (Moore Machine):**

```
S0: NS_GREEN   → 4'b0001
S1: NS_YELLOW  → 4'b0010
S2: EW_GREEN   → 4'b0100
S3: EW_YELLOW  → 4'b1000
S4: ALL_RED    → 4'b0000 (safety interval before switching)
```

**Verilog Skeleton:**

```verilog
module traffic_light_fsm (
    input  wire       clk,
    input  wire       rst_n,
    input  wire       ped,        // pedestrian request
    output reg [1:0]  ns_light,   // 00=RED, 01=YELLOW, 10=GREEN
    output reg [1:0]  ew_light
);

    // State register
    localparam [2:0] S0_NS_GREEN  = 3'b001,
                     S1_NS_YELLOW = 3'b010,
                     S2_EW_GREEN  = 3'b011,
                     S3_EW_YELLOW = 3'b100,
                     S4_ALL_RED   = 3'b101;

    reg [2:0] state, next_state;

    // State transition
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n) state <= S0_NS_GREEN;
        else        state <= next_state;
    end

    // Next-state logic (combinational)
    always @* begin
        case (state)
            S0_NS_GREEN  : next_state = (ped) ? S1_NS_YELLOW : S0_NS_GREEN;
            S1_NS_YELLOW : next_state = S2_EW_GREEN;
            S2_EW_GREEN  : next_state = (ped) ? S3_EW_YELLOW : S2_EW_GREEN;
            S3_EW_YELLOW : next_state = S4_ALL_RED;
            S4_ALL_RED   : next_state = S0_NS_GREEN;
            default      : next_state = S0_NS_GREEN;
        endcase
    end

    // Output logic (Moore — depends only on state)
    always @* begin
        case (state)
            S0_NS_GREEN  : begin ns_light = 2'b10; ew_light = 2'b00; end
            S1_NS_YELLOW : begin ns_light = 2'b01; ew_light = 2'b00; end
            S2_EW_GREEN  : begin ns_light = 2'b00; ew_light = 2'b10; end
            S3_EW_YELLOW : begin ns_light = 2'b00; ew_light = 2'b01; end
            S4_ALL_RED   : begin ns_light = 2'b00; ew_light = 2'b00; end
            default      : begin ns_light = 2'b00; ew_light = 2'b00; end
        endcase
    end

endmodule
```

**Evaluator's Analysis:**

- **FSM Type:** Moore (outputs depend on `state` only) — preferred for **glitch-free** outputs.
- **Encoding:** Binary (3 bits for 5 states) — area-efficient.
- **Reset:** Asynchronous active-low — explicitly defined.
- **Default case:** Included in both next-state and output logic — **latch-free**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FPGA Implementation Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    spec[Project Specification Document] --> rtl[RTL Design Verilog Code]
    rtl --> tb[Testbench Verification]
    tb --> sim[Functional Simulation ModelSim]
    sim --> simPass{Pass All Test Cases?}
    simPass -- No --> fix[Debug and Rewrite RTL]
    fix --> rtl
    simPass -- Yes --> synth[Logic Synthesis Vivado]
    synth --> report1[Synthesis Report LUT FF DSP BRAM]
    report1 --> impl[Place and Route Implementation]
    impl --> timing[Static Timing Analysis STA]
    timing --> timingPass{Meets Timing Constraints?}
    timingPass -- No --> opt[Optimization Increase Effort or Restructure]
    opt --> impl
    timingPass -- Yes --> bit[Bitstream Generation dot bit]
    bit --> prog[FPGA Programming JTAG USB]
    prog --> demo[On Board Hardware Demo]
    demo --> eval[Project Evaluation Relevance Coding Implementation Presentation]
```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

> **Q:** Differentiate between an FPGA and a microcontroller. Why is FPGA preferred for prototyping digital designs in academic projects?

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):**

| Aspect | FPGA | Microcontroller |
|---|---|---|
| Execution Model | Spatial / Parallel | Sequential / Programmed |
| Architecture | Array of CLBs (LUTs + FFs) | CPU + Memory + Peripherals |
| Reconfigurability | Hardware re-programmable | Software re-programmable |
| Determinism | Hard real-time guarantees | Interrupt-dependent |
| Performance | GHz-speed parallel pipelines | Limited by clock & ISA |

**Why FPGA for academic projects (1 mark each):**
1. **Rapid prototyping** — design can be changed and re-flashed in minutes.
2. **Industry relevance** — same workflow used in real ASIC prototyping.
3. **Verilog synthesis** — provides immediate hardware feedback on design quality.
4. **Parallelism** — ideal for demonstrating pipelined / concurrent designs (FFT, filters).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

> **Q:** List any **three common mistakes** in Verilog HDL coding that lead to non-synthesizable or incorrect hardware, and state the correct practice.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**

1. **Using `initial` blocks in design code** — `initial` is simulation-only. Correct practice: use a **reset signal** to initialize registers.
2. **Using blocking assignment (`=`) in sequential `always` blocks** — causes race conditions. Correct practice: use **non-blocking assignment (`<=`)** for flip-flops.
3. **Incomplete `case` statements without `default`** — infers unintended **latches** in synthesis. Correct practice: always include `default` clause.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Design a **Verilog HDL model for a 4-bit BCD up-counter with asynchronous active-low reset and overflow flag**. Write the design module, a self-checking testbench, and explain the synthesis report (LUT/FF utilization).

> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Describe the complete **FPGA implementation flow** starting from Verilog RTL to bitstream generation. Include the role of each stage and the tool used (assume Xilinx Vivado).

#### Model Solution — Q(a) [7 Marks]

**Design Module (3 Marks):**

```verilog
module bcd_up_counter (
    input  wire       clk,
    input  wire       rst_n,
    input  wire       enable,
    output reg [3:0]  count,
    output wire       overflow
);

    assign overflow = (count == 4'd9);

    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n)
            count <= 4'd0;
        else if (enable) begin
            if (count == 4'd9)
                count <= 4'd0;
            else
                count <= count + 1'b1;
        end
    end

endmodule
```

**Testbench (2 Marks):**

```verilog
module tb_bcd_counter;
    reg clk, rst_n, enable;
    wire [3:0] count;
    wire overflow;
    integer errors = 0;

    bcd_up_counter uut (.clk(clk), .rst_n(rst_n), .enable(enable),
                        .count(count), .overflow(overflow));

    always #5 clk = ~clk;   // 100 MHz clock

    initial begin
        clk = 0; rst_n = 0; enable = 0;
        #12 rst_n = 1;
        #10 enable = 1;
        // Run for 100 ns and check wrapping at count==9
        #200;
        if (errors == 0) $display("TEST PASSED");
        else             $display("TEST FAILED with %0d errors", errors);
        $finish;
    end

    // Monitor overflow
    always @(posedge clk) begin
        if (overflow && enable)
            $display("Overflow at time %0t, count=%0d", $time, count);
    end
endmodule
```

**Synthesis Report Explanation (2 Marks):**
- A 4-bit BCD counter typically consumes **4–8 LUTs** (as logic), **4 flip-flops**, and **0 DSP/BRAM**.
- The overflow comparator is a 4-input equality check → maps to **1 LUT**.
- No clock-related timing violations expected for 100 MHz operation.

#### Model Solution — Q(b) [7 Marks]

**7-Stage FPGA Implementation Flow (1 Mark per stage):**

| Stage | Description | Tool / Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Specification** | Define functionality, I/O, timing | Document / requirements spec |
| 2. **RTL Design** | Write Verilog HDL | `.v` source files |
| 3. **Functional Simulation** | Verify logical correctness | Waveform `.vcd` / `.wlf` |
| 4. **Synthesis** | Convert RTL to gate-level netlist | Vivado Synthesis → `.dcp` |
| 5. **Implementation** | Place & Route on FPGA fabric | `.dcp` post-route |
| 6. **Timing Analysis** | STA — check setup/hold slack | Timing report |
| 7. **Bitstream & Program** | Generate `.bit` and load via JTAG | On-board hardware demo |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] What are the **key evaluation parameters** used by KTU examiners to assess a Verilog/FPGA project? Discuss the weightage of each and explain what examiners look for under "HDL Coding and FPGA Implementation."

> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **internal architecture of a modern FPGA** with a neat block diagram. Compare **LUT-based logic** realization with the traditional **gate-array approach**.

#### Model Solution — Q(a) [7 Marks]

**KTU Project Evaluation Parameters (Total 100 Marks / 100%):**

| Parameter | Approx. Weightage | What Examiners Look For |
|---|---|---|
| **Relevance of the Topic** | 15–20% | Real-world problem, novelty, alignment with digital design |
| **HDL Coding (Verilog)** | 30–35% | Modular structure, no latches, synthesizable style, testbench |
| **FPGA Implementation** | 30–35% | Synthesis report, pin constraints, hardware demo, timing closure |
| **Presentation Skills** | 15–20% | Slide clarity, Q&A handling, demonstration confidence |

**HDL Coding Expectations (3 Marks):**
- Modular, hierarchical design (≥ 3 sub-modules).
- Clean testbench with at least 5 test cases.
- No simulation-only constructs in design files.
- Comments and code readability.

**FPGA Implementation Expectations (3 Marks):**
- Successful synthesis with non-zero resource utilization.
- Pin assignment file (`.xdc` / `.ucf`) provided.
- Live demo on FPGA development board (Basys 3, Nexys A7, ZedBoard, etc.).
- Discussion of timing report and any design trade-offs.

**Viva-Voce Tip (1 Mark):**
- Examiner may ask: *"Why did your synthesis consume X LUTs instead of Y?"*
- Always be ready to explain: encoding style, FSM type, pipelining decisions.

#### Model Solution — Q(b) [7 Marks]

**FPGA Internal Architecture (3 Marks):**

A modern FPGA consists of:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  FPGA Chip                          │
│  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐         ┌─────┐         │
│  │ CLB │  │ CLB │  │ CLB │  ...    │ CLB │         │
│  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘         └─────┘         │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────┐     │
│  │  Block   │  │   DSP    │       │  Clock   │     │
│  │   RAM    │  │  Slice   │       │ Mgmt Tile│     │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────┘       └──────────┘     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │      Programmable Interconnect Matrix       │   │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐         ┌─────┐         │
│  │ IOB │  │ IOB │  │ IOB │  ...    │ IOB │         │
│  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘         └─────┘         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**LUT-Based vs. Gate-Array Comparison (4 Marks):**

| Feature | LUT-Based FPGA | Gate-Array ASIC |
|---|---|---|
| Basic Logic Unit | 4/6-input LUT + FF | Fixed NAND/NOR gates |
| Function Realization | Truth-table lookup | Wired gate connections |
| Flexibility | Fully programmable | Mask-defined (fixed) |
| Density | Lower (per area) | Higher |
| NRE Cost | Zero (re-flash) | Multi-million USD mask |
| Time-to-Market | Hours / days | Months |
| Reuse | Bitstream rewrite | Not possible |
| Application | Prototyping, low-volume | Mass production |

**Key Advantage of LUTs:** A 4-input LUT can implement **any** Boolean function of 4 variables — equivalent to a 16×1 ROM. This gives FPGAs **infinite functional flexibility** without changing silicon.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Project Evaluation:**
>
> 1. **No hardware demo (-5 to -10 marks):** A working simulation alone is **not sufficient**. KTU examiners expect at least a **video demo or live board demonstration**.
>
> 2. **Un-synthesizable code (-3 to -5 marks):** Using `initial`, `#delay`, `real` data types, or file I/O in design code makes the project un-implementable.
>
> 3. **Missing testbench (-2 to -3 marks):** Self-checking testbenches are mandatory; a single `initial begin $finish; end` is not acceptable.
>
> 4. **No pin constraint file (-2 marks):** Even if synthesis works, without a `.xdc` file, the design cannot be mapped to physical FPGA pins.
>
> 5. **Inability to interpret the synthesis report (-3 to -5 marks in viva):** Students must know the meaning of **LUT, FF, WNS, TNS, BRAM, DSP** and be able to explain their project's resource utilization.
>
> 6. **Poor presentation slides (-2 to -3 marks):** Text-heavy slides, no block diagrams, and reading from slides signal lack of understanding.
>
> 7. **Over-claiming features (-2 marks):** Promising "AI-based" or "IoT" features in a simple combinational project without implementation invites harsh evaluation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- [x] A **medium complexity digital circuit** typically contains **20–200 gates** and combines combinational + sequential logic.
- [x] **Verilog HDL** has three modeling styles: **Dataflow** (`assign`), **Behavioral** (`always`), **Structural** (gate primitives).
- [x] Always use **non-blocking (`<=`)** in sequential blocks and **blocking (`=`)** in combinational blocks.
- [x] **Never** use `initial`, `#delay`, `$display`, or `$finish` inside design modules — these are **simulation-only**.
- [x] Every combinational `if`/`case` **must** have an `else`/`default` to prevent **latch inference**.
- [x] **FPGA** = Field Programmable Gate Array, built from **CLBs (LUTs + FFs)**, **BRAM**, **DSP slices**, **CMTs**, and **IOBs**.
- [x] A **LUT (Look-Up Table)** is the fundamental logic element — a k-input LUT implements any Boolean function of k variables via a 2^k truth table.
- [x] The **FPGA design flow** has **7 stages**: Spec → RTL → Sim → Synthesis → Implementation → STA → Bitstream.
- [x] **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** reports **WNS (Worst Negative Slack)** and **TNS (Total Negative Slack)** — positive WNS = design meets timing.
- [x] **KTU evaluation parameters** (in order of weightage): HDL Coding (30–35%) ≈ FPGA Implementation (30–35%) > Presentation (15–20%) ≈ Relevance (15–20%).
- [x] A **Moore FSM** output depends only on the current state; a **Mealy FSM** output depends on the current state **and** inputs.
- [x] **Bitstream** is a binary file (.bit) that configures all FPGA resources — it is the "compiled hardware" equivalent of an executable.
- [x] Always include a **pin constraints file (`.xdc`)** mapping Verilog ports to physical FPGA pins.
- [x] **FPGA ≠ Microcontroller** — FPGA is parallel hardware; MCU is sequential software.
- [x] **Verilog is case-sensitive**, and reserved keywords (`module`, `always`, `wire`, `reg`, `input`, `output`) must be lowercase.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for Viva-Voce Success:** If your project works in simulation **and** on hardware, and you can explain **every line of your Verilog code** and **every line of your synthesis report**, you will secure full marks in HDL Coding and FPGA Implementation. The remaining 35–40% is won by choosing a **relevant topic** and delivering a **clear, confident presentation**.
